The waiting times for Gynae are as follows:Priority            Average Wait (Weeks)Red Flag        1, Urgent            11Routine          53COLPOSCOPYPriority            Average Wait (Weeks)Red Flag        6Urgent            2Routine          3GYNAECOLOGY HYSTEROSCOPYPriority            Average Wait (Weeks)Red Flag        2Urgent            <1Routine          2SouthernThe information held from the end of June the longest waiting times  are as follows:Urgent outpatient appointment is 185 weeks Routine appointment is 195 weeks. For inpatient appointment the longest waiting time is as follows:Urgent - 77 weeks Routine - 321 weeks.  For day case the longest waiter is as follows:Urgent - 29 weeks Routine  - 204 weeks.WesternNot all endometriosis is diagnosed by laparoscopy, some may be apparent on ultrasound or MRI, some is found incidentally at surgery for another reason.  Not all diagnostic laparoscopy is carried out to exclude endometriosis. Many women are treated empirically for the symptoms of endometriosis without a laparoscopy.As of 3rd July 2024, waiting time for a Gynae appointment is as follows:Urgent  - 63 weeksRoutine - 268 weeks(ii) Following the recent publication of the Getting It Right First Time Report, which provided a review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland, including those suffering endometriosis, and the 20 recommendations to improve gynaecology services, the Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group have established a forum with Trust and PHA representation to oversee ongoing service improvement in this speciality and ensure that this improvement is implemented at a local and regional level. Trusts have also been asked to estab

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the parking improvement scheme for Hillview Avenue, Cloughfern.
answer_text: Having decided on indicative budget allocations (subject to a consultation on the equality impacts) my officials can now consider the detailed programmes of work across the Department’s areas of responsibility, including plans for infrastructure projects in the Hillview Avenue area.However, as this scheme is considered to be low priority when compared with other safety related schemes in the area it is not likely to be included in the 2024/25 work programme.My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to create a specialist myalgic encephalomyelitis or chronic fatigue syndrome service for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The service model for myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME)/chronic fatigue syndrome(CFS) that was previously agreed with voluntary organisations representing people living with ME/CFS, made provision for a medical lead with a specialist interest in ME/CFS. Within this service model the medical lead would provide guidance and evidence-based leadership to local healthcare teams and GPs to provide appropriate treatments.  To date, Belfast Trust has been unable to fill this post.   My officials are considering the current arrangements for the commissioning of ME/CFS services in Health and Social Care Trusts. This includes a proposal to potentially expand current long covid services to include other post viral illnesses, including ME/CFS. The review of long COVID services is in the final stages. Once this work is complete, work can commence with a view to the inclusion of ME within a post viral service model. The development of this overall service model will include an assessment of the ongoing demand for services; a review of current service provision; and, take cognisance of service developments elsewhere in the UK. This will inform the service model going forward and the appropriate professional input.Subject to approval, this service development will progress through the collective work of my officials in both SPPG and PHA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department will improve service outcomes for children and teenagers living with myalgic encephalomyelitis or chronic fatigue syndrome.
answer_text: Patients who have symptoms of myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME) or chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS) and have contacted their GP, should be offered treatment for their symptoms in line with NICE guidelines. The GP may refer the patient to specialists to exclude other potential underlying causes and may refer the patient for therapeutic interventions.Assessments for children and young people are carried out by paediatric specialists. A paediatric specialist will assess and decide on a plan of care. Each plan is individualised depending on the child or young person’s individual needs.Although the current arrangements for the provision of ME/CFS services in our Health and Social Care Trusts varies; support includes occupational therapy led ME/CFS programme, self-management programmes and support as well as symptom management through core services, that best meet the individual's needs.My officials continue to explore opportunities to build on the current support available for patients suffering from ME/CFS, including children and young people, to improve all patient outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider reviewing the maximum amount payable through Carer's Allowance in light of rising costs for carers.
answer_text: Social security policy and legislation in Northern Ireland, including the main benefit rates, is generally maintained in parity with that in place under the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) in Great Britain. In the annual benefits up-rating exercise the Department has no power to set different rates of benefit from those brought forward by the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions in Great Britain. Similar to other benefits, the weekly rate of Carer’s Allowance increased by 6.7% to £81.90 in April 2024.Depending on individual circumstances carers on a low income may be able to access additional financial support through income-related benefits, such as Universal Credit, and their associated carer premiums/elements.  In addition, the Department’s Make the Call service can carry out an entitlement check to ensure a person is accessing all the benefits, supports and services available to them. It can be contacted via Freephone number 0800 232 1271 or by emailing makethecall@dfcni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will recruit a myalgic encephalomyelitis or chronic fatigue syndrome specialist to cover all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The service model for myalgic encephalomyelitis (ME)/chronic fatigue syndrome(CFS) that was previously agreed with voluntary organisations representing people living with ME/CFS, made provision for a medical lead with a specialist interest in ME/CFS. Within this service model the medical lead would provide guidance and evidence-based leadership to local healthcare teams and GPs to provide appropriate treatments.  To date, Belfast Trust has been unable to fill this post.   My officials are considering the current arrangements for the commissioning of ME/CFS services in Health and Social Care Trusts. This includes a proposal to potentially expand current long covid services to include other post viral illnesses, including ME/CFS. The review of long COVID services is in the final stages. Once this work is complete, work can commence with a view to the inclusion of ME within a post viral service model. The development of this overall service model will include an assessment of the ongoing demand for services; a review of current service provision; and, take cognisance of service developments elsewhere in the UK. This will inform the service model going forward and the appropriate professional input.Subject to approval, this service development will progress through the collective work of my officials in both SPPG and PHA.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline its work to increase visitor numbers to Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: Visitor numbers to Parliament Buildings currently range between approximately 35,000 to 40,000 people per annum. This includes people coming to Parliament Buildings to view Assembly plenary or committee meetings and people arriving at the invitation of Members for a variety of purposes.  These include Member-sponsored events, meetings and tours as well as Commission-led purposes such as public tours, schools programme, Youth Assembly and community engagement events. The Assembly Commission’s Corporate Strategy 2023-2028 identifies one of its four priority areas as:“The public will understand and value the role of the Assembly and be engaged in its work.”To fulfil this priority, the Engagement Team works with the community and voluntary sector to promote understanding of the work of the Assembly and encourage groups to visit or ask Members to sponsor an event in Parliament Buildings. The team also has a presence at high footfall events to engage with attendees, extend an invitation to visit Parliament Buildings, give information on who their local Members are, and how they can get involved with the business of the Assembly. A new Visitor Experience Team will support parliament Buildings tours and events, with a specific, unique focus on the visitor, making sure that every person who comes to Parliament Buildings is appropriately welcomed and informed and ensuring visitors have a positive and memorable experience. Finally, schools from across Northern Ireland come to Parliament Buildings to learn about the Assembly and engage with their Members.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department will engage with providers to help secure a fibre broadband connection for residents at St. Anne's Square, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has engaged with telecoms providers, and has carried out an Open Market Review to establish existing and planned coverage of broadband services over the next three years.  The information provided in response to the Open Market Review indicated that the premises in St. Anne’s Square are in the deployment plans of commercial operators for the provision of gigabit-capable broadband in that timeframe.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the scope of his proposed Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: It is my intention to introduce a Dilapidation bill to the Assembly in the autumn, subject of course to the agreement of my Executive colleagues. 		The proposed Bill would provide councils with a modern and fit-for-purpose regime, including clear powers to tackle dilapidated buildings and neglected sites. Powers would be similar to those available to local authorities in other parts of the UK.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the grass verges at the Fortwilliam and Dargan Road roundabout will be cut.
answer_text: Grass cutting operations in the North Belfast area are scheduled to commence on 27 June 2024 and last for approximately 2 weeks.The grass verges at the Fortwilliam and Dargan Road Roundabout will be cut during this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of efforts to prevent sewage pollution in bathing waters and recreation areas, such as lakes and beaches.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates NI Water’s wastewater discharges through discharge consents issued under the Water Order (as amended) 1999.  		As the regulator, my Department works with DfI and NI Water to prioritise bathing water sites for protection, with priority directed to works within 2km of bathing and shellfish waters. Whilst my Department is committed to playing its part, prevention of sewage pollution is dependent on a range of bodies and adequate funding being available to upgrade the sewerage infrastructure.	My Department will continue to conduct targeted studies to identify the sources at failing sites and develop the appropriate measures for implementation to improve water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether NI Water will increase the levels and extent of monitoring of sewage discharges.
answer_text: The monitoring of sewage discharges is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water is taking forward a multi-year investment programme to install Event Duration Monitors (EDMs) across its wastewater network, to monitor and record the times when a spill is occurring from a storm overflow and also identify the duration of a spill. The first phase of this EDM deployment, as agreed with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, is focused on discharges to designated bathing and shellfish waters.NI Water recognises that EDMs are key instruments in addressing wastewater discharges from storm overflows and the Company has significantly stepped up its focus on both their installation and effective use.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the application by Brantwood Football Club to the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: I have formally signed off on a programme of investment and agreed a set of key principles to frame the longer-term vision of The Northern Ireland Football Fund.My officials are finalising proposals but no call for applications has yet been opened.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on funding support for the Northern Ireland Children's Hospice.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the major capital works scheme at Seaview Primary School, North Belfast.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the installation of a pedestrian crossing at Carnmoney Road, Glengormley.
answer_text: Subject to confirmation of budget allocations, it is hoped to progress installation of the new pedestrian crossing at Carnmoney Road, Glengormley within the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will seek to increase the level of funded nursery places at TLC Nursery, North Belfast, for 2024-25.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is tasked with securing sufficient pre-school provision in local areas.  Stage One of the pre-school admissions process concluded on 25 April with 98.33% of children offered a funded pre-school place; 89.2% at their parents’ first preference setting.  Stage Two of the admissions process is currently underway and parents will be advised of the outcome on 11 June. The EA has confirmed that sufficient funded pre-school places remain in all areas, with 116 funded pre-school places still available within a two-mile radius of TLC Nursery, Belfast at the close of Stage One of the pre-school admissions process.There are therefore no plans to increase the level of funded pre-school places at TLC Nursery.  The EA PEG will continue to monitor pre-school provision in all areas including North Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the request for an extension of double yellow lines to prevent issues around pedestrian safety on Ashgrove Road, directly facing Ashgrove Primary School, Glengormley.
answer_text: My officials have met and discussed the issues with stakeholders including the PSNI Road Policing Unit. An extension of the waiting restrictions has been agreed and the necessary legislation is now being progressed.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. We all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether access to Enhertu will be made available for HER2 low breast cancer patients in Northern Ireland, as it is in Scotland.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. Where NICE has recommended trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for use, it is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity.NICE are currently considering the use of trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for treating HER2-low metastatic or unresectable breast cancer in adults. A date for final guidance is to be confirmed.Under the extant HSC Managed Entry process, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) which is responsible for commissioning, may look to decisions made by other relevant UK bodies to inform commissioning decisions in the absence of a NICE determination. In this case, trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) is currently accepted for use in Northern Ireland for HER2-low metastatic breast cancer in line with the Scottish Medicines Consortium (SMC) advice and is available to patients in NI on an interim commissioning (cost per case) basis, pending any recommendation from NICE to inform longer-term commissioning decisions.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfIn the interim, the Department’s SPPG, in line with the SMC advice, where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity, will continue to make this drug available to patients in NI on an interim commissioning (cost per case) basis, until the NICE final guidance is published.My Department currently has no plans to engage with NICE on the new severity modifier system. Whilst there is a process to check the guidance for legal and policy applicability here, there is no reassessment of the clinical or cost evidence used by NICE in coming to its decisions and forming its advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will engage with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence on the new severity modifier system to seek redesignation of secondary breast cancer, as it is presently assessed as moderate severity.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. Where NICE has recommended trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for use, it is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity.NICE are currently considering the use of trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for treating HER2-low metastatic or unresectable breast cancer in adults. A date for final guidance is to be confirmed.Under the extant HSC Managed Entry process, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) which is responsible for commissioning, may look to decisions made by other relevant UK bodies to inform commissioning decisions in the absence of a NICE determination. In this case, trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) is currently accepted for use in Northern Ireland for HER2-low metastatic breast cancer in line with the Scottish Medicines Consortium (SMC) advice and is available to patients in NI on an interim commissioning (cost per case) basis, pending any recommendation from NICE to inform longer-term commissioning decisions.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfIn the interim, the Department’s SPPG, in line with the SMC advice, where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity, will continue to make this drug available to patients in NI on an interim commissioning (cost per case) basis, until the NICE final guidance is published.My Department currently has no plans to engage with NICE on the new severity modifier system. Whilst there is a process to check the guidance for legal and policy applicability here, there is no reassessment of the clinical or cost evidence used by NICE in coming to its decisions and forming its advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department's assessment of (i) Enhertu being commissioned for use by patients with secondary breast cancer; and (ii) the need for provision of other antibody drug conjugates in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. Where NICE has recommended trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for use, it is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity.NICE are currently considering the use of trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) for treating HER2-low metastatic or unresectable breast cancer in adults. A date for final guidance is to be confirmed.Under the extant HSC Managed Entry process, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) which is responsible for commissioning, may look to decisions made by other relevant UK bodies to inform commissioning decisions in the absence of a NICE determination. In this case, trastuzumab deruxtecan (Enhertu) is currently accepted for use in Northern Ireland for HER2-low metastatic breast cancer in line with the Scottish Medicines Consortium (SMC) advice and is available to patients in NI on an interim commissioning (cost per case) basis, pending any recommendation from NICE to inform longer-term commissioning decisions.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdfIn the interim, the Department’s SPPG, in line with the SMC advice, where infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity, will continue to make this drug available to patients in NI on an interim commissioning (cost per case) basis, until the NICE final guidance is published.My Department currently has no plans to engage with NICE on the new severity modifier system. Whilst there is a process to check the guidance for legal and policy applicability here, there is no reassessment of the clinical or cost evidence used by NICE in coming to its decisions and forming its advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department made any funding allocations, including to Health and Social Care Services and non-departmental organisations, after 31 March 2024 to assist in meeting financial deficits in the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: An additional funding allocation of £4.5 million was issued to Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and made to the Health and Social Care Trusts after 31 March 2024 to meet financial deficits for the 2023-24 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether late allocations from her Department could have been used to assist with 2023-24 financial year pressures faced by the NI Children's Hospice.
answer_text: It was for the Health Minister to consider if additional funding could be utilised from the late easements identified to assist with the financial pressures facing the NI Children’s Hospice.I did not receive any bids from the Department of Health for this funding in 2023-24.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the efforts to have the roads in Willowbank, Newtownabbey, adopted; and (ii) whether there is a statutory time limit after which the Department and NI Water can call on the bonds for the development.
answer_text: The developer of Willowbrook is working to resolve remedial issues within the development’s drainage systems. The developer has advised that when the drainage works are successfully completed, the final surfacing of the roads will be progressed. The department is continuing to monitor the works on the site. A bond agreement does not specify a particular time period for a developer to complete the streets, however, my department encourages developers to be proactive in bringing their developments to an adoptable standard. The Department is not obliged under The Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980 to serve notice or carry out any enforcement actions necessary to bring a development up to an adoptable standard. The powers to serve notice and undertake works are discretionary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his review into further education.
answer_text: The Review is at the stage whereby a number of workstreams have been identified through a co-design process between my Department and the six Further Education Colleges. My officials will shortly be presenting these to me for my determination for next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her legislative plans for the Northern Ireland Fiscal Council.
answer_text: I will be seeking Executive agreement from the Executive in the coming weeks on a draft policy document detailing the content of a Fiscal Council Bill that will place the Council on a statutory footing. Prior to the restoration of the Executive, my Department working with the Office of the Legislative Counsel, had undertaken significant preparatory work in relation to the drafting of this Bill. Therefore it is my intention, subject to Executive agreement, that the formal drafting of the Fiscal Council Bill can be quickly completed, and this important legislation progressed in the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department is aware of recent road traffic collisions at the Glenville Road and Shore Road Junction, Newtownabbey; and (ii) what immediate actions he will take to improve road safety at the junction.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the recent road traffic collisions at the Glenville Road and Shore Road junction.Officials have reviewed the recent collision history and, having consulted with the PSNI Roads Policing Unit, plan to renew and extend the yellow box markings in the coming weeks. This will provide additional visibility. One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will introduce a financial support scheme for households’ transition to green energy.
answer_text: Financial assistance is presently available through NISEP, for fully supported, and part funded schemes, for the installation of energy efficiency measures and heat pumps, with the majority of funding targeted at lower income customers.  My department is providing additional funding to NISEP to support energy efficiency programmes in the current financial year, with plans to further enhance support for heat pump installations and energy efficiency programmes over the next two financial years.		Additionally, my department officials are in the final stages of preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme. The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps.  This will be followed by a further consultation on evidence-based options for future Energy Efficiency support measures.		Departmental officials are also working on a renewable electricity support scheme design which will incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects. Options for microgeneration support, including domestic renewable generation such as solar panels, will continue to be evaluated as part of the ongoing delivery of the Energy Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he intends to legislate for the regulation of home heating oil pricing.
answer_text: If we were to introduce regulations it would require primary legislation to establish the regulatory framework. It is also worth noting that price controls will do little to tackle the underlying causes of oil price fluctuations as the price regulator would have to allow the pass-through of legitimate input costs, most notably the crude oil price. Additionally, the costs of regulation, including administrative and other indirect costs, could add further upward pressure on consumer’s bills. At the present time, the Competition and Markets Authority has jurisdiction to investigate and take action to address anti-competitive practices. In terms of consumer protection against unfair trading practices, my Department’s Trading Standards Services (TSS) has the power to investigate complaints of this nature and can take enforcement action where consumer protection regulations have been breached, including around the sale and delivery of home heating oil. The Executive’s Energy Strategy, “The Path to Net Zero Energy,” sets out a pathway to decarbonising our economy and phasing out fossil fuels. This will end the use of heating oil and serve to protect consumers as we work to decouple all energy sources for heating from the global fossil fuel markets.For these reasons I am not currently planning to regulate the oil industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to the Minister of Finance’s Written Ministerial Statement on 2023-24 Final Plan – Late Allocations, to detail why his Department was unable to utilise additional funding in 2023-24 to provide funding to the Northern Ireland Hospice.
answer_text: Given the funding was only made available after the end of the financial year the Department were unable to utilise the additional funding in 2023-24 as any payment made to the Hospice at that point in time would have been charged to the 2024-25 budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider installing speed bumps on Clonbeg Drive, Rathcoole.
answer_text: Requests for traffic calming measures are assessed in order of the date of request as and when resources permit. Potential schemes are scored against the criteria set out in policy and prioritised accordingly. Schemes at the top of the priority list are considered for inclusion in the traffic calming programme, the extent of which will depend on the relative priority and the resource and funding available. Departmental officials will organise for a traffic calming assessment to be undertaken at Clonbeg Drive, Rathcoole, and will respond directly to you with the findings in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department's road resurfacing plans for North Belfast for the 2024-25 financial year will be published.
answer_text: Now that the Executive has agreed the 2024-25 Budget, my Department is considering how its funding will be allocated across its functions including road resurfacing. Once this is finalised my Roads Divisions will complete and publish their programmes. As part of my Department's annual report to councils the programme for North Belfast will be delivered to Belfast City Council at their meeting which has been scheduled for the Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's support for Public Realm works in Glengormley town centre.
answer_text: Further to my reply to your previous AQW 7135/22-27, I have written to the Minister for Infrastructure on the 2 May 2024 to press for a timeline for a decision on a final route for the Belfast Rapid Transit 2 project (BRT2), and to underscore the strategic importance of the Public Realm Scheme for Glengormley. Until a decision on the route of BRT2 is taken, my Department cannot commit to the Glengormley Scheme.My officials will continue to work closely with Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council and monitor the outcome of the decision-making process for the route of BRT2.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will launch a replacement scheme to the Boiler Replacement Scheme.
answer_text: Approvals to the Boiler Replacement Scheme were paused while the impact of a reduced capital allocation was assessed and then on 21 September 2023, my Department announced the closure of the Scheme due to budgetary constraints.Prior to the closure, the Scheme had seen a significant decrease in both the number of enquiries and application forms received in the previous two financial years.  It was therefore decided to consolidate resources into the Affordable Warmth Scheme.My Department is currently in the early stages of developing a new fuel poverty intervention that will replace the current Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends in March 2026. This new scheme will align with the Climate Change Act, the Energy and Green Growth Strategies and support the principles of the new Fuel Poverty Strategy. This intervention will continue to assist the most vulnerable low-income households by improving the energy efficiency of their homes. Whilst not a direct replacement to the Boiler Replacement Scheme, low carbon heating solutions will be considered as a priority along with other energy efficiency and decarbonisation measures within the design of the new scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has plans to expand the number of places available at (i) Park Special School; and (ii) Longstone Special School.
answer_text: (i) Park Special SchoolThe Education Authority (EA) is progressing a scheme to deliver internal reconfiguration works to provide an additional classroom, multi-purpose room and hygiene room at Park Special School.  Works are expected to commence this month and complete in August 2024 in advance of the new school year.   (ii) Longstone Special SchoolTo meet increased demand for places at Longstone Special School, the EA is delivering works to enhance satellite provision at its Dundonald headquarters site with four modular classrooms in planning to be delivered for September 2024.  As a result of my statement to the Assembly on 30 April 2024, the EA is currently engaging with the school to consider the longer-term need for places and develop a master plan for the school.  The School Enhancement Programme (SEP) project currently in planning at the school may subsequently be adapted, if required, to increase the available places.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following the meeting with his Expert Panel on 1 May 2024, whether he can now provide a definition of a good job, as outlined in his economic vision.
answer_text: Officials in my Department have engaged with a broad range of experts on the development of a definition appropriate to local circumstances. Advice will be presented to Minister Murphy upon his return.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions he has had with Belfast City Council in relation to the vesting of the Tribeca site, Belfast.
answer_text: I have not had any discussions with Belfast City Council in relation to the vesting of the Tribeca site, Belfast.Following a recent request for a meeting by the Council’s Chief Executive Officer, John Walsh, I have asked that Council initially meet with senior officials from my Department on this matter.  At the time of this AQW officials are awaiting contact from Council to schedule the meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on (i) his Department's work to monitor river water quality; and (ii) the prosecution of the polluters.
answer_text: My Department monitors 450 river waterbodies under the Water Framework Directive (WFD) Water Framework Regulations (WFR) which includes 55 cross border river waterbodies. A monitoring network of over 530 monitoring stations is reviewed on a yearly basis with the aim of continuing to provide a robust WFR classification. Additional monitoring is undertaken outside of the WFR for regulated discharges, areas of concern like the Mobuoy site and when deemed necessary after pollution events.My Department takes its responsibilities around water pollution incidents very seriously. Such incidents can present risks to human and animal health as well as to the economy and to the environment. When incidents are identified, NIEA will investigate and take appropriate action depending on the significance of the offence, with enforcement action initiated for all high and medium severity water pollution incidents where the polluter can be identified. To date in 2024 there have been 8 successful prosecution cases through the Court for water pollution.  This has resulted in fines totalling £7,500 from seven cases with the sentencing for the remaining case planned for later in May 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department works with the agricultural industry to help reduce and prevent river pollution.
answer_text: My Department engages with the industry through a range of schemes, programmes and knowledge transfer events with the aim of reducing and preventing river pollution from agricultural sources.Through the Environmental Farming Scheme capital grant support has been provided to farmers for water quality improvement measures. Some 2500 km of watercourses have been protected from livestock access and soil erosion and 200 km of riparian margins have been created.The Sustainable Catchment Programme (SCP) provides both advisory farm visits and capital grant support to carry out works which aim to prevent pollution and improve water quality. The SCP includes support for riparian margins, water course protection, strategic planting of hedges and trees and pollution prevention measures in farmyards. The Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) aims to improve water quality by protecting against pollution from agricultural sources. To help farmers meet the requirements of the NAP, the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) provides a suite of online nutrient calculators.  CAFRE also deliver programmes for farmers at various levels on nutrient management planning to help them apply manures and chemical fertilisers based on soil analysis and crop need.The Soil Nutrient Health Scheme (SNHS) enables Northern Ireland farmers to optimise crop nutrient applications, assess on-farm carbon stocks and build farm resilience. As part of the scheme, CAFRE has developed an online training course for participating farmers to enable them to use their soil analysis data to improve nutrient management planning, farm profitability and water quality.CAFRE also recently delivered two Slurry Management events focussed on utilising slurry efficiently and protecting water quality. Over 400 farmers attended.The Sustainable Use of Livestock Slurry (SULS) initiative seeks to increase sustainable processing of livestock slurry to separate excess phosphorus and generate renewable energy. While currently at research and development stage, the ultimate aim is to provide intensive farms with routes to process slurry, reduce farm nutrient surpluses and help to improve water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the representations he has received from taxi operators for changes to current legislation.
answer_text: I have not received any direct representations from taxi operators for changes to current taxi legislation, however a number of AQWs have been received. I can confirm that my officials are aware of some areas of concern for operators through their engagement with the industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on his Department’s review on air connectivity.
answer_text: Research commissioned by the Department titled “Air Connectivity to Support Inbound Tourism and Business Growth” was completed, as scheduled, at the end March 2024. This research, which includes a number of recommendations, is currently under review, within the Department before wider dissemination and publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to meet with the Licensed Taxi Operators Association to discuss the need for legislative change.
answer_text: I have not received a request to meet with the Licenced Taxi Operators Association.   I am aware that officials within my Department are in contact with a representative from the Association, and that my Permanent Secretary has agreed to meet with a PR company representing them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9597/22-27, to explain why he has no plans to update the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.
answer_text: I have many competing priorities within my Department. A review of the Taxis Act would involve significant resources, take considerable time to conclude, and could involve major legislative change. However, I can assure you that my Department is committed to working with and helping the taxi industry address the range of issues that it faces.I hope you find this information helpful.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions he has had since taking to office aimed at securing direct flights between Northern Ireland and the United States of America.
answer_text: An important element of the Air Connectivity Research, commissioned by my Department, was direct engagement with a broad range of stakeholders, including local airports, key airlines, InvestNI and tourism organisations to identify and assess what our strategic objectives and policy levers should be going forward in the current highly constrained budget operating environment. 	My Department consistently looks for ways we can enhance and support our air connectivity, and in addition Tourism Ireland (TI) continues to provide co-operative marketing support to airlines operating to all three of our local airports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is considering any policy changes to support the sustainable protection of listed thatched buildings.
answer_text: My Department has no formal plans to change policy at present. However, we will keep this under review as we work with owners and other stakeholders to ensure that the value of these buildings can be fully enjoyed and realised by all of our community.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail his Department’s commitment to preserving listed thatched buildings; and (ii) how his Department intends to achieve this.
answer_text: My department aims to help communities enjoy and realise the value of the historic environment. Supporting the preservation, and continued use, of listed thatched buildings is an important part of this commitment. It is a public good that those which remain are sustained for the benefit, use and appreciation of all. My department seeks to improve public awareness of the importance of these buildings through publications, seminars, and talks. Its architects provide advice and technical publications to help owners and, where required, seek to encourage sustainable reuse. They comment upon planning applications that might have an impact and manage our Historic Environment Fund (where thatch roofs are a priority for support).  My department will continue to work with owners to sustain this important resource.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the connectivity of Ulster University Belfast and Shore Road, Belfast, by foot; and (ii) what immediate actions he will take to improve the connectivity.
answer_text: I can advise that the Department is considering a number of proposals to improve connectivity in and around Ulster University and Shore Road Belfast to encourage more pedestrian movements and wheeled users. The measures under consideration include:Options to improve pedestrian and cycling connectivity between North Belfast and the Ulster University city centre area;Options for improvements to active travel and connectivity along York Street, to York Street Railway Station, as part of York Street Interchange scheme;A proposal to widen the footway outside the main University building and relocation of the bus stops;Completion of the consultation and review of the Experimental Order for York Street;Completion of the upgrade of the York Street / Frederick Street traffic signal junction; andEastern Transport Plan – draft vision and objectives include connectivity and safety aspects. Further information can be found on the Department’s website at the following link: Eastern Transport Plan (ETP) 2035 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether it is an efficient use of Health and Social Care Trust resources for patients, who have already received a private ADHD diagnosis, to remain on a Trust assessment waiting list for the same in order to obtain the required funded GP prescription.
answer_text: Service users accepted onto an ADHD waiting list with a private ADHD diagnosis will be placed on a waiting list in chronological order of their referral in line with Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP) guidelines. They will also be advised that they may remain under private health provision, including paying for treatments, such as prescriptions, until they reach their advised appointment date. Having a private diagnosis will not change their position on the waiting list.Trusts will assess the private assessment process against compliance with National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines. If the diagnostic assessment complies with NICE, the diagnosis will be accepted and ongoing support will be provided. If the assessment does not meet with NICE guidelines, a HSC clinician will complete the diagnosis. Drugs prescribed to treat ADHD are controlled and it is advised that they are prescribed initially by a Consultant. With a private ADHD diagnosis anyone requesting a prescription through the HSC, will have to be assessed by the HSC prescribing clinician to assess their medical need.  Drug treatment for children, young people and adults with ADHD should always form part of a comprehensive treatment plan that includes psychological, behavioural and educational advice and interventions (or occupational needs in the case of adults). If prescribing under shared care agreements it should be clear where responsibility for prescribing lies.HSCB wrote to private health providers and GP’s in June 2021 of their duty to inform patients that there may be ongoing costs associated with their private health care treatment until such time as they are seen by a HSC clinician.I hope this information is helpful and provides some clarity to you on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9256/22-27, (i) for an update on efforts to address parking issues on Hightown Road; and (ii) when the additional road markings will be in place here.
answer_text: The line painting for the waiting restrictions at this location was completed on Sunday 28th April 2024.The Legislation for the waiting restrictions is currently being progressed. However, as this can be a lengthy process, my department has introduced temporary legislation to cover the extents of the proposed lining, which will enable enforcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 7940/22-27, whether there is a new anticipated completion date for the pedestrian crossing close to Whitehouse Park, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: Road markings for the new Toucan crossing on the Shore Road Newtownabbey near Whitehouse Park are programmed for Sunday 19 April 2024 and it is anticipated the signals will be fully operational soon after this date. Unfortunately, work has been delayed due to prior contractual commitments of the contractor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how his Department intends to reduce waiting times for Ear, Nose and Throat referrals; and (ii) whether there is a strategic plan to reduce these waiting times.
answer_text: Bringing the waiting lists in Northern Ireland to an acceptable level across a range of specialties demands a long-term collective effort requiring sustained and substantial investment through multi-year budgets, workforce development and system wide transformation.  The Elective Care Framework, published in June 2021, sets out the overarching strategic plan for addressing hospital waiting times.I am determined to do whatever I can, within the budget I have available to me, to improve the waiting list position across all specialities, including Ear, Nose and Throat.  This includes continuing to develop the Elective Care Centre model, continuing to drive forward the productivity and efficiency agenda and transforming and modernising service delivery. As a result of the continued efforts to drive forward improvements in the regional provision of Elective Care Services, from December 2022 to December 2023 there has been a 15.9% decrease in the number of patients waiting for Ears, Nose and Throat inpatient or day case procedures in the Belfast, Northern, Southern and Western HSC Trusts.  Subject to available budget, a regional review into Ear, Nose and Throat services will take place this financial year to facilitate improvement in the extensive waiting lists in this area, to ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible ensuring best possible outcomes and to secure sustainable service delivery in this speciality.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will seek to action the recommendations contained in the strategy document published by the Royal College of General Practitioners, entitled A Workforce Fit for the Future.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Royal College of General Practitioners  ‘A Workforce Fit for the Future’ Retention Strategy and was pleased to speak at its recent launch.  I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice that are set out in the strategy and the impact of the increasing demand and workload pressures on GPs that it highlights.  My Department is working with GPs to help General Practice to deliver, to grow and to be in the vanguard of how we transform health and social care for our population and for our workforce. My Department has taken forward a range of actions that will help address a number of the workforce and workload challenges and recommendations set out in the RCGP strategy.  These include:  	Focussing on building the GP workforce, including the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme, and increasing the number of GP training places to its current level of 121.Taking steps to increase the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care through the roll out of Multi-disciplinary Teams and the introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses and General Practice Pharmacists; all of which are making a difference to the capacity of primary care services and how those services are delivered; and are contributing to improved patient outcomes.Taking speedy action to progress the 2023/24 Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Board recommendation for GPs.  I recently approved the DDRB recommendation of a 6% uplift for independent General Medical Practitioners.  In addition, I also approved an uplift of 6% for staff expenses and a 7.3% uplift (3% recurrent) for other expenses.  Providing significant financial support to help stabilise primary care services.  This has included £3.4m funding to General Medical Services and Out of Hours Services to support the capacity of GP practices to meet the anticipated increase in demand over the winter period.  My Department also made £4.3m funding available to assist GP practices to deliver proactive support and care to those in nursing and residential care homes this winter.  In addition to this, £2.7m was reallocated from other areas of the GP budget to boost capacity in the service and help manage demand.  This included non-recurrent funding support for additional costs of indemnity.  Continuing to take forward work to look at the future model of GP indemnity provision in Northern Ireland.  This includes taking forward options appraisal through the business case process which will conclude with the identification of a proposed preferred way forward for indemnity arrangements in Northern Ireland. Establishing the GP Access Working Group to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for both patients and practices.		The development of guidance that will improve management of the interface between primary and secondary care.  	E

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the recommendations contained in the strategy document published by the Royal College of General Practitioners, entitled A Workforce Fit for the Future.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the Royal College of General Practitioners  ‘A Workforce Fit for the Future’ Retention Strategy and was pleased to speak at its recent launch.  I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice that are set out in the strategy and the impact of the increasing demand and workload pressures on GPs that it highlights.  My Department is working with GPs to help General Practice to deliver, to grow and to be in the vanguard of how we transform health and social care for our population and for our workforce. My Department has taken forward a range of actions that will help address a number of the workforce and workload challenges and recommendations set out in the RCGP strategy.  These include:  	Focussing on building the GP workforce, including the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme, and increasing the number of GP training places to its current level of 121.Taking steps to increase the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care through the roll out of Multi-disciplinary Teams and the introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses and General Practice Pharmacists; all of which are making a difference to the capacity of primary care services and how those services are delivered; and are contributing to improved patient outcomes.Taking speedy action to progress the 2023/24 Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Board recommendation for GPs.  I recently approved the DDRB recommendation of a 6% uplift for independent General Medical Practitioners.  In addition, I also approved an uplift of 6% for staff expenses and a 7.3% uplift (3% recurrent) for other expenses.  Providing significant financial support to help stabilise primary care services.  This has included £3.4m funding to General Medical Services and Out of Hours Services to support the capacity of GP practices to meet the anticipated increase in demand over the winter period.  My Department also made £4.3m funding available to assist GP practices to deliver proactive support and care to those in nursing and residential care homes this winter.  In addition to this, £2.7m was reallocated from other areas of the GP budget to boost capacity in the service and help manage demand.  This included non-recurrent funding support for additional costs of indemnity.  Continuing to take forward work to look at the future model of GP indemnity provision in Northern Ireland.  This includes taking forward options appraisal through the business case process which will conclude with the identification of a proposed preferred way forward for indemnity arrangements in Northern Ireland. Establishing the GP Access Working Group to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for both patients and practices.		The development of guidance that will improve management of the interface between primary and secondary care.  	E

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will approve the use of Rule 84 of the Housing Selection Scheme to grant a resident leaseholder the tenancy of the sold flat due to the high value works at Green End, Rathcoole.
answer_text: I refer you to my response to AQW 9891/22-27 issued on 18/04/2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has considered dualling the Dargan Bridge to allow for additional train services during the construction phase of the York Street Interchange Project.
answer_text: The York Street Interchange project includes proposals to strengthen the Dargan Viaduct foundations and piers, to facilitate the potential widening of the viaduct in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) why funding for the North Belfast Principals’ Forum was removed by the Education Authority; and (ii) whether the Minister will consider its reinstatement.
answer_text: As a result of significant budgetary pressures in advance of the 2023/24 financial year, and in the absence of a budget allocation to the Department of Education by the Secretary of State, the Department made the difficult decision not to fund the North Belfast Primary Principals Support Programme in 2023/24.Work is ongoing within my Department on the design of a programme to reduce educational disadvantage linked to the report “A Fair Start”. The programme will offer the opportunity to look afresh at the issues caused by deprivation and to drive forward a whole community place-based approach to education.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Urban Villages Initiative in North Belfast.
To ask the Minister for Communities when his Department’s review of Neighbourhood Renewal will be completed.
answer_text: The Review of the Executive’s People and Place strategy has now resulted in the development of an outline Strategic Framework, informed by the views expressed through the stakeholder and sectoral engagement exercise to date.That Strategic Framework will then be subject to my consideration and in due course that of my Executive colleagues.  The current planning assumption is that the Strategic Framework will be subject to a full public consultation exercise and then finalised within the 2025/26 financial year.  Implementation will then commence in the 2026/27 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the future of the Laganside Bus Station after the opening of Belfast Grand Central Station.
answer_text: As I outlined in my statement to the Assembly last month, the phased opening of Belfast Grand Central Station commencing later this year has the potential to cause significant traffic and travel disruption until the project is fully completed by the end of 2025. During this time additional bus substitution and priority measures will be introduced and Laganside Bus Station will be a key transport hub as part of this plan to help reduce congestion in Belfast city.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the support his Department offers to Orange Halls.
answer_text: Orange Halls may be able to access some funding streams operated by the Department, subject to meeting the eligibility criteria and evidenced need, I am currently considering what further support can be provided in terms of community infrastructure in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why the labour market statistic, published by his Department each month, no longer includes a comparison of Northern Ireland's unemployment rate with (i) the Republic of Ireland; (ii) other regions of the United Kingdom; and (iii) the EU.
answer_text: Due to concerns over the quality of Labour Force Survey estimates in Great Britain, the table including comparisons with UK regions was not included in NISRA’s publication from October 2023 to January 2024 and in March 2024. ONS have since addressed these quality concerns and the comparison table was included in the most recent Labour Market Report, published in April 2024.Unemployment rates for ROI and the EU have not been included in the Labour Market Report since February 2021 due to differences in the methodologies employed to calculate unemployment rates. The UK headline unemployment definition is based on the population aged 16 and over, whereas the ROI and the EU use the aged 15 to 74 population to measure headline unemployment.NISRA held a user consultation in the summer of 2023 where users were asked to comment on the content of the Labour Market Report and no feedback was received on the decision not to include unemployment rate estimates for ROI or the EU in this report. NISRA welcomes further user feedback and is currently investigating the possibility of including a table of comparisons for a range of countries.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on investment plans for the Newtownabbey campus of the Northern Regional College.
answer_text: Following engagement by my officials with Northern Regional College, it has confirmed plans of a multi-year series of phased enhancements to improve accessibility, curriculum delivery and energy efficiency at its Newtownabbey campus. I expect the College to bring formal proposals to my department in due course, which will be assessed in terms of need and in line with the availability of capital funding.The College continues to utilise Minor Works funding on a yearly basis to upgrade curriculum delivery and its estate up to the value of £1 million per project, pending the availability of capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will reinstate unemployment rate comparisons in the monthly labour market statistics published by his Department.
answer_text: The monthly labour market report produced by NISRA contains comparison details for Scotland, Wales and England regions. Due to differences in the methodologies employed to calculate unemployment rates here compared to both the ROI and EU, these estimates have not been included in the Labour Market Report since February 2021. The UK headline unemployment definition is based on the aged 16 to 64 population whereas the ROI and the EU use the aged 15 to 74 population to measure headline unemployment.NISRA is currently investigating the possibility of including a table of comparisons for ROI and the EU countries.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his Department's assessment of the benefits of insulation schemes available to private home owners in Great Britain which are not currently replicated in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Improving the energy efficiency of our homes is a vital part of addressing fuel poverty and carbon reduction. Cavity wall insulation is recognised as a cost-effective way to reduce home energy bills, improve thermal comfort, and contribute to lower carbon emissions.My Departments Affordable Warmth Scheme is designed to help those on lower incomes, and who are living in private accommodation, with the costs of energy efficiency measures. The Scheme offers a range of prioritised energy efficiency measures and recognises the importance of insulation by placing it as a Priority 1 measure along with ventilation and draught-proofing.My Officials are currently in the early stages of developing a new fuel poverty energy efficiency scheme that will replace the Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends. This new scheme will align with the Energy and Green Growth Strategies, Climate Change Act and support the principles of the new Fuel Poverty Strategy. Insulation will continue to be considered as a priority along with other energy efficiency and decarbonisation measures within the design of a new scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will consider the future commissioning of adult ADHD services in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Department of Health officials are regularly reviewing the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years. Any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of budget availability. Department of Health officials are considering how best to progress the issue, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Subject to future budget availability, potential next steps include the commissioning of research/analysis of adult ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, as well as a regional workshop to explore potential ways forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to widen the income thresholds for access to schemes such as Affordable Warmth, which help to reduce the carbon footprint of households.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the early stages of developing a new fuel poverty energy efficiency scheme that will replace the Affordable Warmth Scheme when it ends in March 2026. This new scheme will align with the Energy and Green Growth Strategies, Climate Change Act and support the principles of the new Fuel Poverty Strategy. The issue of widening the income and eligibility thresholds forms part of this work and is currently a key element of the pre-consultation stakeholder engagement workshops which are running between April – June 2024. My officials will also explore the option of utilising low carbon heating solutions along with other energy efficiency and decarbonisation measures within the design of the new scheme.However, I have no plans to review the income threshold of the existing Scheme which ends March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many fines have been issued for the unlawful renting of short-term let tourist accommodation, under the Tourism (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 since 2019, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Since 2019, one fine has been issued by Tourism NI for the unlawful renting of short-term let tourist accommodation under the Tourism (Northern Ireland) Order 1992.  This property was in the North Down constituency area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Enhanced Investment Zone.
answer_text: I have stated my preference to harness the British Government’s Investment Zone policy to support our green technologies and skills for the green economy. Following the publication of its Enhanced IZ policy prospectus on 26 March, my officials have scoped out the operational and resource requirements to commence a co-design process with the Levelling Up department in London. I expect to have a dedicated team in place soon and, more generally, I will appraise Executive colleagues and the Committee as the co-design process evolves over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the commissioning of new treatments for migraines being made available following National Institute for Health and Care Excellence approval, including Rimegepant and Atogepant.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland. This process is relevant to the commissioning of treatments for migraines.In Northern Ireland, rimegepant (Vydura®) is accepted for use as an option for the acute treatment of migraine with or without aura in adults (NICE TA919) and as an option for preventing episodic migraine in adults who have at least 4 and fewer than 15 migraine attacks per month, only if at least 3 preventative treatments have not worked (TA906)NICE are currently considering the use of atogepant (Aquipta®) and a final determination is expected to be published in May 2024. Under the extant HSC Managed Entry process, my Department may look to decisions made by other relevant UK bodies to inform commissioning decisions in the absence of a NICE determination. Atogepant (Aquipta®) is already accepted for use for the prophylaxis of migraine in adults who have at least 4 migraine days per month in line with advice from the Scottish Medicines Consortium (SMC2259). This position will be reviewed following publication of final NICE guidance, to inform long term commissioning decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of Ulster University's multi-campus model.
answer_text: I am supportive of Ulster University’s multi-campus model as it contributes to the delivery of higher education in a regionally balanced manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the findings of Ulster University's tourism summit.
answer_text: Findings in relation to the University of Ulster’s tourism summit in June 2023 have not been shared with the Department for the Economy. Therefore no assessment has been undertaken at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the Minister of Health has made a request for additional funding for the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice.
answer_text: I have not received a specific request for additional funding for the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice. However, once an allocation is made to the Department of Health in the upcoming Budget, it will be for the Health Minister to allocate according to the department’s own priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what further discussions he has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to additional funding for the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice.
answer_text: I believe the long-term need for financial stability across the hospice sector would benefit from cross-Departmental input, helping to assist organisations in identifying sustainable ways forward.Consequently, I wrote on Friday 09/02/2002 to the Finance Minister to seek further discussions between the charities and our two Departments. The Minister of Finance had advised that should my department need additional financial support for the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice she would be open to a discussion around this.  Senior Departmental representatives met with the NICH on 3rd April 2024 to discuss the current situation in respect of funding, costs and service delivery. They are due to met with NICH on the 9th May and at this time I will have an understanding from NICH about the level of funding they will require to sustain services in the short and longer term. Following this meeting I can make decisions regarding representations for additional funding to the Minister of Finance if this is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what financial support her Department can provide to K9 Search and Rescue (NI).
answer_text: My Department provides financial support to a number of Voluntary Search and Rescue (VSAR) organisations through the NI Search and Rescue (NISAR) arrangements overseen by my Department. Those VSAR organisations who are members of the NISAR Practitioners Group can avail of financial support on an annual basis via an application bid, and subject to budget availability.I am aware that K9 Search and Rescue (NI) has made an application seeking membership of the NISAR Practitioners Group, which provides a route to submit applications for financial support. This application is currently being processed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what legislative changes he is considering to improve the regulation of private medical treatments, aimed at maximising patient safety.
answer_text: The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) is responsible for the regulation (registration, inspection, and enforcement) of establishments and agencies named in the Health and Personal Social Services (Quality, Improvement and Regulation) (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 (2003 Order). Establishments and agencies regulated by RQIA include Independent Hospitals, Independent Clinics, and Independent Medical Agencies as set out in the Independent Health Care Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005. As of April 2024, RQIA has 499 Independent Healthcare establishments/agencies registered with them.My Department is aware of the wider, changing health and social care service delivery environment and how this has changed greatly since the 2003 Order was introduced. Prior to the Covid-19 pandemic, the Department had developed a new draft regulatory policy framework however further development work is required, including consultation on the draft policy.My Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. In that context, work on the review of the regulation is currently paused to allow for other priority projects to progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to recent media reports, whether it his Department's position that the York Street Interchange Project will not be completed until 2035.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.Recent media reports on York Street Interchange followed a Committee for Infrastructure meeting on 10 April 2024 where correspondence I had with them was mentioned. This was subsequently reported in the main news media.The correspondence indicated that following the prioritisation of the Major Roads Projects Programme by officials in August 2023, development work on the scheme is currently paused, other than an ongoing review to consider placemaking and active travel opportunities.If a decision to proceed with the scheme is taken, there will be a need to republish the statutory orders due to the passage of time. Along with further development work this process is likely to take at least 5 years, followed by a 4-year construction period.  The scheme would therefore take an estimated total of 9 years to completion, rather than 11 years as reported. The Committee was informed that the scheme would require a 2-year period to allow for a further public inquiry, however this is included within the 5-year development period, rather than in addition to it. Clarification of this position is being provided to the Committee.Details of the current status of the York Street Interchange project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/york-street-interchange-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the development of a national training centre by the Irish Football Association.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9473/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the effectiveness of the Prohibition of Traffic ( Union Street, Belfast) Order (Northern Ireland) 2022 No. 281; (ii) why traffic continues to use the road; and (iii) what enforcement action has been undertaken by his Department.
answer_text: I can advise that officials are not aware of any particular issue with the prohibition of traffic in Union Street, Belfast and this remains a good example of how road space can be reallocated for the benefit of pedestrians / businesses.  Observations have confirmed that motorists are complying with the restrictions and that all road signage is in place.Enforcement of the prohibition is carried out by the police. Should your constituents witness any disregard of the restrictions, these should be brought to the attention of the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will bring forward Debt Relief Orders in Northern Ireland, in line with other parts of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: My Department’s debt relief scheme was introduced in 2011 and is modelled on a similar scheme in England and Wales.  Several changes have been made to the England and Wales scheme since 2021 and it is my intention to make similar changes to my Department’s scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6433/22-27, for an update on when a final decision will be made.
answer_text: I can confirm once again that my Department is aware of this urgent piece of work and is treating it as such.  I will provide an update in due course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an explanation on the level of underspend in the Victims' Payment Scheme budget for 2023-24.
answer_text: As of week commencing 27 May, almost £43 million has been paid to victims since the Scheme opened for applications on 31 August 2021.Forecasting the budget for payments to victims is challenging. There are a number of uncertainties which have a direct impact on the budget required and are unknown until applications have been determined. These include the assessed level of disablement of each eligible applicant, the choice of a lump sum or monthly payments (for those eligible) and the consideration of other payments which applicants may already be in receipt of. TEO and the Victims Payments Board continue to keep the Scheme under review to look for ways of increasing the throughput of cases.
