question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 9474/22-27, whether advice issued by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group in June 2021 will be reviewed to further improve access to ADHD-related GP prescriptions for patients who have procured a private diagnosis due to health service waiting list times.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to introduce changes regarding access to ADHD related GP prescriptions for patients who have procured a private diagnosis. ADHD medication remains an 'amber status' drug and GPs should not be asked to prescribe ADHD medications in the absence of a suitable agreed Shared Care Arrangement with the private consultant/provider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he expects to receive the business case on the future of Abbottscoole House from the Housing Executive.
answer_text: The business case is expected to go to the Housing Executive Board for approval in June.  Subject to the Board’s approval being granted, the business case will then be referred to the Department for Communities for its consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the vesting of properties and land at Fortwilliam Parade, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department received a Vesting Application for Fortwilliam Parade from the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) on the 24 November 2023. Departmental officials are working to ensure that this application is progressed as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how much has been spent on scaffolding at Green End Flats, Rathcoole; and (ii) for his assessment of the ongoing delay to fix the roofs at Green End Flats, Rathcoole.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“To date scaffolding costs are £139,975.75 including erection of scaffold and then the weekly hire costs.Following the collapse of the parapet wall on to the existing flat roof at blocks 5 and 6 at Green End, the tender documents for the intended roof replacement and External Cyclical Maintenance scheme were amended to reflect a revised scheme that would include additional works necessary to repair the damage and address the causes of the collapse.  This scheme was tendered in October 2023 but there were no responses.  The scheme was therefore re-tendered in November 2023 and three tender submissions were received.  However, the lowest tenderer subsequently withdrew, and the other two tenders were not considered value for money, as a result of which that tender exercise has been stood down.  Consequently, in light of cost inflation in the construction sector NIHE have revised the estimated costs for the scheme and are currently urgently exploring a number of options for delivering the works, which includes reviewing existing contracts as well as further procurements.The Housing Executive is conscious of the impact of the inability to deliver this scheme for their tenants and are taking forward a resolution of the issue as quickly as possible.”  I appreciate that any delays in addressing such matters are difficult for residents. My officials are aware of the NIHE’s progress with tenders and the issues it has experienced. Matters such as these are monitored via the Partnership Agreement between my Department and the NIHE. This Agreement explains the overall governance framework within which the NIHE operates, including the framework through which the necessary assurances are provided to stakeholders. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on efforts to reduce the backlog of children's autism assessments, including strategies to ensure no child is disadvantaged by longer waiting times due to the Health and Social Care Trust region they live in.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he is aware of the delay in progressing the Glengormley public realm scheme as a result of the lack of decision on the proposed route of Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2; and (ii) what action he will take to bring urgent clarity to this project.
answer_text: I am aware of the delay in progressing the Glengormley public realm scheme, however to ensure the correct decision is made, officials in my department are currently finalising the feasibility studies to determine the viability of extending the route into Glengormley.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department still intends to amend the Taxi Operator Licensing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2012, as per the notice issued to operators on 8 February 2024; and (ii) whether his Department has made an assessment of the VAT implications of such changes.
answer_text: A 2021 Court Judgement in England and Wales has prompted some taxi operators to review their current operating model to ensure that it complies with existing statutory provision.  The proposal to amend the current Taxi Operator Licensing Regulations (NI) 2012 has not changed, however I have not yet made a final decision on the need for subordinate legislation here.  However, I should emphasise that any new regulations would not alter the current taxi regulatory framework.  Rather, they would simply clarify the existing requirements of the taxis Act (NI) 2008. Taxi operators may therefore wish to take legal advice on their specific operating model, including any associated VAT implications, to ensure compliance with the 2008 Act, as it already applies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to amend Class C taxi licencing.
answer_text: You will be aware that Class C vehicles can only be used on a pre-booked basis and booked at an operating centre. They cannot be hailed and cannot not be used on taxis ranks. Examples of Class C taxis are vehicles used for Chauffeur services, weddings, funeral and courtesy transport. Uber taxis also fall within the Class C clarification. I can advise that currently I have no plans to amend the definition of Class C taxis or the process to licence this class of taxis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to update the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.
answer_text: I do not have any plans to update the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the pedestrianisation of Union Street, Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department has made an order that prohibits traffic from using Union Street, Belfast from its junction with Little Donegall Street to its junction with Donegall Street.The Prohibition of Traffic (Union Street, Belfast) 2022 order came into operation on 11th December 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) why he did not inform the Committee for the Economy on 20 March 2024 of his intention to create a taskforce to look at expanding student numbers at the Ulster University Magee campus; and (ii) when he will create a similar taskforce to look at expanding student numbers at the Ulster University Coleraine campus.
answer_text: During my attendance at the Committee, I advised that there would be further announcements in relation to Magee.  In line with protocol, the Committee was notified in advance of the launch event.The Magee expansion is an NDNA commitment. There is no similar commitment for the Coleraine campus.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority will transfer unused land to the Housing Executive to develop parking at Fairhill Walk, Belfast.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has, in recent years, engaged with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) in relation to land at Fairhill Walk for potential community use purposes, including residents’ car parking. However, NIHE has formally withdrawn its interest in acquiring the land at the valuation advised by Land and Property Services. The EA has now incorporated this land into the grounds of Cedar Lodge School, to reduce illegal dumping and mitigate against further damage to the EA’s property caused by the depositing and burning of bonfire material.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why GPs cannot prescribe ADHD-related medication to patients who have received a private ADHD diagnosis.
answer_text: The prescribing of ADHD - related medication to patients who have received a private ADHD diagnosis is a matter for individual GPs. Advice issued jointly by the then HSCB (now SPPG) and my Department in June 2021, is that when a GP considers that a privately recommended treatment is clinically appropriate and chooses to prescribe, then under the HSC terms of service they can issue an HSC prescription. In doing so, the GP is accepting clinical responsibility for this decision.In reaching this decision the GP should apply the same criteria, had the request originated within an HSC clinic which may include consideration of a shared care agreement.It is recommended that where a patient requests referral to a private provider, the GP should inform the patient at the outset that they may have to pay for any ongoing treatment or medications which are recommended.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department will take to increase pedestrian safety at (i) Ashgrove Primary School, Glengormley; and (ii) St Mary’s on the Hill Primary School, Glengormley.
answer_text: Departmental officials will review the existing layout at both schools to ascertain what safety improvements are possible, if any, and will communicate their conclusions to you directly in due course.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing driver behaviour. If all of us who are drivers do not change our attitudes towards speeding and careless driving, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what work his Department will undertake to further support the Credit Union movement in Northern Ireland, to mirror support offered in other parts of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: I have set up a dedicated team to deliver the policies outlined in the recently published Irish League of Credit Unions policy manifesto.  The necessary policies and legislation will be co-designed with both the Irish League of Credit Unions and the Ulster Federation of Credit Unions. The work will be prioritised by my Department. It will proceed at pace and be delivered within the three years remaining of the mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he intends to increase the number of higher education student places available.
answer_text: I am committed to working with the higher and further education sectors to grow student numbers in a sustainable manner.	I have recently launched a Taskforce to develop and oversee an action plan to expand Ulster University’s Derry~Londonderry campus to 10,000 students.  The Taskforce will put together a comprehensive plan for expansion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed pedestrian bridge at Sailorstown, Belfast.
answer_text: The Sailortown bridge has been suggested as part of ‘Embracing the Belfast Waterfront’, a framework for the future development of the Belfast Waterfront Promenade. This is led by Maritime Belfast Trust. The proposed bridge is on land owned by Belfast Harbour Commissioners. An update should be sought from Maritime Belfast Trust and the Harbour Commissioners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration his Department has given to additional train stations in North Belfast, including at (i) Loughside Park; and (ii) the Abbey Centre.
answer_text: My Department is working towards finalising the All-Island Strategic Rail Review which considers how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation. The Review includes a recommendation to explore the case for developing new stations in Belfast. However, being at a strategic level, the Review has not considered the location of these stations. Once the Review is published, further work will be required to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support any future investment decisions, including those in relation to the development of any new rail halts along the existing network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department's efforts to adopt the roads and footways at Loughshore Manor, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: It is the responsibility of the developer of Loughshore Manor to provide a layout of streets, footways, and a bridge to the standards suitable for adoption. In this case, the developer has not, to date, provided the necessary assurances that the bridge that meets my Department’s technical approval standards. The developer is aware of these requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed pedestrian crossing at Lilian Blank Park, Ballyclare Road, Glengormley.
answer_text: My Department’s officials have assessed both Ballyclare Road and Carnmoney Road at Lilian Bland Park for controlled pedestrian crossings.  Whilst a controlled crossing on Ballyclare Road did not meet the criteria for further investigation, a proposal to provide a controlled crossing on Carnmoney Road has been approved.  Subject to the availability of funding, it is planned that the crossing on Carnmoney Road would be included in the proposed works programme for the 24/25 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's confirmed resurfacing plans for the North Belfast constituency for the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: My Departments resurfacing programmes for 2024/25 will be prepared when budgets are confirmed and will be presented to Councils in due course. A link to the report will then be issued to each MLA in that respective council area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of pay and grading for non-teaching support staff.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 9211/22-27.This answer will be contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 29 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider additional measures to address (i) road safety; and (ii) pedestrian safety concerns, caused by parked cars at the Hightown Road and Antrim Road junction, Glengormley.
answer_text: My Department are aware of the parking issue at the bottom of the Hightown Road in Glengormley. The issue has arisen since the previously free car park on the corner of the Hightown/Antrim Road became pay and display. This change has resulted in vehicles being displaced onto the lower end of the road. It is the Department’s intention to provide waiting restrictions, with the associated legislation to enable enforcement, from the Hightown Road’s junction with the Antrim Road to Farmley Crescent on its south side and Harmin Park on its north side. It is proposed that the waiting restrictions will be double yellow (no waiting at any time) waiting restrictions from the junction as far as No.5 Hightown Road and then a single yellow working day (Monday – Saturday, 8am – 6pm) waiting restriction on both sides of the road as far as Farmley Crescent and Harmin Park respectively. The consultation process has been completed and officials are currently drafting the legislation for approval.  In the interim, officials have introduced temporary legislation to cover the extents of the restrictions with the intention of providing the road markings over the Easter period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many members of staff are employed in the mental health teams in the (i) Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information provided by the Belfast and Northern Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts on the number of staff employed across adult and child mental health services within acute and community services, as at 29th February 2024, is detailed in the table below.  These figures include those employed in Administration & Clerical, Nursing & Midwifery, Social Services, Professional & Technical, Estates Services, Support Services, and Medical roles.HSCNI Staff Employed in Adult & Child Mental Health Teams, as at 29th February 2024HeadcountWhole-Time EquivalentBelfast HSC Trust1,1151,044.54Northern HSC Trust1,1971,095.45Source: HSC Trusts

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the street lighting upgrade scheme for Ivan Street, Belfast.
answer_text: It is planned that the street lighting scheme in Ivan Street, Belfast will be included within my Department’s programme of works for the 2024/2025 financial year, subject to the availability of funding and resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the mother and baby unit at Belfast City Hospital.
answer_text: A business case for the proposed Mother and Baby Unit at the Belfast City Hospital site is currently being prepared. Timescales for approval of this business case and delivery of this project remain subject to capital and revenue affordability in the context of an overall challenging financial position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (I) for an update on the roll-out of the perinatal mental health teams in the (i) Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: My Department allocated funding in 2021 for the development of Perinatal Mental Health Services in Northern Ireland, which included establishing  Community Perinatal Mental Health Teams in each Trust. The project was divided into two phases. Phase One prioritised a core staffing model for a Community Team, with phase two providing the enhanced service provision. Funding came from the Department at various stages throughout the year.  Phase one of the project has now been completed, recurrent funding has been provided to all Trusts at a total cost of 4,186,280.A number of workshops have been held to look at lessons learned from Phase 1 implementation and what needs to be amended/updated for phase two.Consideration now needs to be given as to which posts are required/no longer required for Phase 2.  Following that process, funding will then be sought to implement Phase 2 of the project across all Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is meeting its New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide three funded cycles of In Vitro Fertilisation (IVF) treatment.
answer_text: On 15 February, I announced that publicly funded In Vitro Fertilisation (IVF) treatment will increase to one full cycle for eligible women. Until that point, those women eligible for publicly funded IVF treatment were entitled to receive one fresh and one frozen embryo transfer, even if they achieve a live birth with the first transfer. Under the recent change, any eligible woman who does not achieve a live birth with either her fresh or first frozen embryo transfers, and still has frozen embryos, will be entitled to further treatment until either she has a live birth or she has transferred all her frozen embryos. It is expected that this will benefit approximately 150 women each year.A woman will be eligible if she meets the published access criteria for IVF and has been placed on the waiting list for publicly funded IVF/ICSI treatment on or after 1 October 2022. Whilst I would support further expansion of the service, it will require recurrent funding assurances, which have not been provided at this time. In addition, the Regional Fertility Centre will need to increase its physical footprint and ensure the appropriate number of specialist staff are available to deliver any further increase in provision. A copy of the press release can be accessed at -https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/increase-funded-ivf-provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AWQ 7265/22-27, what elements of his economic vision will require legislation.
answer_text: My officials are currently plotting the key steps in relation to my four economic pillars and, as part of that process, they will be identifying proposed legislative amendments.  It is expected that there will be amendments in the employment sphere in order to advance my good jobs objective and I have already referenced the ongoing work in the energy sphere. However, other, more nuanced, amendments may be required and, going forward, my Department will be dependent on the advice from the Office of Legislative Counsel.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will answer AQW 7268/22-27.
answer_text: I provided my response to AQW 7268/22-27 on 25th March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 6717/22-27, what action she is taking to improve the performance of the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: As advised in my response to AQW 6717/22-27, performance of the Commission is a matter for the Chief Commissioner. I understand that the Chief Commissioner has addressed performance in the Planning Appeals Commission and Water Appeals Commission (PACWAC) Annual Review for 2022/2023. The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) has sponsorship responsibility for PACWAC, and I am advised that officials meet with the Chief Commissioner on a regular basis to discuss a range of matters, including performance whilst at the same time respecting the independence of the Commission as an independent appellate body. You may wish to write directly to the Chief Commissioner to discuss the matter further.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 7134/22-27, (i) when he expects to receive the feasibility study; (ii) when he will publish the findings.
answer_text: Work on the feasibility studies, to determine the viability of extending the routes into Glengormley and Carryduff, is nearing conclusion. As part of my review of the findings, consideration will be given to the publication of these reports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AWQ 5743/22-27, (i) when the feasibility design study will be commissioned; and (ii) when it will be completed.
answer_text: I can advise that the Department intends to commission the feasibility study for possible improvements to the M2/ Mount Vernon Road junction, Shore Road, Belfast at the start of the next financial year, subject to available resources and funding.  No timescales have yet been set for completion of this study.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the introduction of residents' parking schemes; and (ii) whether his Department will give consideration to the extension of this scheme to streets in the vicinity of Crusaders Football Club.
answer_text: The review of residents’ parking schemes is complete and the report will be published when I have considered the findings, which I expect will be in the coming months. Until such time, I am not in a position to comment on specific requests for further schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 6583/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will answer AQW 6312/22-27.
answer_text: I can advise that AQW 6312/22-27 was answered on 20th March 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 7406/22-27, (i) what consideration has been given to the full pedestrianisation of Hill Street, Belfast; and (ii) when he expects the proposals to come into effect.
answer_text: My Department is currently developing a proposal to pedestrianise Hill Street, Belfast between the junction of Hill Street and Gordon Street to Waring Street, with service access only to the local businesses.  A one-way system from the north end of Hill Street and into Gordon Street will also be included as part of this scheme. There is currently no intention of pedestrianising the full length of Hill Street.I can confirm that the informal consultation on the proposed pedestrianisation is underway and will advise any future formal consultation and legislation.  There are currently no timescales for implementation of the scheme as this will depend on the outcome of the legislative process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will answer AQW 6716/22-27.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my response to AQW 6716 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department is aware of concerns relating to the use of powers of entry by the Health and Safety Executive.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy is not aware of any concerns relating to the use of powers of entry by the Health and Safety Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 7498-22-27, (i) when his officials will be in contact with the NI Hospice to discuss their funding requirements; and (ii) for his assessment of when he will be in a position to submit that bid to the Minister of Finance.
answer_text: (i)	Officials have been in contact with NI hospice to arrange a meeting to discuss funding.  The meeting is scheduled to take place on 3 April 2024.(ii)	My Department is currently facing a significant number of funding challenges across Health and Social Care and will be in a clearer position to address specific funding pressures when the budget settlement is agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he plans to review the powers of entry afforded to the Health and Safety Executive under the Health and Safety at Work (Northern Ireland) Order 1978, to ensure compliance with other relevant legislation.
answer_text: The powers of entry afforded to the Health and Safety Executive under the Health and Safety at Work (Northern Ireland) Order 1978 are necessary to allow inspectors to carry out their work. The powers of entry are considered fit for purpose and there are no plans to conduct a review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he plans to introduce powers of entry guidance for the Health and Safety Executive.
answer_text: The powers of entry afforded to the Health and Safety Executive under the Health and Safety at Work (Northern Ireland) Order 1978 are necessary to allow inspectors to carry out their work. The powers of entry are considered fit for purpose and are exercised by inspectors both legally and proportionately. There are no plans to introduce powers of entry guidance for the Health and Safety Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what work he is doing to increase the number of women and girls going into apprenticeships.
answer_text: It is important that apprenticeships provide a high-quality pathway to good jobs across the economy that appeal to everyone.  February’s Apprenticeship Week, which was organised by my Department, included a range of events to showcase the opportunities that apprenticeships offer.  As an example, North West Regional College organised an event called ‘Women In Construction’, the theme of which focused on females in the Construction and Engineering sectors.   In addition, the annual Apprentice Awards highlight the achievements of our apprentices and now include a Diversity and Inclusion award to recognise those employers who are supporting efforts to make apprenticeships accessible to more people.Finally, the introduction of All Age Apprenticeships in September 2023 provides an additional pathway to help more women return to the workforce.  		Officials are exploring a range of options as to how gender diversity in apprenticeships can be best supported and promoted, including portraying more female apprentices in the Department’s promotional material.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the economic impact of reducing corporation tax.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he is considering the creation of a skills fund to help close the skills gap.
answer_text: The OECD recognises the need to provide long term strategic investment in our skills system to enable a flourishing economy. I will be considering the best means of closing the skills gap, including associated programmes and funding, in the delivery of my economic vision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Advanced Manufacturing Innovation Centre, Belfast City Deal Project.
answer_text: The Advanced Manufacturing Innovation Centre is one of the flagship projects within the Belfast Regional City Deal. The Centre will operate at the interface between academia and industry and reinvigorate the region’s industrial potential.		Significant progress has been made to date, with all business case approval stages now completed. It was announced in September 2023 that Henry Brothers has been awarded the contract to construct the new Factory of the Future facility at Global Point in Newtownabbey and construction is on course to commence this spring.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his work to reduce the employment rate gap for those from the most deprived areas.
answer_text: I recognise that there are differences in employment and deprivation rates  across regions and have been clear in my new economic vision that the opportunities we are creating will benefit all our citizens.  Interventions to address this and other economic disparities will be included in the Sub-Regional Economic Plan I will publish in the autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his work to reduce the employment rate gap for those with a disability.
answer_text: One of my priorities is to increase the number of good jobs in our economy. This means increasing the quality and accessibility of work. My Department provides several programmes to support people living with disabilities to access employment or access training which will help them secure employment. These include:Disability Support Service which helps those with disabilities participate in the vocational training programmes Skills for Life and Work and ApprenticeshipsNI.Skills for Life and Work has an extended age eligibility for young people with a disability.a disability supplement is payable to allow training suppliers to provide additional support to apprentices participating on the ApprenticeshipsNI programme.  The Disability Access to Education and Employment programme (DARE) is a strand of the Department’s Step Up project which offers support to those with a disability to progress into work.Within Further Education Colleges the Additional Support Fund helps provide the necessary additional technical and/or personal support required to allow students with learning difficulties or disabilities to participate in mainstream Further Education courses or in discrete programmes.The Skills Equality, Diversity, and Inclusion (SEDI) sub-committee of the Northern Ireland Skills Council has also been established to consider access and participation for individuals in under-represented or disadvantaged groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to reduce waiting times and the backlog of referrals for adult ADHD assessments.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there is currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the level of prevalence of child and adult ADHD is leading to individuals waiting longer for  assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of patients with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.Department of Health officials are keeping the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services under review, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what plans his Department has to enhance facilities at King's Park Primary School, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: As a controlled school it is the responsibility of the Education Authority, in its capacity as Managing Authority, to put King’s Park Primary School forward for any School Enhancement Programme (SEP) or Major Capital calls.  I can confirm that the EA has not submitted the school for a previous Major Capital call or for the second SEP call.   A future call to the SEP is likely to represent the school’s best opportunity to receive significant capital investment. There is, however, no current live call for SEP projects and given the current constrained budget position, I cannot indicate when there might be a new call for SEP.As I set out in my Statement to the Assembly on 12 February, I believe that all of our young people deserve to be educated in schools that are fit for purpose.  That is why I have made a bid to the Executive for significant capital funding for 2024/25 and subsequent years to deliver capital works to improve schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will review the pay grading of staff in the NHS Interpreting Service.
answer_text: Face-to-face spoken language interpreters are independent self-employed contractors who are not employed by the Health and Social Care (HSC) service but are paid for their service through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) at an agreed rate.HSC Interpreters must be registered with BSO and adhere to the HSC Terms of Engagement.  This stipulates an hourly rate of pay as well as a preparation payment.  The Department is aware that pay has not increased for a number of years and the impact this is having on the valuable service that interpreters provide. You will however be aware of the significant financial constraints within the wider Health Service.  I have nevertheless asked the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to review this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the funding provision for community counselling services in North Belfast; and (ii) for a breakdown of organisations awarded funding to deliver such services, in the 2023-24 financial year.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency currently fund a wide range of agencies across the city of Belfast to deliver services under the Protect Life 2 strategy. They resource the provision of 4 key agencies to offer counselling services in North Belfast and 8 to deliver a range of specialist counselling services across the city. It is important to note that due to anonymity people often prefer to access a service that is outside of their own community. The 4 organisations offering services within North Belfast are listed below and were funded to the value of £600,524.00 in 2023/2024 Name of organisationAmount awarded in 23/24NotesLighthouse£145,791Counselling, family/individual/peer support & crisis de-escalationAction Mental Health£230,939Counselling, family/individual/peer support & crisis de-escalationExtern£191,539.00Counselling, family/individual/peer & crisis de-escalationAshton Community Trust£32,255Counselling & complimentary therapiesTotal£600,524.00

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to address water drainage and flooding issues on (i) York Road; and (ii) York Street, North Belfast.
answer_text: My Department inspected and cleaned the road gullies in York Road and York Street on 12 March 2024 and, at that time, no flooding issues were identified. The inspection did highlight that many of the road gullies are of the older foundry type, and it is proposed to replace these gullies with new modern storm pots. However, programming of these works will be subject to the availability of the necessary financial resource.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will confirm funding continuation for Integrated Services for Children and Young People (Greater Shankill Team).
answer_text: The Greater Shankill Partnership is a current recipient of my Department's Core Grant Scheme.Once the budget position for 2024/25 is known, I will be able to consider the availability of funding for the Core Grant Scheme for next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the allocation of the additional £1 million support for students in higher education facing financial hardship, broken down by institution.
answer_text: Following the receipt of forecast additional support fund requirements from each higher education institution for the academic year 2023/24, the following allocations were made. InstitutionAllocation (£k)Queen’s University861Ulster University200St Mary’s University College5Stranmillis University College18The Open University40

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Project Gigabit.
answer_text: An Outline Business Case for Project Gigabit is in development and my Department continues to work with Building Digital UK on the development of a procurement approach for the project.  My Department is planning to award a contract for Project Gigabit in 2024, subject to necessary approvals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what work his Department will carry out at the pedestrian underpass tunnel at York Street Train Station to improve (i) connectivity; and (ii) the appearance of the area.
answer_text: Work is currently progressing on the new York Street Station site and I can confirm that when it’s completed later this year, it will include the delivery of a new high-quality public realm creating an ambient and attractive public space, with the provision of 18 Belfast Bike spaces.  It will also include a high-quality landscape plan for the new station, which will include planting of trees, hedges, and shrubbery.Improved street lighting will be provided to the existing underpass as part of the new York Street Station project and new signs will also be erected at the Nelson Street entrance to improve wayfinding for pedestrians and cyclists.  Work to remove offensive graffiti and litter is also currently being undertaken and a proposal from a community group to install artwork in the underpass is also under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his planning improvement programme.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way.  I intend to take some time to review the progress that has been made to date through the planning improvement programme and will continue to work closely with key stakeholders to collectively improve processes and increase the efficiency of the system.  Progress is however dependent on the resources available to key areas of the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how much of the £2.8 million financial hardship fund for higher education students remains unspent this year, broken down by institution.
answer_text: Higher education support funds are provided on an academic year basis, therefore funds issued in the financial year 2023/24 may be distributed to students by their institution throughout the academic year 2023/24. On 31 January 2024, the following unspent support fund balances were held by the higher education institutions.InstitutionBalance (£k)Queen’s University Belfast261Ulster University763St Mary’s University College29Stranmillis University College12The Open University0

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what discussions his officials have had with the owners of Giant's Park, Belfast, to ensure pedestrian connectivity.
answer_text: As a statutory consultee to the Belfast City Council Planning Authority, my officials were involved in the Pre-Application Discussion process for a Recreational Space proposed for Giants Park in August 2021.  They responded with several recommendations for the developer, including the inclusion of development of local infrastructure to provide safe and convenient facilities for pedestrians both within and around the proposed development site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department plans to introduce no-fault divorces, similar to other parts of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: This is an area that I am considering in the context of other areas of work within my Department’s civil law reform remit. My officials have undertaken preliminary engagement with those with knowledge of the divorce system here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the pedestrian crossing at Whitehouse, Newtownabbey, will be completed.
answer_text: The toucan crossing on the Shore Road at Whitehouse, Newtownabbey is anticipated to be operational before the end of March 2024.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they are considering the extension of the Urban Villages programme in Belfast.
answer_text: Urban Villages is highly successful. The latest evaluation (up to 31 December 2023) shows that over 1m people across Belfast and Derry-Londonderry have attended Urban Villages events, with 91% reporting that the programme fostered relationships between and within communities.  A copy of the latest Programme Evaluation has been placed in the Assembly Library.Officials are preparing options on the future of the programme. We look forward to updating Members once we have considered how best to continue with our aim of promoting thriving places.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6856-22/27, whether he will use those powers to begin the process of vesting Crumlin Road Courthouse to bring it back into public ownership.
answer_text: We would only use our heritage powers to vest this listed building in order then to transfer ownership to a partner sufficiently resourced for and committed to the required conservation/refurbishment to restore the Courthouse (cost estimated in 2020 to be £20-30m).	In addition, DfC Regeneration do not have any specific programmes which could provide a basis to vest the Courthouse.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will publish his Department’s research into air connectivity and flight routes for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In October 2023, my Department commissioned research into Air Connectivity to support tourism and business growth. This research will be completed by end March 2024. The research will then be circulated for review before it is published.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he is considering the introduction of financial support schemes for households to transition to green energy as a means of heating their homes.
answer_text: My department is currently preparing a public consultation on a low carbon heat support scheme in line with Action 8 of the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2023.[1] The outcome of this consultation will help to inform options regarding the design, rules, and guidance of future support for low carbon heating technologies which will include heat pumps.[1] https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/economy/Energy-Strategy-2023-Action-Plan-Report.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what discussions she has had with HM Treasury regarding VAT abatement as part of the Budget process.
answer_text: Discussions on VAT abatement will form part of broader discussions with Treasury on the Fiscal Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of comments by the Minister of Finance in the Assembly on 27 February 2024, when he will submit a bid for additional funding for the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice.

To ask the Minister for the Economy, following oral evidence by the Chief Executive of Invest NI to the Committee for the Economy on 28 February 2024, for his assessment of the evidence provided in relation to the devolution and reduction of corporation tax.
answer_text: At the Committee for the Economy, the Chief Executive of Invest NI recognised that tax was one of several factors that companies consider when making investment decisions. However, the way in which Treasury proposed to deduct funding from the Executive's block grant, at a time of public spending cuts, meant that reducing Corporation Tax was not previously affordable.			As Kieran Donoghue also noted, market access, skills and people are also key factors that determine investment. As my economic vision is delivered I want to ensure that companies have access to the right skills and can take advantage of the full range of market access opportunities that are available under the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department will take to improve (i) connectivity; and (ii) pedestrian safety at Ulster University, Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that the Department is considering a number of proposals to improve connectivity in and around Ulster University, Belfast, including:Options to improve pedestrian and cycling connectivity between North Belfast and the Ulster University city centre area;Options for improvements to active travel and connectivity along York Street, to Yorkgate Railway Station, as part of York Street Interchange scheme;A proposal to widen the footway outside the main University building and relocation of the bus stops;Completion of the consultation of the Experimental Order for York Street;Completion of the upgrade of the York Street / Frederick Street traffic signal junction; andEastern Transport Plan – draft vision and objectives include connectivity and safety aspects. Further information can be found on the Department’s website at the following link: Eastern Transport Plan (ETP) 2035 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the work he will undertake to secure regular passenger transatlantic flights between Northern Ireland and the United States of America.
answer_text: My Department consistently looks for ways we can enhance and support our air connectivity. In October 2023, my officials commissioned aviation consultants to update research previously published by the Department in July 2019. This research is focusing on innovative ways to identify a set of strategic routes which will help drive inbound tourism and business growth and identify policy levers available to the Department to enhance our air connectivity. This research will be completed by end March 2024, and will inform the development of policy and work required to secure new routes, including the potential for transatlantic flights between here and the United States of America.Tourism Ireland (TI) continues to provide co-operative marketing support to airlines operating to all three of our local airports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Studio Ulster project at Giant's Park, North Belfast.
answer_text: The Studio Ulster project is progressing well and is due to open in September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons for the delay in introducing the pedestrianisation of Hill Street, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department is currently carrying a high number of staff vacancies.  During periods of high workload, our limited staff resource means that, at times, some work areas may have to be prioritised over others.  Unfortunately, due to workload pressures in recent months, it has been necessary to delay the development work for the informal consultation associated with Hill Street pedestrianisation.  This work has now recommenced with the informal consultation programmed to be undertaken April 2024.  Further progress on this matter will be subject to the satisfactory completion of the legislative process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what financial support his Department can provide to community-based tourism providers in North Belfast.
answer_text: Tourism NI has in the past operated capital programmes to create new or enhance existing visitor attractions and amenities. There are no capital programmes open at present, however Tourism NI is exploring options and investment opportunities for future years, budget permitting. Tourism NI can add contact details of any community-based tourism provider in North Belfast to its mailing list if the named contact provides an email to tds@tourismni.com.  When a new funding programme is being launched, they will then be notified automatically.Tourism NI’s Industry Development department supports businesses across the sector, including the community sector, to be more innovative, sustainable and inclusive.  It monitors and evaluates industry performance and provides tailored support based on identified areas for improvement and growth.  This team also leads on industry-wide issues including skills and career development and runs in-person and online seminars and webinars.  In February 2024, Tourism NI launched MyTourismNI, a dedicated innovative E-Learning platform specially developed to support the local Tourism Industry to build expertise, knowledge, competence and confidence.  It is free to access, and the tourism industry can choose from a range of learning modules, covering business-critical topics such as Sales and Marketing, Digital Sustainability, People and Operational Effectiveness.  Businesses and community based tourism providers, including those located in North Belfast, can sign-up to www.tourismni.com to receive regular and targeted events, news and updates. Tourism NI is providing input and advice to Belfast City Council on a new council-led £350k Neighbourhood Tourism Investment Programme which will run over the next 18 months and will be of particular interest to community-based tourism providers in North Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to review the use of a general NHS bed benchmark as the model for the funding of NI Hospice costs.
answer_text: Departmental Guidance introduced 50/50 funding for agreed adult inpatient hospice services with effect from 2004/05 and 50/50 funding for agreed adult community services with effect from 2005/06.  Funding arrangements relate to the agreed commissioned costs and not a hospice’s total running costs.The legacy Regional Health and Social Care Board undertook several reviews of funding to hospices, initially in 2014 and more recently in 2019/20. As part of the most recent review,  the costs of hospice services were benchmarked against equivalent services provided in the NI Health and Social Care system via Trusts.In respect of in-patient services in a hospice, the benchmark used was that of the average cost of a Trust provided general medical bed across NI. The benchmarking exercise also including consideration of day-hospice costs and those of the specialist community palliative care nursing services. Hospice cost for day-hospice were accepted, as there was deemed to be no comparable service in the HSC system, while the costs of a Trust community specialist nurse were comparable to those of a community hospice nurse. In considering this approach the former Regional Board firstly undertook a desktop exercise to understand the funding of similar services in GB and secondly took professional medical and nursing advice from the Public Health Agency in respect of the professional inputs to the various element of the services commissioned from hospices to ensure broad comparability with services in the NI HSC system as the benchmark.The review concluded that in terms of inpatient services, the bed-day cost of a General Medical bed was an appropriate benchmark comparator against which to assess funding for hospice inpatient beds.  This review resulted in all adult hospice services receiving further funding to reflect the agreed 50/50 funding position. The additional investment to hospices amounted to £867k, of which the NI Hospice received additional funding from this exercise of £360k. This was backdated to 1 April 2019.The Department has annual monitoring arrangements in place with hospice providers and where possible will respond to pressures highlighted through this monitoring process. The Department continues to work with all hospices to understand and resolve any funding concerns identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when his Department last carried out commissioning for adult palliative care; and (ii) to detail that process.
answer_text: Commissioning is the process of securing the provision of health and social care and other related interventions that is organised around a “commissioning cycle” from assessment of need, strategic planning, priority setting and resource acquisition, to addressing need by agreeing with providers the delivery of appropriate services, monitoring delivery to ensure that it meets established safety and quality standards, and evaluating the impact and feeding back into a new baseline position in terms of how needs have changed. The commissioning of services within my Department is led by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group [SPPG]. Commissioning of services is not a one-off event but is an ongoing process of investment and review.  Commissioning health and social care services, including those associated with adult palliative care services, is a continuous process, taken forward on a rolling annual commissioning cycle. Palliative care services are differentiated as either generalist or specialist services. Health and Social Care Trusts and Primary Care providers are the main providers of generalist services.  Services are characterised by the provision of the district nursing, General Practice and other multidisciplinary team services.  Specialist services are provided by both HSC Trusts and hospices whose staff have undertaken specialist training in palliative care to support patients, and their families, managing more complex symptoms.(ii) As referenced to above the process followed to secure services is the commissioning cycle and involves planning based on needs assessment, engagement with service users and potential providers, negotiation and the development of business cases and contracts with service providers. Once investments are made there are ongoing monitoring and performance management arrangements in place and an evaluation of investments to ensure value for money and health gain for the population. Specifically in the area of adult palliative care, as part of the contractual arrangements in place for the commissioning of adult specialist palliative care hospice services, my Department has Service Level Agreements with independent hospice providers across NI for the provision of contracted services.  Under these arrangements, my officials meet at least once a year with each of the four independent hospices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the spend by Tourism NI in North Belfast during this current financial year.
answer_text: Tourism NI (TNI) has undertaken spend in the North Belfast area in the current 2023-24 financial year, summarised as follows: - International Tourism Events Fund 2023-24 Of the 11 applications received under the International Tourism Events Fund 2023-24, Tourism NI received two applications relating to events that would take place within the Belfast North constituency.  Both applications progressed to Letter of Offer and received the following grant awards:Cathedral Quarter Arts Festival					£40,000Belfast City Marathon (4 x Belfast constituencies)		£50,000Experience Development Programme 2021-23 capital funding scheme A total of £8,945.29 has been spent in the North Belfast constituency, in the current 2023-24 financial year, towards a capital development tourism project at Crumlin Road Gaol through the TNI Experience Development Programme 2021-23.  This remaining expenditure was part of an overall £450,000 capital grant administered by TNI to Crumlin Road Gaol, most of which was issued in the previous financial year. Embrace a Giant Spirit Portfolio Development Programme 2021-23 capital funding scheme. Four businesses from the constituency participate in the Embrace a Giant Spirit Portfolio programme and have access to a range of dedicated supports and training interventions to maximise their tourism potential, driving growth and productivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail future investment proposals for Belfast Royal Academy.
answer_text: The Department is progressing a project under the School Enhancement Programme (SEP) at Belfast Royal Academy.  This project will see investment of circa £4m in enhanced accommodation at the school.  A design team is currently being appointed to plan and develop the scheme. Budget constraints and pressures regarding Special Educational Needs placements and urgent health and safety works mean future investment will have to be prioritised in line with available budget.  I will continue to make the case for increased investment in the education estate, to allow much-needed projects to be delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why his Department is not currently honouring a previous commitment to fund 50 per cent of the NI Hospice's direct costs for adult care.
answer_text: Hospices play an important role in supporting individuals, and families, at the end of life by providing care for those with complex physical, psychological and social needs. Care is provided in hospices or in the persons own home, or care home, by specialist community teams. Annually around 3% of all deaths in Northern Ireland are recorded as having occurred in hospices.Departmental Guidance introduced 50/50 funding for agreed adult inpatient hospice services with effect from 2004/05 and 50/50 funding for agreed adult community services with effect from 2005/06.  A subsequent review of the level of funding, under this approach, to hospices was undertaken in 2014 and more recently again in 2019/20. The 2019/20 review, which benchmarks hospices service cost against broadly similar services provided by HSC Trusts, resulted in all adult hospice services receiving additional funding to reflect the agreed 50/50 funding position, this additional investment to hospice services amounted to £867k, of which the NI Hospice received additional funding from this exercise of £360k. The Department has a formal monitoring process in place with hospices and meets all hospices at least once annually. This process allows the Department to assess contract performance and provides hospices with the opportunity to identify any funding pressures they may be experiencing.  Annual funding for adult hospices across NI increased from £7.5 million in 2019/20 to £9.1 million in 2022/23. Furthermore, during the Covid 19 pandemic my Department made available additional non-recurrent funding to all hospices in the order of £16m of which the NI Hospice received £8.2m in total across both Adults (NI Hospice) and Children’s (NI Children’s Hospice) services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of his Department's progress in meeting the New Decade, New Approach commitment to hospices.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's policy for funding children's hospice care.
answer_text: The Department of Health recognises the valuable role of hospices in delivering specialist care to people with palliative and end of life care needs across Northern Ireland. The Department has provided core funding of £1.6m in 2023/24 directly to the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice for a range of services for children with life limiting conditions and their families, including respite care and end of life care.The Department of Health is aware of the wider funding pressures linked to the increasing costs of care provision. Consequently, I wrote on Friday 09/02/2002 to the Finance Minister to seek further discussions between the charities and our two Departments.On the 27th February the Minister of Finance acknowledged that she would be committed to working with myself and that if Hospice services are not considered affordable within the Departments baseline, that a bid could be submitted for additional funding. My officials are currently working on proposals around funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what work his Department is considering to increase the number of taxi drivers.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry, in relation to taxi driver, taxi operator and vehicle licencing.  The recruitment of new and the retention of existing drivers is the responsibility of the taxi industry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he would support legislative change to provide a legal right to palliative care.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of palliative and end of life care for people with life-limiting conditions and for their families and carers.  	I have no plans to introduce legislation that would provide for a legal right to palliative care.  Palliative care should be, and is, provided in response to patients’ needs.  People who are identified as having palliative and end of life care needs therefore will receive care that is based on an assessment of their individual clinical, psychological, emotional, spiritual and social needs. The majority of palliative care services are delivered by generalist health care professionals, including the patient’s GP, District Nurse and other healthcare professionals in hospitals and care homes. When individuals have more complex or unresolved needs, or symptoms which cannot be resolved by generalist teams, they would be referred to specialist palliative care, which is provided through specialist health and social care services, such as hospital-based teams and specialist palliative care community teams, including through hospices.  In the case of palliative care for children and young people, this is provided through HSC Trusts and the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice, again in line with the holistic needs of the child or young person.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's strategy for palliative care for (i) adults; and (ii) children.
answer_text: I am very aware of the importance of palliative and end of life care in supporting people with palliative care needs to live well and achieve the best quality of life as their illness progresses.The Palliative Care in Partnership Programme, co-led by my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency, continues to oversee implementation of the Department’s ‘Living Matters: Dying Matters’ palliative and end of life care strategy for adults and the development and improvement of palliative and end of life care for adults across Northern Ireland.  The Partnership brings together a wide range of stakeholders across the statutory, independent and voluntary and community sectors, including hospices, as well as service users, to support the development and implementation of services for people who are living with palliative and end of life care needs and those important to them. The Partnership’s remit includes:	ensuring the delivery of key priorities in palliative and end of life care both regionally and locally;	ensuring what is designed and developed is person-centred; and	advising on and sharing best practice already in place to support people with palliative care needs.The Partnership has recently agreed its workplan priorities for 2023-26.  The workplan focuses on 4 priority areas:Early identification of and timely intervention for people with palliative care needs; Co-ordination & Quality Improvement in palliative care services; Palliative care education and training; and A public health approach to palliative care. A range of workstreams have been identified and are in place to support these priority areas. These priorities and associated workstreams will inform and support the Palliative Care in Partnership Programme and its member organisations in taking forward a range of actions to further improve palliative and end of life care for adults. I am aware that there have been calls for the development of a new palliative care strategy for adults. An Assembly debate in April 2021 saw all party support for the inclusion of death, dying and bereavement in the new Programme for Government. It would be appropriate therefore to wait for the Programme for Government process to be progressed as this will provide the strategic driver for the future development of palliative and end of life care.  In terms of Paediatric Palliative Care, this has been and is an important priority for my Department.  This dates back prior to the publication of the Strategy for Children’s Palliative and End-of-Life Care, which was published in 2016.  This strategy sets out 23 objectives across the spectrum of paediatric palliative care from diagnosis through to bereavement care.  Thankfully, as a result of innovations in treatment, children born or who develop palliative care conditions survive much longer, many into adulthood.  Whilst this is welcomed, it highlights the importance of the objectives within the Strategy and how crucial i

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6433-22-27, (i) for his assessment of the urgency of the situation; and (ii) whether he has considered setting officials a deadline for completing this work.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department is aware that this work is urgent and is treating it as such.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new build for (i) Seaview Primary School, Belfast; and (ii) Abbey Community College, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: Seaview Primary School was announced under the Major Capital Works Programme in March 2016.  An Integrated Consultant Team (ICT) was appointed for the project in November 2023.  The ICT are currently undertaking a technical feasibility study to explore options and costs to provide accommodation for the school, and it is anticipated that a report will be submitted to the Department for consideration in May 2024. Abbey Community College was announced under the Major Capital Works Programme in June 2014. The project is currently at technical design and pre-tender stage (RIBA Stage 4), and it is anticipated that this stage report will be lodged with the Department for consideration by mid-March.  Planning approval for the project was granted in June 2023.  Without additional capital funding the site purchase required for the Abbey Community College project cannot be progressed and neither project can progress to the construction stage.As I have referenced on a number of occasions, I will be bidding for additional capital funding to deliver these much needed projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 6579/22-27, whether she will recommit to meet the New Decade, New Approach commitment of increasing the number of PSNI officers to 7,500.
answer_text: The recruitment of police officers is an operational matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, and I am committed to respecting his operational independence and the role of the Policing Board.  However, I will continue working with the Chief Constable, to ensure we have a police service that is properly resourced in all aspects for the challenges it faces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to confirm the continuation of (i) Industrial Derating; and (ii) small business rates relief scheme for 2024-25.
answer_text: (i) 	I can confirm that Industrial Derating will continue for the coming rating year and will see the support continue for around 4,400 industrial properties.  This assistance is currently worth over £71million to these businesses.  (ii) The Small Business Rate Relief Scheme has also been extended for the forthcoming 2024-25 financial year.   This will provide in the region of £20 million of assistance automatically to 29,000 small businesses who will benefit from a reduction of between 20% to 50% on their rates liability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the pedestrianisation of Hill Street, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department proposes to pedestrianise Hill Street, Belfast between the junction of Hill Street and Gordon Street to Waring Street, with service access only to the local businesses.  A one-way system from the north end of Hill Street and into Gordon Street will also be included as part of this scheme.I can confirm that the informal consultation on the proposed pedestrianisation is planned for April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the process he undertook to appoint independent experts for his Economic Vision.
answer_text: I have asked a small number of experts to be critical friends and provide external challenge to me and my Department. I believe having external challenge will be important in ensuring that we deliver on the priorities I have set out. These are not public appointments and they will not be paid for this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 6308/22-27, (i) whether he has received the business case from the Housing Executive to approve the demolition of Abbotscoole House; (ii) whether he is aware of the strong public objections to the demolition of Abbotscoole House; and (iii) whether he will commit to retaining Abbotcoole House, given the level of housing need in North Belfast.
answer_text: My Department has not received a business case from the Housing Executive relating to Abbotscoole House.  I have not received any correspondence indicating public opinion on this issue during my time in office.  If my Department receives a business case in relation to Abbotscoole House, it will be given due consideration and scrutiny before a decision is made.  I am not in a position to comment further at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail his legislative plans in advance of the Assembly summer recess period.
answer_text: I have assumed that the question relates to my plans for primary legislation.  The legislative programme has not yet been agreed by the Executive and discussions in that regard are ongoing. I can, however, confirm that the legislative proposals that I will be prioritising within my Department are those that will advance my economic vision, which I have already outlined to the Assembly, including legislative proposals in a number of areas related to energy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will bring his Economic Vision to the Executive for approval.
answer_text: My department is developing policy and interventions that will support the delivery of my Economic Vision.  Many areas will require working across departments, and Executive approval will be sought where required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the business organisations he (i) consulted with; and (ii) received endorsement from, in advance of publishing his Economic Vision.
answer_text: I consulted numerous business organisations when developing my Economic Vision prior to my appointment as Minister. I did not seek any public endorsements for my Economic Vision but the feedback has been extremely positive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for update on the York Street Interchange project.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.In October 2022 I published a Placemaking and Active Travel Review (PATR) report on the scheme and asked my officials to carry out further work on three scenarios recommended within it. This work is expected to conclude in Spring 2024. Details of the current status of the York Street Interchange project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/york-street-interchange-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much his Department has spent on the York Street Interchange project.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safetyA total of £23.7m has been spent on the York Street Interchange project to 31 March 2023.A further £1.0M has been allocated for the 2023/24 financial year.Details of the current status of the York Street Interchange project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/york-street-interchange-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any planned public realm works at the new York Street train station.
answer_text: My Department does not have any planned public realm works at the new train station, York Street, Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Glengormley town centre public realm project.
answer_text: The proposed Glengormley Town Centre Environmental Improvement Scheme is a high priority project for Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council and my Department. An Outline Business Case from Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council has been received by my Department and is under consideration.  However, there is a key interdependency for the proposed Scheme with the route of the Belfast Rapid Transport 2 (BRT2) project. The Department for Infrastructure is currently considering the feasibility of the BRT2 route going through Glengormley and until a decision is taken my Department cannot commit to funding the Glengormley Town Centre Environmental Improvement Scheme.My officials will continue to work closely with Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council and monitor the outcome of the decision-making process for the route of BRT2.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the work to extend Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2 to Glengormley.
answer_text: In my previous tenure as Infrastructure Minister I announced the North to South Belfast Glider route from Carryduff in the South, along the Antrim Road to Glengormley.  Officials are currently  finalising feasibility studies to determine the viability of extending the routes into Glengormley and Carryduff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans his Department has to reduce the waiting lists for adult Autism Spectrum Disorder assessments.
answer_text: The Department is aware of the extensive waiting lists for adult autism assessment and the challenges that this presents to those people who need support. A Review of Adult Autism Services across the region has been undertaken and the recommendations were presented to the Permanent Secretary.  One of the recommendations is the appointment of a Lead for Adult Autism Services - recruitment of this post is currently underway. This role will be responsible for taking forward the recommendations emerging from the review; this will include the management of waiting lists for adult autism assessment waiting lists. Each Health & Social Care Trust (HSCT) is also implementing a number of actions to improve service access and reduce waiting lists:Belfast Trust - continues to recruit and train staff within available resources, however demand for Autism diagnosis and subsequent interventions continues to rise. There is a need for recurrent investment to meet the recurrent demand and also non-recurrent investment to manage pressures that have built up over many years, exacerbated by COVID-19. The Trust also provides an Advice service (BAAS Belfast Autism Advice Service) for individuals aged 16+years and their families/carers which is supported by a multi-agency approach and is delivered in partnership with Cedar Foundation, DEL Careers Advice Service, DES Disability Employment Service, NISSA, NAS, AI, Carers NI and the Belfast Metropolitan College.Northern Trust– The Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) Service is currently funded for 6.9 whole time equivalent staff to provide diagnostic assessments and interventions to adults in the Northern Trust area.  However, the demand for the service significantly outstrips capacity. Additional funding is required to provide to enhance staffing levels in order to address waiting times for adult autism assessments.Southern Trust – have appointed a new ASD Mental Health Coordinator who is expected to take up post by the end of March 2024. This additional support will enable the ASD Adult Diagnostic service to increase its assessment clinics from once per month to weekly (circa 4 assessments per month from current baseline of 1 per month) commencing from April 2024.  Additional investment and appropriately trained staff will be required to significantly reduce waiting times to Integrated  Elective Access Protocol (IEAP) targets.  South Eastern Trust– have updated their short-term intervention service model to a predominately group-based intervention which then enables capacity to be redirected to completing diagnostic assessments.  They are currently in the process of recruitment and have progressed unfilled posts in a timely manner.  Slippage money from an unfilled post is being used to commission a provider to undertake diagnostic assessments as a way to reduce the diagnostic waiting list. The Trust are also in the process of funding an external provider to deliver a group work programme and as a result the additional capa

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 5744/22/27, whether he can confirm that, from 1 April 2024, his Department currently has no funding in place to deliver the core grant scheme for community and voluntary organisations.
answer_text: I refer to my previous response to AQW 5744/22-27 and confirm there are no further updates on the budget position for 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the future proposals for Mountain Hill Youth Centre, Belfast.
