answer_text: Mountainhill Youth Centre is scheduled for redevelopment and planning work has already began on the scheme. However due to pressures on the education budget, there was no youth capital avaiable in 2023-2024, which has impacted all youth capital timescales. Therefore, there is no definitive date for commencement of redevelopment work.In the past three months, maintenance issues have been raised with regards to the current building which are being addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether the Housing Executive still plans to develop land at Old Irish Highway for social housing; and (ii) whether he will instruct the Housing Executive to make the site available to a housing association given that planning permission has been secured.
answer_text: (i) The Housing Executive remains committed to enabling the development of its land at Old Irish Highway for social housing.(ii) The Housing Executive nominated Apex Housing Association to develop the site for social housing and this nomination was accepted in November 2023. I understand that Apex are currently working to appoint a design team to take the scheme forward. The Housing Executive will continue to work with Apex to ensure an appropriate scheme development timetable for the development of the site is put in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what powers his Department has to bring Crumlin Road courthouse back into public ownership.
answer_text: Under Section 202(1) of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 - Compulsory acquisition of listed buildings - if ‘…reasonable steps are not being taken for properly preserving (a listed building), the Department may, if it considers it expedient in order to preserve the building, compulsorily acquire the building.’ Under Section 202(4): ‘The Department must not commence proceedings for the compulsory acquisition of a building under this section unless at least two months previously it has served on the owner of the building, and not withdrawn, a notice (in this section referred to as a “repairs notice”)—(a)specifying the works which the Department considers reasonably necessary for the proper preservation of the building; and(b)explaining the effect of this section.’In addition, the Department can also vest buildings under its regeneration powers in Article 87(1) - Part VII (Development Schemes and Acquisition of Land) - of the Planning (Northern Ireland) Order 1991.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will advance a parking improvement scheme for Hillview Avenue, Cloughfern.
answer_text: My Department has considered proposals to improve parking at Hillview Avenue.  However the land required for a parking improvement scheme at this location is not currently owned by the Department.  Further progression of the proposal would require land acquisition and associated funding.Details of the planned capital improvement schemes schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the Cloughfern area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council which can be found at; Annual Report to Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council 2023 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)The programme for 2024/25 will be developed once future budgets are agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the effectiveness of the Planning Appeals Commission; (ii) whether she will commission a review into the effectiveness of the Planning Appeals Commission; and (iii) to what actions she will take to improve the effectiveness of the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: The Chief Commissioner has addressed performance matters in the PACWAC Annual Review for 2022/2023. The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) has sponsorship responsibility for PACWAC, and I am advised that officials meet with the Chief Commissioner on a regular basis to discuss a range of matters, including performance whilst at the same time respecting the independence of the Commissions. PACWAC is an independent appellant body. However, I understand that the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) has commissioned a Planning Improvement Programme and that the Chief Commissioner is considering PACWAC specific actions.(iii) 	NICTS Officials continue to meet with the Chief Commissioner to address resourcing pressures with work on-going both in terms of recruitment exercises and the future resourcing model, which will be subject to budget constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his legislative plans to improve the planning process.
answer_text: The report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 made numerous recommendations/actions covering key elements of the planning system including legislative changes.  Good progress has already been made, including the implementation of a planning fee inflationary uplift; the progression of work to bring forward arrangements for statutory validation checklists which will improve the quality of applications entering the system; the launch (December 2023) of a public consultation into a fundamental review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015; and a review of the internal administration of the ‘call-in’ process. Enhanced use of digital technology in the planning system has also been aided with introduction of the Regional Planning IT System (Planning Portal).I will be reviewing progress with my officials; progress is however dependent on the resources available to me within my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Chamber of Commerce and Industry’s Planning Improvement and Reform Position Paper; (ii) whether he will be implementing any of the recommendations of the paper.
answer_text: I am aware of the NI Chamber of Commerce and Industry’s recent position paper on Planning Improvement and Reform. I intend to take some time to review the paper in conjunction with the progress that has been made to date by my own Department to improve the planning system.  I remain committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way and will continue to work closely with key stakeholders, including the NI Chamber, to collectively improve processes and increase the efficiency of the system.  Progress will however be dependent on the resources available to key areas of the system

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, as per her first day brief, whether her Department will be advancing Levelling-Up Funding.
answer_text: We are awaiting formal confirmation from Treasury on what funding streams have been included in the Financial Package. Any decisions in relation to the funds which have been included in the Financial Package will be a matter for the Executive. Separately, other Levelling Up funding streams not included in the Financial Package will continue to be delivered by Whitehall’s Department of Levelling Up, Housing and Communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he intends to support the Community Pharmacy sector.
answer_text: I can assure the member that I am committed to working with the Community Pharmacy sector via their representative body, Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI) to deliver fair, sustainable and affordable funding for community pharmacy. Following a meeting I held with representatives of CPNI on 21st February 2024, I have committed to an inflationary uplift of £6.1m for pharmacy services in 2023/24. Furthermore, an additional £4m was provided in recognition of service pressures for the 2023/24 year. Following the commitments in relation to £10.1m, arrangements were made with The Business Services Organisation to invest 10/12th (£8.4m) within the February payment. Arrangements have been put in place to make the remainder available with the March and April payments. Further work will be taken forward to review community pharmacy financial arrangements in the coming months with CPNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department will prevent a delay in Special Educational Needs pupils receiving suitable school places when transferring to a post-primary school.
answer_text: Children on the Special Educational Needs (SEN) register but without a statement of SEN (63% of all children with SEN) transitioning from primary to post-primary follow the general school admissions process. Children with a statement of SEN (37% of all children with SEN) transitioning to post-primary follow a different process, which seeks to ensure that their new school placement is appropriate to meet their needs as determined by the annual review of their statement of SEN carried out in primary 7.Meeting the significant increase in demand for specialist education provision in recent years has been and remains challenging as our special schools are operating at full, and in many cases over, capacity and there is a rising need for more specialist provision in our mainstream schools.The Education Authority (EA), in collaboration with my Department, is working at pace to create additional specialist education provision across the education system for the 2024/25 academic year, working towards ensuring that the majority of children with SEN moving to post-primary education receive notification of an appropriate school place at the same time as their peers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 1884/22-27, (i) when his Department forwarded the Vesting Order to the Departmental Solicitors Office; and (ii) when he expects to receive their advice.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department received a Vesting Application for Fortwilliam Parade from the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) on the 24 November 2023. This was forwarded to the Departmental Solicitors Office (DSO) for their advice on the 27 November 2023.DSO are awaiting additional information from the NIHE to allow the legal process to proceed, so at this stage I cannot say when the final advice from DSO will be received. However officials from my Department are liaising closely with the NIHE and DSO to ensure that this Vesting application can be progressed as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her commitments to increasing the number of PSNI officers.
answer_text: Police officer numbers are an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am of course committed to supporting the Chief Constable to ensure we have a police service that is properly resourced in all aspects for the challenges it faces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what support her Department can provide to the Northern Ireland Children's Hospice.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how his Department can support the growth of the tourism industry in North Belfast.
To ask the Minister of Education to outline his plans to expand Special Educational Needs provision in North Belfast.
answer_text: The EA is currently taking forward work to implement two Development Proposals approved by my Department on 13 October 2023. DP 702 changed the designation of Harberton Special School from age 3-11 to age 3-19 operating across a dual campus, extending provision at the Fortwilliam campus in North Belfast.DP 703 approved a new 3-19 Special School in the Belfast City Council area that will provide 275 special school places on a phased basis.  The EA will seek major capital investment for the new build 3-19 special school in the Belfast City Council area, on a location to be determined.The EA has advised that it is currently in the process of refurbishing space within Harberton Special School (North Campus) for September 2024 and is also engaging with Cedar Lodge Special School to see how additional capacity can be provided.  As part of Phase 2 of the Plan, it will look to increase the number of special school places in the Belfast area, with consultation on proposals expected before June 2024.Belfast has been identified as an area of significant demand for specialist education provision for September 2024, and the EA is proactively working to create additional capacity in both special schools and specialist education provisions in mainstream schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what investment plans his Department has for Glengormley Integrated College.
answer_text: There are no current capital works planned at Glengormley Integrated College. However, following correspondence from the school to the Education Authority (EA) on the 8 February 2024, highlighting concerns in relation to capacity issues due to the continuous growth of the school enrolment numbers, the EA has now generated a minor works initiation form to progress a project to deliver feasible solutions to meet the capacity needs of the school.Integrated College Glengormley has a gymnasium and multi-purpose hall, both of which exceed the DE building handbook recommendations therefore no action has been taken in relation to the provision of a sports hall at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action can be taken to address the lack of sports hall provision at Glengormley Integrated College.
answer_text: There are no current capital works planned at Glengormley Integrated College. However, following correspondence from the school to the Education Authority (EA) on the 8 February 2024, highlighting concerns in relation to capacity issues due to the continuous growth of the school enrolment numbers, the EA has now generated a minor works initiation form to progress a project to deliver feasible solutions to meet the capacity needs of the school.Integrated College Glengormley has a gymnasium and multi-purpose hall, both of which exceed the DE building handbook recommendations therefore no action has been taken in relation to the provision of a sports hall at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will give formal approval to implement Rule 84 of the Housing Selection Scheme to grant a resident leaseholder the tenancy of the sold flat, where there are high value works.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department is in discussions with NIHE colleagues on options to enable the granting of a tenancy to a resident leaseholder of a sold flat, where there are high value works. Further work is required before a final decision can be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 5473/22-27, (i) whether his decision required Executive approval; and (ii) whether he will now bring this matter to the Executive for decision.
answer_text: (i) My decision did not require Executive approval; and (ii) for that reason I will not bring this matter to the Executive for decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether his Department is aware of projected capacity issues at Glengormley Integrated College; and (ii) what action will be taken to address these issues.
answer_text: There are no current capital works planned at Glengormley Integrated College. However, following correspondence from the school to the Education Authority (EA) on the 8 February 2024, highlighting concerns in relation to capacity issues due to the continuous growth of the school enrolment numbers, the EA has now generated a minor works initiation form to progress a project to deliver feasible solutions to meet the capacity needs of the school.Integrated College Glengormley has a gymnasium and multi-purpose hall, both of which exceed the DE building handbook recommendations therefore no action has been taken in relation to the provision of a sports hall at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he plans to bring forward a dilapidation bill; and (ii) whether dilapidated wasteland, including that without any known ownership, is being considered within the scope of the bill.
answer_text: I am considering introducing a Dilapidation bill to the Assembly and will be receiving briefing from my officials in due course. Introduction would of course be subject to the agreement of my Executive colleagues. The proposed Bill would provide councils with a consistent, modern and fit-for-purpose regime, including clear powers to tackle dilapidated and/or dangerous buildings and neglected sites. The intended powers would enable councils to act in the absence of an identified owner by expanding the range of persons on whom a notice could be served to include any person with an interest in any neglected wasteland (e.g. owner; occupier; a person with a charge over the land or another financial interest in it; or a person who has a responsibility for the management or maintenance of the land).  In such a scenario, where an ‘owner’ could not be established, a council would still be able to take action by serving a notice on an ‘interested person’.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what action they are taking to improve the application process to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: As of week commencing 8 July, over £48.5 million has been paid to victims since the Scheme opened for applications on 31 August 2021.This is a novel scheme and each application is unique with its own complexities.  Many applications may also include multiple related  incidents (as defined in the Victims Payments Regulations 2020)The Executive Office has committed to funding a range of support services to enhance the application process to ensure a victim centred approach.To date, funding of almost £5 million has been provided to support victims’ and survivors’ representative groups via the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS).  This has facilitated the recruitment of welfare officers and admin support staff to assist applicants with the application process and additional funding to provide health and wellbeing support. These groups have been working closely with the Victims’ Payments Board and VSS on improving the process for applicants, and feedback from those discussions will be considered at the Sectoral Engagement Group, which is chaired by TEO.		TEO also continues to provide additional resources to evidence retrieval partners to assist in the retrieval of evidence on behalf of applicants and the Victims’ Payments Board.  TEO officials also engage regularly with colleagues in the Department of Justice and the Victims’ Payments Board to consider scheme process and communications issues.  In addition, the Victims’ Payments Board are currently engaged with the Department of Finance’s Business Consultancy Service to conduct a high-level review of the scheme administration processes to help enhance the delivery of the scheme.The Victims’ Payments Board conducted a leaflet drop to each household here to raise awareness of the scheme during February 2024.  The leaflet was also distributed to other relevant stakeholders, including all MLAs.  The Board will also engage with wider stakeholders across England, Scotland, Wales and the south of Ireland in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has received a request from the Minister of Health for additional funding for the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice.
answer_text: ANSWERI appreciate the important role of hospices in delivering specialist care to people with palliative and end of life care needs across the North. I have received a request from the Minister of Health asking for assistance in supporting hospices in any way possible.I have written to the Health Minister encouraging the Minister to bid for additional funding as part of the Budget 2024-25 exercise to support these services if they are not considered affordable within the Department of Health baseline. This, along with any other bids, can then be considered by the Executive as part of the budget setting process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) how many applications have been made to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme; (ii) how many of these applications have been approved; and (iii) how many applications have been deemed ineligible under the no fault of their own requirement.
answer_text: As of week commencing 29 April, almost 7,400 applications have been submitted to the Scheme with over £40.6 million paid out to victims.  Over 1,300 determinations have been made by the Victims’ Payments Board, with 848 determined as eligible for payment. This figure includes determinations at first hearing and appeals. 462 applications have been determined as ineligible for payment.Applications may be ineligible for a payment under the Scheme due to a number of reasons as determined by a panel of the Victims’ Payments Board.  There are no specific categories for those reasons and so details of the number of applications ineligible under the no fault of their own requirement is not available.

answer_text: The future of Abbotscoole House, Rathcoole is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. 		You should, therefore, direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 6960 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what work his Department is undertaking to deliver City Deal projects.
answer_text: The City and Growth Deals Programme will see over £1.3bn invested in our economic infrastructure and will play an important role in delivering regional balance. Positive progress has been made, but I want to see delivery of projects accelerated. I have instructed officials to play a greater role in supporting the projects, through co-design and partnership working, to develop proposals that can be approved and delivered as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for his assessment of the success of Project Stratum; and (ii) how his Department will build upon its success.
answer_text: (i) Project Stratum was developed to improve connectivity for premises unable to access broadband services of 30 Megabits per second or greater. Of the more than 81,000 eligible premises which are scheduled for intervention under Project Stratum, over 71,000 have been addressed. The contractor for Project Stratum, Fibrus Networks Ltd., has also addressed a further 14,000 premises which are close to the network being constructed under the project. Over 26,000 premises have ordered a broadband service made available through Project Stratum. (ii) My Department continues to explore what additional measures can be implemented under the British Government’s Project Gigabit plan.  Project Gigabit is the UK Government’s flagship £5 billion programme that is expected to benefit premises that are unable to access gigabit-capable broadband services and are not part of suppliers’ commercial deployment plans. My Department has carried out an Open Market Review and a Public Review for Project Gigabit and at present 59,000 premises have been categorised as eligible for intervention under the project. A further 60,000 premises are categorised as Under Review, where reported commercial coverage claims have not been verified, or there are risks to delivery plans; these premises could be brought into the scope of Project Gigabit at a later date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to address health inequalities in North Belfast.
answer_text: Making Life Better (MLB) is the Executive’s overarching strategic framework to improve health and address health inequalities. It was developed based on the evidence that health and wellbeing, and health inequalities, are shaped by many factors including age, family, community, workplace, beliefs and traditions, economics, and physical and social environments. Many of these are outside the control of the Department of Health.Reducing inequalities in health is a significant challenge. Through MLB, and across a range of key issues, my Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) continue to collaborate across departments and agencies, local government, the community and voluntary sector, and with communities themselves to address the factors which impact on health and wellbeing locally and regionally. The COVID-19 pandemic has no doubt exacerbated existing inequalities and the PHA continues to deliver a range of actions to address the impact of this, and other health conditions and behaviours, with the most deprived communities to help reduce these health inequalities. Partnership working and collaboration between organisations is essential in this work.The PHA invests approximately £12 million into Health Improvement Activity within the Belfast Trust area and targets resources specifically to address local need. For example, in relation to the prevention of Drug related mortality and alcohol related admissions the PHA commissions a range of services including:•	Belfast Drugs &Alcohol Connections Service•	Belfast Youth Engagement Services•	Belfast Drugs and Alcohol Targeted Prevention Services for 	Young People.In the North Belfast Area, locally based services commissioned by the PHA include Ardoyne and Shankill Healthy Living Centre (HLC), Women in Sport & Physical Activity (WISPA), New lodge and Duncairn HLC.These organisations provide a range of services to the local community, including services which promote physical activity and nutrition, improve mental health, and reduce isolation – all key factors in reducing health inequalities - and they work to promote their services to those most in need in the local area, including people experiencing poor mental health, and isolated older people.The PHA also supports the wider strategic health and wellbeing activity in North Belfast through the Health and Wellbeing Forum, Carezone project and the Complex Lives Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what work his Department will undertake to support the implementation of the Union Connectivity Review.
answer_text: My department and Translink have been successful in receiving funding through the British Government’s Union Connectivity Development Fund to take forward three studies focused on railway improvements.  These are the electrification of the railway between Belfast and the Border; the reopening of the Antrim to Knockmore railway line with a link to Belfast International Airport; and the Portadown to Armagh railway extension.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact the cost of childcare has on economic activity.
answer_text: The Early Years and Childcare Strategy which is being developed by the Department of Education is an Executive priority.  I recognise that there is a need for high quality affordable childcare to enable parents to work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how much the Public Health Agency will spend in North Belfast this financial year to address health inequalities; and (ii) for a breakdown of this spend.
answer_text: The Department, through the Public Health Agency (PHA), works in partnership with a range of statutory, voluntary and community organisations and other NICS Departments to address health inequalities under Making Life Better, the Executive’s overarching strategic framework for public health.Factors which contribute to health inequalities include; educational attainment, housing, cost of living, fuel poverty, food poverty, access to employment opportunities, social connections, transport and the environment & surroundings people live in.Partnership working and collaboration between organisations is therefore essential to improve the wellbeing of people in Northern Ireland and to reduce health inequalities.The PHA invests around £42 million in Health Improvement Services across the whole of the region. These services are delivered by a range of providers including Trusts, Councils and the Community and Voluntary Sector.These services focus on improving health and wellbeing of the population and tackling health inequalities and include services to support physical activity, mental wellbeing, suicide prevention, obesity prevention, older people and social inclusion, community based Healthy Living Centres, addictions services, support to stop smoking, early years interventions and support for parents and carers, among others.Within the Belfast Trust Area, approx. £12 million was invested in 2023/24. Many of these services are Belfast wide, for example Belfast Healthy Cities is a city-wide Programme however they support a specific programme of work at the Care Zone, North Belfast. Other examples include Belfast Age Friendly Coordinator, Active School Travel Programme, Warm and Well Programme, The Green Gym Project and therefore, not quantifiable at a local District Electoral Area (DEA) level.The following table however does outline the investment to those projects specifically based within North Belfast.Organisation InvestmentCLARE Project £86,000Ashton Bridge of Hope: Suicide & Self Harm Therapeutic Support£32,000Lighthouse Family Support Services£146,000Lighthouse: Flourish Inter-Churches Initiative on Mental & Emotional Wellbeing£27,500Action Mental Health £198,000Ligoniel Healthy Living Centre £125,000Ardoyne and Shankill Healthy Living Centre£151,000Cancer Lifeline£100,000WISPA£49,500New Lodge/Duncairn Healthy Living Centre£130,000Extern family support services£192,500These organisations work to promote their services to those most in need in the local area and provide a range of services to the local community, including services which promote physical activity and nutrition, improve mental health, and reduce isolation – all key factors in reducing health inequalities.Services funded outside of North Belfast may be utilised by residents in North Belfast, and likewise the services funded in North Belfast are available to residents outside of this DEA. Therefore, the above list does not represent all funds that have been provided to tac

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider measures to improve traffic flow at the adjacent mini roundabouts on Oldpark Road, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the increase of traffic flows on the Oldpark Road and surrounding roads at peak traffic times, particularly at school starting and finishing times which is consistent with many areas within the greater Belfast area. An investigation of the road traffic collision information available for the last three years, supplied by the PSNI, would not indicate a particular issue.The Department has no plans to change the current road layout at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what safety works his Department plans to carry out at the Mount Vernon and M2 junction, Shore Road, Belfast.
answer_text: The Department carries out an investigation of road traffic collisions on the adopted road network on an annual basis for the bringing forward of possible improvement schemes that have a common pattern of collisions that can be treated by engineering methods.I can advise that the most recent review has identified a common pattern of collisions at this location involving vehicles turning right towards the M2. As such, Eastern Division intend to commission a feasibility design study to consider options into possible road improvements to fully control this right turn movement within the traffic signal layout.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his Department's review of the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme.
answer_text: The review of the Executive’s People and Place strategy is progressing well with co-design structures in place to facilitate engagement with a wide range of stakeholders including the Neighbourhood Renewal partnerships, representatives from Section 75 groups and the wider voluntary and community sector including those from rural communities.The development of the draft strategy, which will be informed by the views expressed through the engagement with the key stakeholders and other focus groups, will be progressed over the coming months in advance of a full public consultation exercise.The extensive engagement to date has highlighted the complex nature of the challenges facing those who live in the most deprived communities, challenges that have been exacerbated by the cost of living crisis.  Given the complex nature of deprivation providing the right support for those communities and the individuals who are living in poverty will require a collaborative and integrated cross Departmental approach by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the Building Successful Communities initiative.
answer_text: The Building Successful Communities (BSC) initiative was introduced as part of the Facing the Future - Housing Strategy for Northern Ireland in 2013 to pilot the use of housing intervention as one of the main catalysts for local regeneration.  As a result my Department has committed an investment of around £11.9 million across six designated areas of deprivation.As the BSC Programme is now approaching an end a review is currently underway and a Post Programme Review report will be presented to me later this year for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider the reinstatement of his Department’s core grant scheme for community and voluntary organisations which ended in October 2023.
answer_text: I regret that I am unable to reverse the decision to reduce core grant for 2023/24 due to the Department’s continuing financial challenges. Once the budget position for 2024/25 is known, I will be in a position to consider the availability of funding for the Core Grant Scheme for next year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline his Department’s capital programme for North Belfast.
answer_text: As budgets are currently set on a one-year basis my Department’s capital budget is not in place beyond 31 March 2024.  While it is not possible to dis-aggregate capital budgets by constituency, details are provided below where capital is committed to projects in the North Belfast constituency. Officials in my Department and Invest NI are supporting local authorities in delivering City and Growth Deals.  City and Growth Deals are split by Council area and the Belfast Region City Deal encompasses North Belfast and includes anticipated contributions from my Department of £25 million for Studio Ulster, £34 million for the Centre for Digital Healthcare Technology and £65 million for Belfast Stories.Invest NI is also working closely with a number of businesses in the North Belfast constituency to progress capital investment.  These are commercial in confidence until such time as they are announced.  Invest NI hope to bring these projects to fruition promptly in the new financial year.Over a five-year period to 2024/25, £199 million has been allocated to Project Stratum to provide access to gigabit-capable broadband infrastructure to 81,100 premises.  It is not possible to attribute spending at a constituency level but, 112 premises in North Belfast will benefit from Project Stratum.The North Belfast constituency encompasses Northern Regional College’s campus at Newtownabbey.  The Department provides capital funding through its ongoing Further Education Minor Works Scheme for upgrades to the building fabric, sustainable estate solutions, emerging equipment requirements and to ensure Health and Safety standards are met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will allocate additional funding to enable the Northern Ireland Children's Hospice to maintain present bed capacity.
answer_text: I recognise and applaud the vital work of the NICH and all hospices in Northern Ireland.As requested by me, DoH officials have engaged in detailed discussions last week with NI Hospice.It is clear to me that the financial pressures facing NICH go much deeper than any reduction of Departmental support.  This reduction involved the ending last summer of an additional but temporary annual payment of £170k. This decision was notified to the NICH in June 2023 and took effect from September 2023 which resulted in £85K reduction in this financial year.  I took the decision on Friday 09/02/2022 to reinstate that funding in full for this year.This payment is in addition to the core annual funding of £1.6m which my Department is providing directly to NICH this year. This core funding included a £420,000 component which had not been recurrent, meaning it would have to be subject to confirmation each year. I can confirm that, going forward, this will be made recurrent, giving the Hospice greater certainty.I am keenly aware that the financial challenges facing NICH go much deeper than this or next year. Like all health care providers, it has encountered significantly rising running costs, reflecting inflationary pressures.Regrettably, the NICH has advised my Department that the restoration of the full £170k for this year will not lead to the reversal of the service reduction made public this week.The NICH and NI Hospice parent body will need to develop longer-term solutions, to which I am sure Government will want to be supportive.Given the current absence of a budget for next year, and the well-documented financial uncertainties and challenges for Government, my Department is at the limit of what it can do right now.I am also very conscious that other hospice organisations, and many other health and care providers, are also grappling with growing budget pressures and will be seeking further statutory finance.I am grateful for the support and personal interest of the First Minister and deputy First Minister regarding NICH’s position. I am also aware of the fact that other NI Departments have provided financial support to NICH in the past.I believe the long-term need for financial stability across the hospice sector would benefit from cross-Departmental input, helping to assist organisations in identifying sustainable ways forward.Consequently, I wrote on Friday 09/02/2002 to the Finance Minister to seek further discussions between the charities and our two Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has an expected date for commencement or completion from contractors for the resurfacing of Fortwilliam Crescent, Belfast.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads within every council area, including Belfast, that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the Belfast area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Belfast which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-2023-annual-report-to-belfast-city-council.pdf.  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.The programme for 2024/25 will be developed once future budgets are agreed.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department is aware of the traffic flow issues at the McDonald’s restaurant, Shore Road, Belfast; and (ii) what steps can be taken to improve the traffic flow.
answer_text: I can advise that planning permission was granted for the construction of a McDonald’s drive-through restaurant including access, parking, and associated site works in March 2017, with the development becoming operational in February 2018.Parking proposals, including queuing at the drive-through, were assessed under Planning Policy Statement 3: Access, Movement and Parking; Parking Standards which state the minimum length of drive-through queue provision.  The proposal demonstrated capacity for 15 queuing vehicles within the site in accordance with Planning Policy PPS3.  Access to the site via private road has the capacity for a further 6 queuing vehicles, which when combined was acceptable under the Belfast City Council’s planning policy.The Department is aware that at times some congestion can occur from vehicles queuing to access the drive through.  The access road from Shore Road to McDonald’s is not adopted and does not form part of the adopted road network.Should queuing become an issue causing obstruction, this would fall under the responsibility of the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the planned vesting and redevelopment of Fortwilliam Parade, Belfast.
answer_text: I wish to advise that my Department received a draft Vesting Order from the Northern Ireland Housing Executive on 24 November 2023. This has been forwarded to the Departmental Solicitors Office (DSO) for their advice.The vesting process can take some time depending on the advice received from DSO and any potential amendments that need to be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the last road inspection took place on Glenvarna Drive, Glengormley; (ii) how many road or footpath defects were identified and actioned from this inspection; and (iii) the estimated date of the next routine inspection at this location.
answer_text: I can confirm that the most recent inspection of Glenvarna Drive by my officials took place on 6 September 2022.  One defect, which met my Department’s intervention criteria, was identified and instructed for repair.  The next inspection is scheduled to take place on 6 December 2022.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the last road inspection took place on Ballyhenry Avenue, Glengormley; (ii) how many road or footpath defects were identified and actioned from this inspection; and (iii) the estimated date of the next routine inspection at this location.
answer_text: I can confirm that the last inspection of Ballyhenry Avenue by my officials took place on 6 September 2022.  There were two defects identified which met my Department’s intervention criteria, which were instructed for repair.  The next inspection is scheduled to be carried out on 6 December 2022.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department have an expected commencement or completion-by date from contractors for the resurfacing of Glebecoole Avenue, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: Glebecoole Avenue, Newtownabbey is included in my Department’s three year resurfacing programme for the Antrim and Newtownabbey area, delivery of which is subject to the availability of funding and contractor resource.  In the event the scheme cannot be delivered this financial year, it will be programmed for delivery early next financial year.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department have an expected commencement or completion-by date from contractors for the resurfacing of Fortwilliam Crescent, Belfast.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the process of finalising its resurfacing programmes following confirmation of its 2022/23 contingency funding allocation.  Details will be included in my Department’s Reports to the respective Councils which will be presented in due course, published on the Department’s website and also placed in the Assembly library. Completion dates for individual schemes have still to be determined and will be decided after discussions with the appointed Contractor have taken place and a works programme has been agreed.  Unfortunately given our current level of funding, Fortwilliam Crescent is unlikely to be included in this year’s resurfacing programme, and whilst it is on our three year priority resurfacing programme I am currently unable to indicate an expected commencement or completion date due to the higher priority of other competing schemes.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much was spent by his Department on the Placemaking and Active Travel Review report for the York Street Interchange project.
answer_text: In July 2020 an Independent Assurance Review of the York Street Exchange scheme was commissioned, which concluded in March 2021,my predecessor announced the outcome of the review and the recommendations which she had accepted.One of the recommendations was to review the scheme to ensure it aligns with the concept of “placemaking” and new best practice in terms of urban transport design. From April 2021 an independent Placemaking and Active Travel Review (PATR) of the scheme was undertaken to consider how to maximise the social, economic and environmental benefits and its contribution to the future development of Belfast.  The PATR report which was published on 11 October 2022 provides proposals to strengthen the place-making and active travel provision of the project. It advises that additional measures could be implemented in tandem with the scheme, to maximise the benefits for communities.  The report recommends that three of the options considered are further developed and refined to allow an informed decision to be taken on the way forward. The costs spent on the PATR review between April 2021 and September 2022 are: 2020/21 -    Placemaking = £572.72             Active Travel = £0  2021/22 –   Placemaking = £131,548.49      Active Travel = £54,766.00   2022/23 –   Placemaking = £47,252.36        Active Travel = £0                Total –        Placemaking = £179,373.57      Active Travel = £54,766.00      Combined total – Placemaking and Active Travel = £234,139.57

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on his Department’s work to introduce On-Street Residential Charge Point Schemes for electric vehicles in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As you are aware, my Department has set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network. Derry City and Strabane District Council, which leads the On-street Residential Charge Point Scheme (ORCS) council consortium, is represented on this Task-Force. The Action Plan will be submitted to me shortly.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). The British Government has made £20 million in grant funding available to local authorities/councils in GB-NI during 2021-22 to provide charge points for residents without off-street parking (ORCS)). The council consortium submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust, which manages the fund on behalf of OZEV, on 30 March 2022. It received a Grant Offer Letter from OZEV in July of funding to install 124 charge points across 9 council areas. My Department is represented, in an advisory capacity, on the ORCS council consortium. The Department has also made £500,000 match-funding available, subject to public expenditure processes, from the Department’s Blue Green Fund to assist local councils making an application to the ORCS Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the current charge point network for electric vehicles.
answer_text: The current electric vehicle public charge point network is owned by the Electricity Supply Board (ESB) and is operated on a commercial basis. ESB announced in November 2021 that it had been awarded £3.27 million from the Levelling Up Fund to expand and enhance the EV charging network. ESB can be contacted directly regarding its plans for its charge point infrastructure. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure. I am pleased that my Department has been able to support the FASTER Project, funded through the INTERREG VA Territorial Co-operation Programme which is managed by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB). The project has received funding to install a total of 73 EV Rapid charging points across the island of Ireland and the West of Scotland by 31 May 2023. It is expected that 20-30 of these will be located in the North.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). A local council consortium, led by Derry City and Strabane District Council, submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust which manages the fund on behalf of OZEV on 30 March 2022. Derry City and Strabane District council is best placed to advise about the application. My Department is represented, in an advisory capacity, on the ORCS council consortium. My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network.

answer_text: The delivery of digital devices to schools has now commenced and the following settings will receive digital devices during Phase one of the roll-out of the scheme provided for under “A Fair Start”.School Ref NumberSchool NameNumber of Devices3010862Abbots Cross Primary School, Newtownabbey301016076Ballysillan Primary School251210022Belfast Boys' Model School1291210021Belfast Model School for Girls1331230321Blessed Trinity College1561046596Bunscoil Bheann Mhadagain201010282Carr's Glen Primary School, Belfast301310003Cedar Lodge School271056483Cliftonville Integrated Primary School, Belfast301010063Currie Primary School, Belfast201010323Edenbrooke Primary School, Belfast301016485Glenwood Primary School, Belfast401260269Hazelwood College, Newtownabbey1291066531Hazelwood Integrated Primary School, Newtownabbey461030194Holy Cross Boys' Primary School, Belfast471030331Holy Cross Girls' Primary School, Belfast201036566Holy Family Primary School, Belfast501016537Lowwood Primary School, Belfast201230104Mercy College, Belfast921036041Mercy Primary School, Belfast281030326Our Lady's Primary School, Belfast313010895Rathcoole Primary School121036471Sacred Heart Primary School, Belfast191010266Seaview Primary School, Belfast481036048St Mary's Star of the Sea Primary School, Belfast161036698St Patrick's Primary School, Belfast561036042St Vincent de Paul Primary School301016532Wheatfield Primary School, Belfast15Total1,329

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action will the Northern Ireland Housing Executive undertake as a result of the roof collapse in the Green End flats, Rathcoole.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that they anticipate that scaffolding will be erected this week to allow further investigation of the collapse by a structural engineer. The engineer’s report will advise on the nature of the structural concerns. Once the Housing Executive is in receipt of this report they will move forward as quickly as possible to remediate and will make arrangements to survey other similar properties if required. Residents have been kept informed of developments by the Housing Executives local office and Patch manager and further updates will be given once information is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the improvement works to the Green End Flats, Rathcoole.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that they plan to begin the external improvement scheme for the Green End Flats in the 2023/24 financial year. This is a major scheme which will include the replacement of roofs and windows. The Housing Executive is currently finalising tender documents before the procurement process begins. The start date of the scheme will be subject to successful procurement of a contractor, consultation with residents including leaseholders, and receiving all required the approvals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 3954/22-27, to provide details of the scheme commenced by his Department on 3 October, aimed at providing access to non-registered patients who are experiencing an emergency or urgent dental condition.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) how personal medical records are transferred onto the NHS England System should an individual move from Northern Ireland and register with a GP in England; and (ii) how long this process takes.
answer_text: Currently when a Northern Ireland registered patient transfers to an English Health Authority the Business Services Organisation receives the following: -1. a deduction notification instruction to remove patient from the GP list and;2. a request to forward the appropriate medical records. This notification is issued by the new Health Authority in England.BSO registration unit staff remove the patient from the GP list on the central register in Northern Ireland and request the patient’s medical record (hard copy) to be returned to BSO offices for onward transfer to the new Health Authority.When medical records are not returned, BSO medical record unit staff send reminders to the Northern Ireland GP every four weeks until patients’ medical records are received.Once received, receipt of the medical record is recorded on the BSO registration system NHAIS along with confirmation the record has been forwarded to the new Health Authority.BSO utilise a weekly courier service to transport records to health authorities in England and all medical records are sent to Capita Primary Care Support England, in Darlington, for onward distribution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work to redevelop the derelict properties at Fortwilliam Parade, Belfast.
answer_text: The Housing Executive obtained approval from its Board to vest and demolish the properties at 53-55 Fortwilliam Parade and to vest adjacent land at 57 Fortwilliam Parade.   They are currently working to progress the options to redevelop the properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support is available to those people on Pension Credit and are unable to secure an NHS place, with the cost of dental treatment.
answer_text: Access to routine dental care continues to be significantly affected by the COVID-19 pandemic, which caused reduced activity and therefore reduced capacity in dental practices. In order to increase activity in General Dental Services, I introduced the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), which rewards dentists based on treatments being provided.  The RSS has seen an increase in activity levels of around 16.5% over the first 5 months of 2022/23 and support will continue until the end of the financial year.Any General Dental Practitioner signed up to the RSS is required to treat patients based on clinical need, regardless of their registration status. This is to ensure that those patients most in need are able to obtain a dental appointment.In addition, any patient who has an urgent or emergency dental need, including those on pension credit or those entitled to free dental treatment, is able to make use of an Emergency Dental Clinic during weekends or Public Holidays. There the patient will be triaged remotely, and appropriate advice given or treatment arranged.To further help those in most need, on 3 October 2022, my Department commenced a scheme throughout Northern Ireland to provide access to non-registered patients who are experiencing an emergency or urgent dental condition.Support being provided to dental practices will continue to remain under ongoing review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the York Street Interchange.
answer_text: Following the independent assurance review of the York Street Interchange project, work has been continuing on the Placemaking and Active Travel Review (PATR). The PATR has identified options to improve placemaking and active travel provision as part of the project.  Further work is required to develop these options and consider factors relating to their potential implementation. This work is likely to take until early 2023 to complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the work to improve road safety at the Ulster University Campus, Belfast.
answer_text: You may be aware that officials have been working together with key stakeholders as part of the Junctions Working Group to determine the long-term arrangement for the York Street/ Frederick Street/Great Patrick Street junction and the intention would be to deliver this as part of the Department of Communities, Belfast Streets Ahead Phase 3 Public Realm project.In the interim my Department has commenced a scheme to further enhance the existing pedestrian crossings at this junction.  Officials estimate this work will be ongoing until mid-February 2023 and will include:signal controlled crossing of left slip lanes (currently uncontrolled);wider crossing points to facilitate signal controlled crossings and enhance capacity;enhancement of pedestrian footways and larger traffic islands to increase space for waiting pedestrians; andrestaging and re-signalising of the existing junction to maximise opportunities for pedestrians to cross.Officials have been careful to ensure that this work will complement the longer term plans for the junction and will also be monitoring the operation of the junction to ensure it meets the needs of all road users, including pedestrians.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at:  dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to list the resurfacing works that will be completed this financial year in the North Belfast constituency.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the process of finalising its resurfacing programmes following recent confirmation of its 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. Details will be included in my Department’s Reports to the respective Councils which will be presented in due course, published on the Department’s website and also placed in the Assembly library.Commencement dates for individual schemes have still to be determined but will be after discussions with the appointed contractor have taken place and a works programme has been agreed.   Should you have any further questions on this issue, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to address the lack of NHS places in dental practices in North Belfast.
answer_text: The pressures affecting dental practice capacity, caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, are being felt across Northern Ireland and the UK. The Department has responded to these pressures by investing more: £76m in support payments through 2020/21 and 2021/22.In the first half of 2022/23 the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS) has invested an additional £4.1 in dental practices by paying dentists an additional 25% for dental treatments.  The RSS has been extended until the end of 2022/23, enhancing payments to dentists by 10%, expected to cost an additional £2m.To further help those in most need, on 3 October 2022, my Department commenced a scheme throughout Northern Ireland to provide access to non-registered patients who are experiencing an emergency or urgent dental condition.However investment alone will not deliver the improvements we need and that is why the Department’s Dental Contract Reform Group (CRG) was established.  The CRG is looking at how to make improvement in the dental contract in short and longer term to ensure that Health Service dentistry is sustainable and delivers for both patients and the profession.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work has been undertaken by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to develop the derelict land at the entrance to the Mount Vernon estate, North Belfast, for social housing.
answer_text: A scheme on Housing Executive owned land at Mount Vernon Walk has been included in the draft Social Housing Development Programme for 2023/24.  In relation to the wider lands, a feasibility study is anticipated to commence this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what support her Department can offer to the BUILD project, based in North Belfast.
answer_text: My Department through the Belfast Regeneration Directorate (BRD) has been engaged with the BUILD project group since December 2021.  The BRD Director and senior officials have met with BUILD project members to offer support and advice on the regeneration of vacant lands within the Greater Shankill Area.BRD is currently in the process of assessing all vacant lands in the area to identify potential regeneration sites which may be deemed suitable for advertising via a Development Brief process on the open market.   My Department will continue to provide assistance to the BUILD project group as they move forward with any future proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the introduction of a one-way system at the Glenwell Road, Glengormley.
answer_text: The proposed one-way system is an initiative of Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council and is currently being considered for an area of council owned land in front of the Glengormley Sports Bowl/Movie House Cinema, Glengormley.  DfI Roads is a consultee in the process, and is providing part funding for an upgrade of traffic signals at the junction of Antrim Road, Glenwell Road and Church Road.  I would recommend you contact Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council directly for an update on the overall progress of the proposal, Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive on the redesignation of the Shore Crescent flats in North Belfast, to people over the age of 55 only.
answer_text: Following the request to consider the proposed redesignation of the Shore Crescent Flats to people over the age of 55 only, Housing Executive staff met to consider the viability of this proposal. The Housing Executive has advised that the current housing demand in the area (both in terms of applicants in the over 55 age group and other applicants on the Social Housing Waiting List); the age profile of the current residents in the flats would not support the redesignation of these flats to accommodation for over 55s at this point in time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the development of land at the junction of Sunningdale Park and Ballysillan Road by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised me that the tender exercise for its Modern Methods of Construction (MMC)/Low Energy pilot scheme at this site has now been completed.  However, as the price exceeds the cost in the business case that was approved by my Department, the Housing Executive are required to seek revised approval and have submitted an addendum (to the original business case) to DfC. My officials are currently reviewing this addendum. When this process is complete, the Housing Executive may be better able to advise you of the timescales regarding this pilot scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 2399/22-27, for an update on the plans to replace finlock gutters in the Silverstream area of North Belfast.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to consider the advice that they received and scope options regarding this complicated issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department will publish the preferred routes for Phase 2 of the Belfast Rapid Transport Scheme.
answer_text: Work on the Public Consultation Report for BRT2 is now at final draft stage. Further development of the Outline Business Case, which will help inform selection of the preferred route, is also well advanced. At this stage it remains too early to provide a timeline for the preferred route announcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for details of the proposed cycle path at the Cavehill and Limestone Road area of North Belfast.
answer_text: I am very keen to support sustainable transport options that connect communities, tackle the impact of climate change and improve lives.Following initial objections to the construction of Phase 1 on the Limestone Road, I have asked my officials to review this scheme and initiate a new consultation process in the autumn, which will cover Phase 1 – Limestone Road (York Street to Antrim Road) and Phase 2 - Cavehill Road (Antrim Road to Westland Road). The consultation will seek views on the option of a permanent cycle lane.Once officials have suitably developed the permanent cycle lane option, contact will be made with key stakeholders and elected representatives.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the redevelopment of Yorkgate Train Station.
answer_text: Details on the redevelopment works at Yorkgate Train station is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update of his Department’s weed spraying programme in North Belfast.
answer_text: The Department’s weed control work was delayed this year due to a contractual issue. In August 2022 DfI Roads’ external contractor began the weed control programme and it is expected that all adopted roads will be treated before the end of the growing season (31 October 2022).Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on Blue Green Infrastructure projects in North Belfast.
answer_text: I am very keen to support sustainable transport options that connect communities, tackle the impact of climate change and improve lives. There are a number of ongoing projects and schemes that have been completed and are currently planned for North Belfast.With regards to Hill Street in Belfast City Centre, my officials are currently finalising the public consultation exercise that has just been completed, which will be released in the coming weeks.My officials are also working closely with Belfast City Council to move forward on the ‘greening of alleyways’ initiative. Following on from ongoing engagement with Ulster University I have brought forward an interim scheme to enhance the existing pedestrian crossings at the York Street/ Frederick Street/Great Patrick Street junction.  These works will:Physically enhance pedestrian footways and enlarge traffic islands to widen crossing points and increase space for waiting pedestriansOptimise the signalisation of the existing junction to maximise opportunities for pedestrians to cross. Works should be completed in approximately 12-14 weeksOfficials will be monitoring the operation of the junction to ensure it meets the needs of all road users and pedestrians.Feasibility studies are being prepared for the delivery of routes within the Belfast Cycling Delivery Plan, including those within the North Belfast constituency area.Following initial objections to the construction of Phase  on the Limestone Road, I have asked officials to review this  and initiate a new consultation process in the autumn which will cover Phase 1 – Limestone Road (York Street to Antrim Road) and Phase 2 Cavehill Road (Antrim Road to Westland Road), the consultation will include the option of a permanent cycle lane.Once officials have developed the permanent cycle lane option, contact will be made with key stakeholders and elected representatives.My Department has been working with partners as part of the ‘Relink the Westlink’ project and is looking at options for a number of bridges on the M2- Westlink corridor. A feasibility study has been carried out and initial designs are being finalised. Officials plan to carry out installation works, on the pilot scheme including Clifton Street Bridge, this autumn.My officials are progressing a number of small scale schemes including the provision of accessible dropped kerb and tree planting. Should you have any further questions on this matter, or indeed if you have any proposals which you would like me to consider including within my Blue Green programme of works, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at:  dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the future investment in (i) the estate; and (ii) development of services, at the Mater Hospital.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is currently developing a longer term strategic plan to better meet the needs of our local and regional population which may mean changes in the range and location of services across the Trust’s various sites, including the Mater Hospital. The Trust is also in early discussions with commissioning colleagues and others around a number of potential service developments on the Mater site, in particular the development of an elective overnight stay centre surgery, for intermediate complexity procedures. An allocation of general capital has been made to the Trust which includes funding for estate maintenance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the capital development of Blessed Trinity College, in North Belfast.
answer_text: Following a competition to procure an Integrated Consultancy Team, the contract was awarded to Hood Magowan Kirk in February 2022.Officials from my Department and the EA are currently engaging with the ICT on a number of issues, following which it is expected the ICT will undertake a Technical Feasibility Study to inform the business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider improving accessibility and safety on the Fortwilliam Roundabout for cyclists travelling between Shore Road and Dargan Road.
answer_text: The release of the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan (BCNDP) 2022 – 2031 in March 2022 follows on from ‘Making Belfast an Active City – Belfast Cycling Network 2021’ which was published in June 2021.I can advise that cycling infrastructure around Fortwilliam Roundabout is identified within the long term plan of the BCNDP.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development of a new school premises for Mercy College, Belfast.
answer_text: The Education Authority is in the process of procuring an Integrated Consultancy Team (ICT) for the Mercy College major capital works project. It is anticipated that the ICT contract will be awarded in March 2023. Following appointment, the ICT will undertake a technical feasibility study, which will inform the business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Department’s work to invest in Belfast Royal Academy.
answer_text: The Department is progressing a School Enhancement Programme (SEP) project at Belfast Royal Academy. This project will see investment of circa £4m in enhanced accommodation at the school. The scoping report for the SEP has been completed by the Department’s technical advisers and a meeting has been arranged to discuss and agree this with the school. Once the scoping is agreed, the Department will initiate the procurement of a design team. A minor works scheme for roof repairs to the Bruce Building is nearing completion. A further scheme to replace the sports pavilion steps is at construction stage.With competing demands for capital investment and a limited capital budget, capital investment in minor works schemes are being prioritised to ensure the budget is targeted to the areas of greatest need. Only contractually committed schemes and the highest priority 1 schemes are currently in a position to proceed i.e. minor works schemes that meet inescapable statutory requirements such as health and safety, fire protection, obligations under the Disability Discrimination Act and SEN or Development Proposal related works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on a new build for Seaview Primary School.
answer_text: The Education Authority is in the process of procuring an Integrated Consultancy Team (ICT) for the Seaview Primary School major capital works project.The ICT will be appointed in March 2023 when they will undertake a technical feasibility study which will inform the business case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the public consultation regarding the pedestrianisation of Hill Street, Belfast.
answer_text: The discussions with local stakeholders and representatives have been completed and my officials are currently finalising the public consultation regarding the pedestrianisation of Hill Street.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown of the funding awarded by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to external organisations to address homelessness, in the last three years.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness and have provided this information.The table below outlines funding awarded to external organisations to address homelessness by the Housing Executive over the past three years: External Funding2019/20 (£k)2020/21 (£k)2021/22 (£k)Total (£)3,3544,5466,355It should be noted that the funding outlined in the table above supports the Housing Executive in meeting their statutory duties via a number of programmes.  These include the Homelessness Prevention Fund, Funding of Outside Agencies and, more recently, initiatives delivered as part of their COVID-19 response. Please also see this table below which reflects Grant funding paid by the Housing Executive through the Supporting People programme to external organisations for the provision of Housing Related Support Homelessness Services:Grant Funding2019/20 (£k)2020/21 (£k)2021/22 (£k)Total (£)26,54826,89327,212

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work her Department undertakes to prevent homelessness.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has statutory responsibility for responding to homelessness. The Housing Executive has recently published the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27, which, outlines its overall approach to addressing homelessness and is supported by a Year 1 Action Plan detailing 44 actions to help those who are homeless or threatened with homelessness.  Any households who present as homeless to the Housing Executive are provided with support and assistance through Housing Solutions and Support Teams who will seek to provide person centred services which are tailored to meet the needs of each household and support them to achieve sustainable housing solutions.  My Department, through the Housing Executive provided funding for the Homeless Prevention Fund which in 2021/22 funded a total of 47 projects resulting in a total of 7,788 interventions. The Department leads on an Inter-Departmental Homelessness Action Plan which looks to complement the Housing Executive Homelessness Strategy 2022-27.  The Action Plan focuses on addressing gaps in non-accommodation services that have the most impact, or have the potential to more positively impact, on the lives and life chances of people who are homeless and those who are most at risk of homelessness.         In addition the Department provides core funding to Housing Rights, a charity that works to improve lives by tackling homelessness and housing problems.  A Contract for Funding has issued for £2.798m (subject to available budget) for the next 3 years (2022-25) to provide assistance in 9,350 cases and prevent homelessness in 450 cases in each year of the contract.The prevention of homelessness remains a priority for the Department and the Housing Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps the Northern Ireland Housing Executive is taking to support tenants during the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The cost of living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.The Housing Executive will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on her Department’s review into the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my previous answer to a similar question, AQW 989/22-27 in which I stated the review of the existing ‘People and Place - a strategy for Neighbourhood Renewal’ is currently underway through an inclusive co-design approach.  Updates on the Review can be accessed on my Departments website https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/people-and-place-review

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Welfare Mitigation Review.
answer_text: The Welfare Mitigations independent advisory panel have presented their final report, including recommendations for new mitigation measures, to the Department. The report is under consideration and will be published at the earliest opportunity. The implementation of any new payment schemes will require Executive agreement on the allocation of necessary funding
