answer_text: The Housing Executive has provided the table below detailing the breakdown of the number of applicants for North Belfast on the housing waiting list as at the end of June in each of the last three years. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the discussions his Department has undertaken with HM Treasury regarding the cost of living.
answer_text: My officials engage on a regular basis with their Whitehall counterparts across a range of policy and operational issues. In recent weeks I met with then Chancellor of the Exchequer, Nadhim Zahawi MP, on several occassions and I highlighted the inflationary pressures being felt by households and businesses in Northern Ireland and the need for UK Government action. Following these engagements, I welcome the UK Government’s continued commitment to deliver the much needed £400 Energy Support Payment Scheme in Northern Ireland. In addition the announcement by the Prime Minister of an Energy Price Guarantee and her commitment that the same level of support will be made available in Northern Ireland. This relief will give households and businesses a degree of certainty with their bills over the coming months.		My Department will continue to engage with the UK Government and others to ensure these support measures reach Northern Ireland households and businesses as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the additional funding her Department will require this financial year because of the rising costs of energy.
answer_text: The additional funding required for energy costs because of the rising cost of energy is currently estimated at £27.2m in 2022-23. However, this will need to be kept under review given the evolving situation around energy costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the additional funding her Department will require this financial year because of the rising costs of energy.
answer_text: My Departments increased energy costs are currently under review for this financial year. This additional cost is expected to be significant across the Departmental and ALB estates.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has considered the introduction of a pilot project to address the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all programmed resurfacing works that will take place in North Belfast for the remainder of this financial year.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the process of finalising its resurfacing programmes following recent confirmation of its 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. Details will be included in my Department’s Reports to the respective Councils which will be presented in due course, published on the Department’s website and also placed in the Assembly library.Commencement dates for individual schemes have still to be determined but will be in due course after discussions with the appointed contractor have taken place and a works programme agreed.   Should you have any further questions on this issue, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 2395/22-27, whether the legal action taken regarding the Adam Street bonfire required Executive approval.
answer_text: As you will be aware, the decision to take legal action in respect of the Adam Street bonfire has been the subject of litigation, both in the High Court and Court of Appeal.		Summary of judgment - In re JR 169 (Bonfire)	https://www.judiciaryni.uk/sites/judiciary/files/decisions/Summary%20of%20judgment%20-%20In%20re%20JR%20169%20%28Bonfire%29%20-%20210921%20%28002%29.pdf  		Summary of judgment - Court dismisses appeal to refusal of application for leave to apply for judicial review  	https://www.judiciaryni.uk/sites/judiciary/files/decisions/Summary%20of%20judgment%20-%20Court%20dismisses%20appeal%20to%20refusal%20of%20application%20for%20leave%20to%20apply%20for%20judicial%20review.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the need to strengthen sentencing guidelines in Northern Ireland in cases of (i) violence against women; and (ii) attacks on frontline workers.
answer_text: Sentencing guidelines are a matter for the independent judiciary and as such I am not in a position to comment on their content.  The magistrates’ courts’ guideline on assault occasioning actual bodily harm (which can be seen at https://www.judiciaryni.uk/sites/judiciary-ni.gov.uk/files/media-files/Assault%20Occassioning%20Actual%20Bodily%20Harm.pdf)recognises the seriousness of both of these issues, by stipulating that where a court finds the offence was committed in the context of domestic violence, or where the victim was engaged in providing a service to the public, it shall use a starting point higher than that prescribed, and may impose a sentence outside the prescribed sentencing range. The guideline goes on to identify a range of aggravating factors which would be expected to lead to an increased sentence, a number of which may be particularly relevant in domestic abuse cases.In addition, the judiciary-ni website includes a sentencing guidelines paper specifically on domestic violence and abuse.  I am also aware that the leading guideline case on tariff setting for life sentences identifies murders of particularly vulnerable victims or those providing a public service as murders attracting the higher starting point for tariff calculation.My Department’s review of sentencing policy examined current arrangements for sentencing guidance, and in particular considered the importance of transparency, consistency and public confidence.  I have agreed that the Department’s proposed Sentencing Bill will include provision ensuring that the relevant judiciary will be required to have regard to sentencing guidelines or relevant guideline cases unless they are satisfied that to do so would be contrary to the interests of justice; and that, should the court depart from relevant guidance, it will be required to give its reasons for doing so.I have also agreed that the seriousness of assaults on a range of frontline workers should be recognised in the magistrates’ courts with a higher maximum penalty than is currently available and that, in more serious cases, the fact that the victim is a frontline worker should become a statutory aggravating factor.  This will ensure that the courts will specifically address the fact that the victim was an emergency worker in their sentencing decisions.  In addition, I have taken a number of measures during my time as Minister of Justice to recognise the seriousness of domestic abuse, which predominantly but not exclusively affects women, including the new domestic abuse offence that came into operation on 21 February.  Where the domestic abuse offence involves a child, or there is any other offence in a domestically abusive context, statutory aggravators may apply which would allow sentencing to be increased above what it may otherwise have been.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 2396/22-27, whether the legal action taken regarding the Adam Street bonfire required Executive approval.
answer_text: Decisions made in respect of the bonfire at Adam Street were largely taken by my predecessor and as such I am not in a position to comment on those decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action can be taken to remove Japanese Knotweed from unregistered land.
answer_text: Controls on Japanese Knotweed are limited, with PSNI investigating suspected offences of introduction into the wild under the Wildlife (NI) Order 1985, and NIEA regulating the removal and disposal of the plant under the Waste Licencing Regulations (NI) 2003.At present, it is not actually an offence to have Japanese knotweed growing on private property. There is no statutory mechanism in Northern Ireland to compel an owner or an occupier of premises or land to control Japanese knotweed, or to permit others to carry out control measures without an owner or occupier’s consent. It therefore follows that there is no statutory mechanism for control of Japanese Knotweed on unregistered land.The Department considers that where Japanese knotweed is spreading from one property to another, it constitutes a civil matter between the two respective landowners, and must be resolved as any other issue of joint property maintenance would be. The Department’s policy context towards all invasive species is primarily to protect the ecology of an area as opposed to safeguarding property or other commercial assets. Detailed information on the relevant legislation and how this species can be managed is available on our website: www.invasivespeciesni.co.uk
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) total spend by her Department on the JobStart programme; and (ii) number of jobs created, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: At 31 May 2022 my Department had spent £10.366m on the JobStart Scheme to date. This is not the final spend figure for the Scheme as there are still current participants on the Scheme.  The Scheme closed to final participants on 17 June 2022.  Those currently on the Scheme will remain for their full 6 or 9-month JobStart job opportunity and JobStart Scheme spend will therefore continue up to March 2023. The number of job opportunities offered by employers for the Scheme was 6,772 and the number approved by the JobStart team following a rigorous assessment of employer applications was 4,218.  My Department collects statistical information by Council area and not by Parliamentary Constituency and a breakdown of this data is provided below. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding awarded by her Department to support athletes representing Northern Ireland at the Commonwealth Games.
answer_text: Sport NI, an Arm’s Length Body of my Department, has awarded substantial National Lottery funding to support local athletes in their preparation and participation at Birmingham. In total, just over £2m has been allocated through the following award categories:£253k for Commonwealth Games NI;£1.6m for the Performance Environment Transition Programme (2022-23); and £182k for individual athletes awards.In addition, our athletes have benefitted from the expertise provided by Sport NI’s High Performance Centre at the Sports Institute in Jordanstown.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will be attending the Commonwealth Games in Birmingham.
answer_text: I can confirm arrangements are currently being finalised for me to attend the Birmingham Games.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the York Street Interchange.
answer_text: You may recall my response to your query in May (AQW 84/22-27), when I stated work is continuing on the refinement of the shortlisted options as recommended within the Placemaking and Active Travel Review report.This work is due to complete at the end of the year and will provide clarity on the implications of implementing the different place-making and active travel enhancements contained within the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the (i) planned investment by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at the Rush Park multi-storey flats; and (ii) timescale for completion.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that the Tower Blocks Action Plan originally set out a Short Term (1-5 years) future for both Beechwood and Woodland House, with a proposal that disposal of the blocks through sale on the open market would be explored. A soft market test exercise was commissioned to test the feasibility of this proposal and concluded that this was unlikely to be a viable proposition. Given this finding, the Housing Executive’s Board asked for a re-assessment of future options for the Rushpark blocks. This will be done through the development of a business case to determine the blocks’ future which will include consideration of retention and refurbishment as part of a suite of options. Until completion and approval of the business case, investment at the blocks is limited to necessary repairs and health and safety works. At present work is ongoing to survey and address issues with balcony glazing to all properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of planning applications objected to by Northern Ireland Water due to a lack of capacity within the sewerage and wastewater network in Belfast.
answer_text: Providing advice on the availability of capacity within the sewerage networks and wastewater treatment works for planning applications is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore, direct your question to the Company at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 1885/22-27, to provide (i) details of the new proposals for the pedestrianisation of Hill Street; (ii) the list of stakeholders the Department are engaging with; and (iii) a timescale for the completion of the consultation.
answer_text: My Department is currently engaged with key stakeholders on Hill Street via the Cathedral Quarter BID team and proposals are currently being refined following this engagement.  It is the intention that these proposals will then be the subject of a public consultation which is currently planned to be completed by the end of September.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) what work his Department is undertaking to promote On-Street Residential Chargepoints for Electric Vehicles; and (ii) the current process to approve the installation of On-Street Residential Chargepoints.
answer_text: Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). The British Government has made £20 million in grant funding available to local authorities/councils in GB-NI during 2021-22 to provide charge points for residents without off-street parking. My Department has also made match-funding available, subject to public expenditure processes, from the Department’s Blue Green Fund to assist local councils making an application to the On-street Residential Charge Point Scheme (ORCS). A local council consortium, led by Derry City and Strabane District Council, submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust which manages the fund on behalf of OZEV on 30 March 2022. My Department is also represented, in an advisory capacity, on the ORCS council consortium. Departmental officials will continue to make themselves available to local councils to provide support, advice and guidance. Any other organisation without a statutory right that wants to carry out works which break open the surface of the road to place apparatus must apply for a street works licence under Article 11 of the Order. Further information on the street works licence procedure and application process can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/apply-licence-place-apparatus-street-street-works-licence. Organisations that are proposing to install EV charge points are likely to need a street works licence. The person granted a Street Works Licence (the Licensee), becomes an Undertaker for the purposes of the Order, and therefore attracts the relevant duties and responsibilities imposed by the Order and its associated Regulations and Codes of Practice.A traffic control permit is also required from the Department for Infrastructure for all works restricting traffic or that require a road closure, before any works can take place.My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AWQ 1245/22/27, (i) to detail the advice being sought; and (ii) when the advice will be received.
answer_text: My officials have received advice which requires further consideration before any decision on how to proceed can be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a breakdown of the costs incurred by his Department as a result of the legal action taken against the PSNI in the summer of 2021 in relation to the Adam Street area of North Belfast.
answer_text: In relation to litigation involving my Department at the Adam Street site in North Belfast, the following litigation is relevant:-a.	the issuing, by DfI and DfC, of JR proceedings against the PSNI;b.	the defence of JR 169 issued against both Departments;c.	defending the Bryson JR in the High Court which was dismissed on application by both Departments; andd.	defending the appeal by Mr Bryson of this JR in the Court of Appeal when Mr Bryson’s application for JR was again dismissed.My Department has incurred legal costs of £28,715.11 to date in the above matters (in a and b above, £10,192.59 and in c and d above, £18,522.52).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a breakdown of the costs incurred by her Department as a result of the legal action taken against the PSNI in the summer of 2021 in relation to the Adam Street area of North Belfast.
answer_text: In relation to litigation involving my Department at the Adam Street site in north Belfast, including:a.	taking the JR against the PSNI;b.	defending JR 169;c.	defending the Bryson JR in the High Court; andd.	defending the Bryson JR in the  Court of Appealmy Department has incurred costs of £28,715.11 to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW1449/22-27, (i) whether the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has plans for development of the Old Irish Highway site; and (ii) whether the site will be made available to a Housing Association given that planning permission has been secured.
answer_text: The Housing Executive have included a pilot programme of new build housing using modern methods of construction to achieve low carbon homes in their Draft 2022/23-2024/25 Corporate Plan subject to approvals and budget provision. The Old Irish Highway site in Rathcoole is one of the sites being considered for that proposal. In the event that this does not come to fruition the Housing Executive will consider the site as a new build transfer scheme to be delivered by a housing association through the Social Housing Development Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide an update on her Department’s work to introduce Harper’s Law in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland a life sentence is already mandatory in any murder case, and, in circumstances considered appropriate by the Court, can be imposed where a defendant is found guilty of manslaughter.   My Department’s review of sentencing policy in Northern Ireland, which resulted in 54 recommendations, examined the need to introduce new legislation in relation to sentencing for attacks on frontline workers. I have agreed that the seriousness of assaults on a range frontline workers should be recognised in the magistrates’ courts with a higher maximum penalty than is currently available and that, in more serious cases, the fact that the victim is a frontline worker should become a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the Courts will be obliged to specifically recognise emergency workers in their sentencing decisions.  The report on the way forward setting out my decisions was published on 21 April and can be seen online at Sentencing Policy Review - Way Forward Report.I have no plans to make further changes to the law in Northern Ireland at this time.  However, I have asked my officials, who are taking preparatory work forward with a view to introducing a sentencing bill during this mandate, to monitor the application of Harper’s Law in England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline (i) what discussions his Department has had with the Department for Communities regarding the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer; and (ii) the number of bids received from the Department for Communities to deliver the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: In February this year I received correspondence from the Communities Minister indicating that she was determined to agree a plan to deliver the funding needed for the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer. In my response I noted that the collapse of the Executive meant that new proposals could not be brought forward for agreement. I confirmed, however, that both the Regional and Sub Regional Stadia Programme are agreed Executive Flagship Projects which will be the recipient of ring-fenced funding.Three bids covering each of the Budget years were received from the Department for Communities for the Sub-Regional Programme for Soccer as part of the Draft Budget process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 1737/22-27, to outline the costings for the new proposals for the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: I refer the Member to response in AQW 1737/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities pursuant to AQW 218/22-27, for a start date for the development of the homes on the corner of Sunningdale Gardens and Ballysillan Road.
answer_text: The tender was advertised on 20th June, with a return date of 2nd August.  Subject to an acceptable tender being received and approved; and, in particular, the price being within my Department’s spend approval for the project, commencement on site is anticipated in early October 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 902/22-27, what steps the Education Authority are taking to address the backlog of unallocated cases in the North Belfast Education Welfare Team.
answer_text: The Education Welfare Service within the Education Authority has developed a waiting list triage pathway which is used to prioritise cases.  Senior Education Welfare Officers review unallocated cases and prioritise the allocation of those which identify those of most concern. A pilot of early intervention and triaging of the waiting list is being considered and a proposal is being finalised; this will run ahead of the full implementation of the new Education Welfare Service (EWS) model and as part of the Transformation Programme.The Education Authority’s Children and Young People Services will also assist EWS by providing support to children and young people who are known and referred to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department’s work to extend the bus network to the Upper Hightown Road in Glengormley.
answer_text: I am very keen to improve bus services on the Upper Hightown Road however this is dependent on road improvements to make bus access possible. With that in mind, I am pleased to advise that my Department has completed the design work for widening and realignment of a section of Hydepark Road and a “Notice of Intention to Make a Vesting Order” was published during the weeks commencing 20 June and 27 June 2022.Subject to the Vesting Order being successful alongside securing funding for the project, it is envisaged that work could commence on site during 2023. Completion of this work will allow Translink to bring services into the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will carry out safety improvements to the road markings at West Crescent, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: My Department has recently upgraded the traffic signals at the Church Road/West Crescent junction, Rathcoole and, as part of this scheme, the existing road markings need to be altered slightly to take account of the changes to the kerb layout.  This work is programmed for early July.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 463/22-27, (i) for an update on work to provide additional funding to the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice; and (ii) when this additional funding will be released.
answer_text: The Department commissions the Northern Ireland Children's Hospice to provide a range of services for children with life limiting conditions and their families. These services include short breaks for children with complex physical healthcare needs, a paediatric and life limited service for babies and children coming to the end of their short lives, bereavement care and support in the community to children with life limited conditions and their families. The Department has been working with the NICH to review the contract for children's services and correspondence was issued to NICH on the 16th June offering additional funding to the NICH on an in year basis without prejudice to work that will be done to rebase the contract. A proposal regarding the funding for 2022/23 on the basis of a revised contract was also provided and NICH has been asked to confirm by the 1st July if the additionality is acceptable on the basis outlined in terms of the proposed revision to the contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on funding for the redevelopment of Westland Community Centre.
answer_text: My department is not engaged in a project to redevelop Westland Community Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the current condition of the road and footpath surfaces in Dorchester Avenue, Glengormley.
answer_text: My officials advise that Dorchester Avenue is routinely inspected every six months with any defects meeting current intervention level, as per current policy, duly actioned for repair.  No actionable defects were found during an additional adhoc inspection carried out on 27 June 2022.Although not aesthetically pleasing in terms of appearance my officials confirm they are content that the carriageway and footways in Dorchester Avenue are in a safe condition and being maintained in line with current policy.We will continue to carry out our routine maintenance inspections in this area and carry out repairs to any actionable defects in accordance with our current policies.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many children live with (i) type 1; and (ii) type 2 diabetes, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Diabetes is one of the disease registers in the Quality and Outcomes Framework.  Disease prevalence data collected as part of the QOF can be found at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/prevalence-statistics.  The number of children with Diabetes is not collected centrally and is not available.  The Diabetes register contains the number of patients aged 17 years and over with Diabetes Mellitus (specified as type 1 or type 2).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 619/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 619/22-27 was answered on 19 August 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 1368/22-27, whether his Department will include Glengormley in the Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2.
answer_text: Development of an outline business case, which will help inform selection of the preferred route, is well advanced. At this stage, it is too early to provide detail of the routes and the areas which Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2 might serve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the current wait times experienced by patients at the Mater Hospital's Emergency Department.
answer_text: Urgent and emergency care services have been under significant and increasing pressure for over a decade.  The delayed handover of patients to Emergency Departments (EDs) is a symptom of the wider pressures across the system.  A number of short and longer term actions are under way to help to address these system-wide pressures. My Department’s strategic response is the Review of Urgent and Emergency Care and a public consultation on proposals to reform these services, which I launched on 16 March 2022, has recently concluded.I believe the proposed reforms will help to ensure that all citizens in Northern Ireland have equal access to safe urgent and emergency care services, tailored to their specific needs, at the right time and in the right place.While work on the Review was impacted by the COVID-19 pandemic, I published the No More Silos Action Plan in October 2020, which included 10 key actions to ensure that urgent and emergency care services across primary and secondary care can be maintained and improved in an environment that is safe for patients and for staff. The key initiatives which have laid the foundations for the reform of urgent and emergency care included: the introduction of Phone First services in Northern, Southern and Western Trusts, with interim services also available in Downe and Lagan Valley Hospitals; the introduction of Urgent Care Centres; the Timely Discharge Initiative; ED Ambulance Handover Bays; and enhanced investment in Hospital at Home services.  Collectively, these actions help to improve patient flow, ease congestion at ED, and provide alternatives to ED or hospital admission for patients who require urgent treatment but are not facing an immediately life threatening illness. A total of £21.2 million was invested in the No More Silos Action Plan during 2021/22.  My Department is continuously working with the Public Health Agency, the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) and the five other Health and Social Care Trusts to address waiting times at our EDs, enhance the flow of patients through the system, facilitate timely discharge and coordinate better management of our ambulance resources.Emergency Departments at the Royal Victoria Hospital and the Mater Infirmorum Hospital continue to operate under extreme pressure.  While staff in the Emergency Department will make every effort to keep waiting times as short as possible, patients are treated according to clinical priority with a focus on the most severe and life-threatening conditions.There is no quick fix to the pressures currently being felt in our EDs, Ambulance Service and wider hospital system.  Indeed, we need sustained investment in our services to improve the situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Northern Ireland Housing Executive's work to develop the former Grove Primary School site in North Belfast.
answer_text: The Housing Executive have advised they are supportive of the development of new social housing on the site of the former Grove Primary School (North Queen Street, Belfast). A letter was issued to Grove Housing Association in March 2022 confirming support for 30 new social homes The proposals are currently programmed to start on-site in 2023/24 as part of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP). Grove Housing Association has however indicated an ambition to potentially start on-site sooner subject to the satisfactory resolution to issues relating to site surveys and wastewater network capacity issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work of her Department in supporting the Urban Villages Programme in North Belfast.
answer_text: The Executive Office (TEO) is leading the Urban Villages initiative

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the achievements of the Building Successful Communities programme in North Belfast.
answer_text: The Building Successful Communities (BSC) programme is a pilot initiative introduced as part of the Facing the Future – Housing Strategy with the aim of using housing intervention as one of the main catalysts to support local regeneration.  The pilot includes 6 areas.  In North Belfast the following areas are included in the BSC programme:Tigers Bay & Mountcollyer; andLower Oldpark & Hillview.An Action Plan agreed by a local Community Forum was developed in each area and identified both early intervention and catalyst projects focused on supporting regeneration activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when outstanding repairs to the washroom facilities at the Emergency Department at the Mater Hospital will be completed.
answer_text: I can confirm that the repairs to the washroom in the Mater Emergency Department are on course to meet the planned completion date of 6th July 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the Northern Ireland Housing Executive’s plans to redevelop derelict properties at Fortwilliam Parade, Belfast.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has obtained approval from its Board to vest and demolish the properties at 53-55 Fortwilliam Parade and to vest adjacent land at 57 Fortwilliam Parade.  The Business Case is currently going through the formal approval process which is required to commence the vesting process.  Once the required approvals are in place, it is the intention of the Housing Executive to nominate a Housing Association to develop two family homes on the cleared site.  This is currently programmed to start in 2023/24 via the Social Housing Development Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the pedestrianisation of Hill Street, Belfast.
answer_text: My officials recently developed a new proposal for the pedestrianisation of Hill Street and are currently engaging with stakeholders and local businesses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how his Department can improve road safety at White City Community Centre, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: I can advise that officials my Department’s Roads Eastern Division are not aware of any recent concerns being raised about road safety issues at White City Community Centre. A site visit has confirmed that the existing traffic calming measures are in good condition and effective in controlling traffic speeds.As such, the Department currently does not have any plans to implement any additional measures at this location.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when a contractor will be appointed to carry out weed spraying in North Belfast.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department’s internal contractor began its weed treatment programme covering selected areas of North Belfast on 13 June 2022. We have encountered some difficulties with our external contractor regarding the weed spraying element of their contract and meetings are to be arranged in the coming weeks to seek to find a resolution to these issues.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will introduce a cap on school uniform costs.
answer_text: The Department has commenced a Review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria.  The purpose of this review is to ensure that FSM and uniform grants are available to support the most vulnerable children in our society and the review will consider the current rates of uniform grant as a contribution to the cost of school uniforms. The review will consider options to extend the FSM and uniform grant criteria and the rate of uniform grant. I am also aware of legislation that was introduced in England which will require schools to follow new statutory guidance on uniform costs, instructing them to keep prices down. We will consider this legislation and the potential to introduce similar legislation here, as part of the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the impact of the reduction of the number of taxi drivers on the arts sector in Belfast.
answer_text: A reduction in the availability of timely, affordable, safe, reliable and accessible transport can limit access to arts events in general, but particularly so in the evening.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact of the reduction of taxi drivers upon Belfast’s night-time economy.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken an assessment on the impact of the reduction of taxi drivers upon Belfast’s night-time economy. I am concious that adequate transport infrastructure is a critical aspect of a city’s night-time offering. Difficulty accessing safe transport could dissuade people that otherwise would have contributed to our vibrant night-time culture and economy. 		The responsibility for the provision of transport infrastructure rests with the Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions his Department is undertaking to (i) increase the number of taxi drivers; and (ii) remove barriers to becoming a taxi driver.
answer_text: As Minister I am responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry across the North.  The taxi industry operates within the wider context of the existing economic situation.  The number of people deciding to be a taxi driver will reflect their individual choices, including the option to work in other driving jobs that do not involve unsociable hours.  The role of the Department in licencing taxi drivers is to provide assurance to taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers, and to improve road safety.  I do not believe that there is room for compromise on either passenger or road safety.  My officials will continue to work with the industry to understand how the Department can best meet the needs of passengers, drivers and operators.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the taxi industry in Belfast.
answer_text: As Minister I am responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry across the North.  This requires me to balance the needs of taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers, as well as drivers and operators.   The taxi industry operates within the wider context of the existing economic situation and will obviously be impacted by the current very high fuel costs.  I remain committed to working with the industry to understand where the Department can have a role meeting the needs of passengers as well as drivers and operators.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what reforms his Department is planning to improve the taxi industry in Belfast.
answer_text: As Minister I am responsible for the regulation of the taxi industry across the North.  This requires me to balance the needs of taxi customers, including vulnerable passengers, as well as drivers and operators.   The taxi industry operates within the wider context of the existing economic situation, and I am committed to working with the industry to understand where the Department can have a role meeting the needs of passengers as well as drivers and operators.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 986/22-27, will he consider additional crossings at the (i) O’Neill Road; and (ii) Shore Road, to provide surrounding communities with safe access to the V36 Park.
answer_text: I am pleased to be able to confirm that my Department is currently progressing plans to provide new Toucan crossings on Prince Charles Way; Church Road; and on both O’Neill Road approaches to O’Neill Road Roundabout. It is currently envisaged that all of them will be operational later this financial year.I can also confirm that plans are being developed for a new Toucan crossing on Shore Road to link Glas na Braden with Gideon’s Green.Should you have any further questions on these matters, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 758/22-27, to detail the changes made to the School Building Handbook since October 2020.
answer_text: The Primary School Handbook was last changed in September 2020. Since October 2020, a list of changes has been prepared to supplement the Post Primary Handbook to reflect a modest increase in the Limit of Internal Area (LIA) to provide additional ancillary space:Technology & Design -	• 20m² project store	• 5m² technician spaceMiscellaneous -	• 30m² archive store	• 30m² / 40 m² meeting room

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 757/22-27, (i) for a timeframe for completion of the review of the Education Welfare Service; and (ii) will the review look at increasing the 85 per cent school attendance figure required for a referral to the Education Welfare Service.
answer_text: Education Welfare Service (EWS) Transformation Review is to be completed by February 2023.Within the EWS Transformation Review ‘Draft’ Model, the aim is that all children and young people experience preventative activities provided by EWS working on its own or with others. Preventative work is therefore, universal. Consequently, less children and young people will require direct support programmes, targeted at need and implemented as early as possible. This will help to further reduce the number of children and young people that will require intensive intervention. The model is theme focused, based on identified need, effective practice and on achieving positive outcomes for children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 889/22-27, to provide details of the new proposals she will bring to a new Executive.
answer_text: Details of proposals cannot be shared in advance of consideration by a functioning Executive Committee.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities why she requires Executive approval for the Department’s new proposals for the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
answer_text: I have received legal advice in respect to the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer and this confirms that I have a responsibility to bring my proposals to the Executive for the necessary approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 1220/22-27, to detail the proposed alterations to the existing signalised junction at Glenwell Road, Antrim Road and Church Way, Glengormley.
answer_text: Work in relation to the proposed alterations to the existing signalised junction at Glenwell Road, Antrim Road and Church Way, Glengormley is currently at design stage. Upon completion of this work and when the final design has been agreed, I and my officials should then be in a position to provide you with details of the alterations being implemented.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 537/22-37, when will Eastern Division finalise their resurfacing programme, following the confirmation of capital allocations by his Department.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the process of finalising its resurfacing programmes following recent confirmation of its 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. Details will be included in my Department’s Reports which will in due course be presented to the respective Councils, published on the Department’s website and also placed in the Assembly library.Commencement dates for individual schemes are not available at this time as they can only be confirmed following discussions with the appointed contractor and agreement of a works programme.Should you have any further questions on this issue, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will reopen the Homeless Prevention Fund for applications from community organisations.
answer_text: The continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget is having serious consequences across a wide range of areas. It is critical that the Executive is re-established and a budget is agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 687/22-27,  to provide a timetable for the appointment of an Integrated Consultancy Team.
answer_text: The timetable for the appointment for an Integrated Consultancy Team is bound by procurement regulations and is as follows;TaskDatePrepare PQQ and CfT documentsAug 2022PQQ IssueSept 2022PQQ returnOct 2022CfT issueNov 2022CfT returnDec 2022Contract AwardMar 2023

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department can take to improve road safety at Seaview Primary School, Belfast.
answer_text: Traffic congestion occurs at Seaview Primary School due to a concentration of vehicles arriving and departing within a short time period. Such congestion, which is normal at most schools during drop-off and pick-up times, is usually of relatively short duration.I can advise that officials from Roads Eastern Division have been in discussions with the school and the PSNI regarding various traffic issues over a number of years.  During that time, a number of ‘School Keep Clear’ road markings have been introduced in some of the streets around the school. Since the start of the school year in September 2021, a number of observations at Seaview Primary School have been made during peak school times. While it was evident the streets around the school were busy, apart from one incident there were no excessive traffic progression issues noted. While there are currently no plans to introduce additional measures at the school, I can advise that inconsiderate parking or parking in such a way as to cause an obstruction is an offence that the PSNI can deal with. In such instances residents should contact the local PSNI on their non-emergency number 101. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of regionally significant planning applications awaiting a decision; and (ii) when these applications were submitted to his Department.
answer_text: Data extracted from my Department’s Planning Portal, taken from the latest published information, indicates that a total of 4 regionally significant planning applications awaiting decision.  The member should also note that data extracted from my Department’s Planning Portal, taken from the latest published information, indicates that 2 retained Section 26 (former Article 31) major planning applications are also awaiting decision.Details of when these applications were received by the Department are as follows:Regionally Significant Applications			Retained Section 26 (former Article 31)LA02/2020/0458/F	30-Jun-2020			H/2009/0264/O	06-May-2009LA02/2021/1205/F	22-Dec-2021			P/2015/0218/F		16-Mar-2015LA03/2015/0234/F	12-Jun-2015	LA10/2017/1249/F	27-Nov-2017

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the support provided by her Department to the advice sector in North Belfast.
answer_text: The work of the Independent Advice Sector is vital and must be protected especially at this time as the cost of living crisis continues to escalate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the redevelopment of Tennent Street Police Station, Belfast.
answer_text: The Member will wish to note that the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB) owns police estate and that management of the estate is delegated to the Chief Constable of the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI).  As the Member may be aware, the Chief Constable is operationally independent from my Department and accountable to the NIPB.I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the NIPB.  You should, therefore, direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) his Department’s resurfacing programme for North Belfast for the remainder of this financial year; and (ii) a commencement date for each scheme.
answer_text: My Department is currently in the process of finalising its resurfacing programmes following recent confirmation of its 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. Details will be included in my Department’s Reports to the respective Councils which will be presented in due course, published on the Department’s website and also placed in the Assembly library.Commencement dates for individual schemes cannot be confirmed at this stage but will be in due course after discussions with the appointed contractor have taken place and a works programme agreed.   Should you have any further questions on this issue, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the average time for a decision to be issued by his Department for a regionally significant planning application, in the last five years; and (ii) the number that failed to meet his Department’s timeframe targets.
answer_text: Data extracted from my Department’s Planning Portal, taken from the latest published information, indicates that the average processing time for the eight regionally significant planning applications (RSD) processed from date the application is deemed valid to decision or withdrawal from 1 April 2016 to 31 December 2021 was 172.5 weeks. Two of the RSD applications were measured against the previous Departmental target, one was not subject to a Pre-Application Discussion and therefore did not fall under the remit of that target and the second met the target having been progressed to Ministerial recommendation in 19 weeks.	A revised target was introduced for the processing of RSD applications from date valid to a ministerial recommendation or withdrawal date within an average of 30 weeks in April 2019. This 30 week period for recommendation/withdrawal was exceeded for the remaining six applications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a start date for the pedestrian crossing at Carnmoney Road, Glengormley.
answer_text: I can confirm that design work for the provision of a new pedestrian crossing at Carnmoney Road is currently underway.  Unfortunately, I am unable at this early stage to confirm a start date for construction, however officials advise it their intention to have it installed this financial year.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the current status of the ARC21 planning application for the Hightown Road, Glengormley.
answer_text: You may be aware of the recent legal challenge to my Department’s decision to refuse this application. However, the decision notice refusing the application, which issued on 1st April 2022, remains valid at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current licensed landfill sites in North Belfast.
answer_text: Landfill sites in Northern Ireland are classified either as; hazardous, non-hazardous or inert waste as required under Article 4 of the Landfill Directive.I can advise that there are four currently authorised landfills in the North Belfast Constituency. Details of each are listed below:	Cottonmount landfill, Mallusk Road, Mallusk is operated by Biffa Waste Services Limited.  The Operator holds a Pollution Prevention and Control (PPC) Permit Reference P0090/05A for the acceptance of non-hazardous and hazardous waste. The facility is currently permitted to accept a maximum of 300,000 tonnes of non-hazardous waste, 25,000 tonnes of inert waste, and 25,000 tonnes of asbestos waste annually. The company also hold a Waste Management Licence LN10/19 covering the closed section of the landfill.		Wolfhill Landfill, Ballyhill Road, Belfast is operated by Anderson Haulage Limited. The Operator holds a Pollution Prevention and Control (PPC) Permit Reference under reference P0345/10A for the acceptance of a maximum of 75,000 tonnes of inert wastes annually. No waste has currently been deposited.		Hightown Inert Landfill site, Upper Hightown Road, Newtownabbey is operated by Laura McLaughlin T/A Macwill Services. The Operator holds a Pollution Prevention and Control (PPC) Permit Reference under reference P0485/15A for the acceptance of a maximum of 74,999 tonnes of inert wastes annually. A Waste Management Licence LN10/13 is also in place covering the closed section of the landfill.		North Foreshore Closed Landfill, formerly Dargan Road is operated by Belfast City Council under Waste Management Licence LN08/46. This landfill no longer accepts waste for disposal.Further details can be found at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/regulation-landfills-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Northern Ireland’s Housing Executive Low Energy pilot scheme to build their own homes in Rathcoole using modern methods of construction.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that the site at Old Irish Highway in Rathcoole was initially being considered for the Modern Methods of Construction/Low Energy new build pilot scheme.   However, following assessment of these a site at Ballysillan in north Belfast was chosen for this scheme.The Housing Executive has no plans for a low energy pilot scheme in Rathcoole at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the timetable to deliver improvements to Belfast Royal Academy, under the School Enhancement Programme.
answer_text: Officials from my Department and Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD) are working with the school to agree the scoping for the School Enhancement Programme project. It is anticipated that this will be completed by the end of July 2022. When the scoping has been agreed, the procurement of an Integrated Consultant Team (ICT) will be initiated. The ICT will take the project through the design stages and oversee the construction phase. When appointed, the ICT will develop a programme for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what discussions his Department has had with Belfast City Council regarding the development of Giant's Park, in North Belfast.
answer_text: Invest NI and Belfast City Council are in contact regarding the City Council's plans for the Giant's Park site. Belfast City Council, who are responsible for the development and marketing of the site are actively seeking to bring forward a number of proposals, including a project under the Belfast Regional City and Growth Deal (https://www.brcd-innovation.co.uk/projects/smil).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the expansion of Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2 into North Belfast.
answer_text: Development of an outline business case, which will help inform selection of the preferred route for North Belfast, is well advanced. At this stage, it is too early to provide a timeline for the preferred route announcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the development of a Civil Service Regional Hub in Glengormley.
answer_text: Department of Finance officials are working with Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council to finalise a location for a Connect2 hub.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department’s investment plans for the Mount Vernon estate, in North Belfast.
answer_text: NI Water has identified some capital works to be undertaken on the sewer network within the Mount Vernon area under the Living with Water Programme. These works will address Combined Sewer Overflows, which are categorised as Unsatisfactory Intermittent Discharges by NI Water’s Environmental Regulator, the NIEA. These proposals are currently at an early feasibility stage with various options still to be considered and will be subject to business case approvals and affordability considerations in due course.
