The project is due to complete in September 2022.My Department, through Sport NI’s Building Better Sports Facilities programme, has provided £25k in capital funding t

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline his Department’s capital investment plans for North Belfast.
answer_text: In the absence of an agreed Executive budget, a budget planning envelope has been communicated by the Department of Finance (DoF) to all Departments.  My Department has made the necessary plans to live within that planning envelope for 2022/23, until a budget is agreed.Officials in my Department and Invest NI are supporting local authorities in delivering City and Growth Deals throughout Northern Ireland.  City and Growth Deals are split by Council area and the Belfast Region City Deal encompasses North Belfast.  Anticipated total capital investment includes £72 million for Studio Ulster and £42.9 million for the Centre for Digital Healthcare Technologies (CDHT), both of which will have a presence in North Belfast.   Studio Ulster will provide a large-scale Virtual Production studio complex to include world-class Virtual Production stages, fully supported by an integrated research, development and innovation centre of excellence in real-time and Virtual Production.  The CDHT will provide a world-class space for academia, industry and clinicians to innovate and boost the productivity of the life and health sciences sector by accelerating the development of medical technology devices and diagnostics.  Technology, Community and Clinical Living Labs will be based beside the Ulster University Belfast campus and at the Royal Victoria Hospital.  The Outline Business Cases for Studio Ulster and the CDHT project have secured Department for Economy and DoF approval.  The Contracts for Funding are currently being finalised for both projects.  Separate to the Budget 2022-25 process, Project Stratum, the broadband intervention scheme, has received funding as part of the DUP/ Conservative Confidence and Supply Agreement, and additional funding from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.  Project Stratum is improving broadband services across Northern Ireland.  Although it is not possible to dis-aggregate funds for Project Stratum to a constituency level, there are 112 premises in the North Belfast Constituency eligible to benefit from improved broadband because of Project Stratum, with deployment due to commence in spring 2023.Further information for individual premises can be found on the website www.hyperfastni.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice why her Department has not delivered the New Decade, New Approach commitment to recruit additional PSNI Officers.
answer_text: The funding package which accompanied the New Decade New Approach (NDNA) document fell well short of the amount needed to deliver all of the priorities that were set out by the British and Irish governments. Delivery of this NDNA commitment is, therefore, largely dependent on the availability of Executive funding. In 2021-22, the PSNI received an in-year allocation of £12.3m to enable it to retain its police numbers of approximately 7,000, and to further enable the recruitment of 100 police officers in 2021-22 towards those within the NDNA document.  However, based on the assessment made of the impact of the 2022-25 draft budget for PSNI, the Chief Constable decided on a precautionary basis to defer the March 2022 PSNI recruitment intake and therefore the target of 7,100 officers by end of the 2021-22 was not achieved.The proposed draft Budget for 2022-25 is insufficient to increase police officer numbers to 7,100.  Increasing police officer numbers towards the NDNA target of 7500 will not be possible unless further funding is made available.  I have raised concerns regarding the Police Budget with the Finance Minister and my Executive colleagues on several occasions since the publication of the draft Budget 2022-25.The current position is that in the absence of an Executive, a budget for 2022-23 and future years cannot be agreed. In order to assist with decision-making, the Department of Finance has provided each department with a Contingency Planning Envelope.  The envelope for PSNI did not contain specific funding for police numbers and is insufficient to maintain, let alone increase, police numbers.The 2022-23 financial position for the Department looks extremely challenging at this stage and, if confirmed, would require difficult decisions to be made.  There have been a number of changes to the funding position for 2022-23 from the announcement of the Draft Budget and there is now a significant amount of additional resource funding available for allocation in 2022-23.  However, in the absence of an Executive, this funding cannot be allocated.  It is imperative that we come back together as an Executive and work collectively. The prioritisation of policing resources, including for recruitment, is ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. In the interim, I remain committed to continue working with the Minister of Finance and the Chief Constable, to ensure that we have a police service that is properly resourced in all aspects for the challenges it faces.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on improvements to the Green End flats in Rathcoole.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that the Green End scheme is programmed for a start on site in this financial year.  Contract documents and costs are currently being updated with the intention of going out to tender in August.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider resurfacing the Fairhill and Waveney area of North Belfast.
answer_text: I can confirm that the footway at Fairhill Park is currently listed on Roads Eastern Division’s resurfacing priority list, where it will compete with other similar schemes for inclusion in future annual resurfacing work programmes, delivery of which is subject to the availability of funding and contractor resources.Following recent confirmation of our 2022/23 contingency funding allocation my officials are now finalising resurfacing work programmes. I have asked my officials to update you on resurfacing plans for this area of North Belfast in due course. Details will also be included in the Department’s Report presented to Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council, which will be published on the Department’s website and also placed in the Assembly library.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the Northern Ireland Housing Executive’s investment plans for the Ardmillan flats in Rathcoole.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has provided the table below detailing the scheduled schemes which include Ardmillan Drive, Rathcoole.YearWorkgroupScheme Name2022/23RoofsRathcoole Medium Rise Flats Roof Replacements2022/23HeatingNewtownabbey 1 Heating (19/20)2022/23ECMArdmillan Drive/Bawnmore/Longland External Cyclical Maintenance2022/23DoorsArdmillan/Bawnmore/Longlands Doors2023/24Bathrooms Lwr Rathcoole 2/Ardmillan/Avonlea Bathrooms

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 709/22-27, for her assessment of her Department’s work in finding a solution.
answer_text: Officials have been working closely with the Housing Executive to find a way of implementing a solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 709/22-27, (i) a timeframe for these considerations; and (ii) information on the options being considered.
answer_text: These considerations are in development. Officials have sought advice and therefore it is not possible to provide a timeframe until this has been received and considered.Once advice has been considered, I will be in a position to set out the options available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a list of undeveloped land owned by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive in North Belfast.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has provided the table below detailing land listed on its Undeveloped Land Schedule (ULS) within North Belfast.  ULS land has been assessed as having independent development potential, the remainder of land in Housing Executive ownership in North Belfast has been classified as existing open space. RegionDistrictLocationSite Area (HA)ULS ClassificationBelfastNorth BelfastMountcollyer Close0.50Retain for Future UseBelfastNorth BelfastMileriver Street0.38Retain for Future UseBelfastNorth Belfast208-212 Limestone Road0.1Retain for Future UseBelfastNorth BelfastWest Cliftonpark Avenue0.08Retain for Future UseBelfastNorth BelfastOpposite 24-28 Torrens Crescent0.03Retain for Future Use

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will improve the traffic flow at the junction of Antrim Road and Glenwell Road, Glengormley.
answer_text: My Department is currently working with Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council on its public realm proposals for Glengormley which will incorporate alterations to the existing signalised junction at Glenwell Road/Antrim Road/Church Way.  The new layout should improve pedestrian facilities and traffic progression, increase road safety and reduce journey times at this busy junction.  Work is currently scheduled to commence later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the planned resurfacing of Glebecoole Avenue, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: I am pleased to be able to confirm that resurfacing of Glebecoole Avenue is scheduled to take place later this financial year, however the commencement date has still to be confirmed.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the planned new build social housing schemes, supported by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, to meet housing need in the Rathcoole area of North Belfast.
answer_text: There are 166 social housing units currently under construction.103 of these units are located at the Newtownabbey High School site being developed by Apex Housing and due for completion in 2024/25. A further 63 units are also under construction at Knockenagh Avenue which is being developed by Arbour Housing with a completion date in 2023/24.  A further 61 units are programmed to start as part of the current Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP). Clanmil have 39 units scheduled to start at the Abbey Caravan Site in 2022/23. Connswater Homes have a scheme to deliver 22 homes at Barna Square which is programmed to start in 2023/24. Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the planned new build social housing schemes, supported by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, to meet housing need in the Shore Road area of North Belfast.
answer_text: The Housing Supply Strategy sets out a framework and direction of travel for the collaborative action required across the public, private and voluntary & community. Options are being considered for finalising the Strategy in the continuing absence of the Executive and an agreed budget.The following new social housing schemes are planned.Grays Lane Phase 2, Shore Road, Belfast (Apex Housing) – 13 units programmed to start in 2022/23256 North Queen Street (Grove HA) – 24 units programmed to start in 2023/24. This site is the location of the former Grove Primary School.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Glengormley Town Centre Public Realm Scheme.
answer_text: My officials are working closely with Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council to develop the Glengormley Town Centre Public Realm Scheme, which is due to commence within the 2023/24 financial year, subject to all approvals being in place.The scheme is still at a very early stage, and Departmental funding will be dependent on both the completion of a business case demonstrating value for money and availability of budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Northern Ireland’s role in the UK and Ireland Euro 2028 bid.
answer_text: Representatives of both the Department for the Economy and the Department for Communities alongside officials from Tourism Northern Ireland continue to engage in the bid process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the work undertaken by Invest NI’s overseas offices.
answer_text: Invest NI’s professional, passionate and commercially-driven teams across four overseas territories; Asia Pacific, India, Middle East & Africa (IMEA), GB & Europe and the Americas and some 24 international locations, continue to provide vital support to educate, facilitate and support businesses from across Northern Ireland and across key sectors to identify, develop and grow sales around the world. The team also plays an essential role in promoting Northern Ireland as an inward investment location and plays a key role in advancing NI’s position as being second only to London as the most attractive location for FDI in the UK.The work of Invest NI’s overseas teams has a direct bearing on Northern Ireland’s export performance as well as our track record in FDI attraction. Its success is not only reflected in export data and FDI statistics, but also in the feedback from companies and investors. In the last year I have been privileged to witness Invest NI’s work first-hand and hear testament, from both NI companies and international investors, about the quality support they received from Invest NI and how it has been pivotal to their success.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the number of applications that have been made to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme since August 2021.
answer_text: The Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme is delivered by the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) which is independent of the Department of Justice.  I am therefore unable to comment on any specific issues related to administration of the scheme.I understand, however, that a significant number of applications have been made to the Scheme since it opened for applications on 31st August 2021.  I am very pleased that victims and survivors of the Troubles have access to this important scheme. Any specific questions in relation to operation of the Scheme can be raised directly with the Victims’ Payments Board.  Contact details are available from www.victimspaymentsboard.org.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is engaging with The Executive Office in relation to the progress of applications to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: The Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme (TPDPS) is delivered by the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) which is independent of the Department of Justice.  I am therefore unable to comment on any specific issues related to administration of the scheme.  The Department of Justice, however, has been designated by The Executive Office (TEO) to put in place the necessary administrative arrangements to support the VPB in delivering TPDPS.  The VPB is an arm’s length body of TEO and my officials, including the administration team supporting the VPB, engages with the TEO as part of normal sponsorship arrangements and on issues relating to administration arrangements supporting the VPB.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the number of applications to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme that have been deemed ineligible through no fault of their own requirement.
answer_text: The Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme is delivered by the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) which is independent of the Department of Justice.  I am therefore unable to comment on any specific issues related to administration of the scheme.  Any specific questions in relation to operation of the Scheme can be raised directly with the Victims’ Payments Board.  Contact details are available from www.victimspaymentsboard.org.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the delays in the assessment of applications to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
answer_text: The Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme is delivered by the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) which is independent of the Department of Justice.  I am therefore unable to comment on any specific issues related to administration of the scheme.  I am aware, however, that the President and the Victims’ Payments Board are committed to ensuring that applications are processed as expeditiously as possible.Any specific questions in relation to operation of the Scheme can be raised directly with the Victims’ Payments Board.  Contact details are available from www.victimspaymentsboard.org.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the number of applications to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme that have been approved since August 2021.
answer_text: The Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme is delivered by the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) which is independent of the Department of Justice.  I am therefore unable to comment on any specific issues related to administration of the scheme.  I understand, however, that the Victims’ Payments Board has been making determinations on applications to the scheme which I very much welcome.Any specific questions in relation to operation of the Scheme can be raised directly with the Victims’ Payments Board.  Contact details are available from www.victimspaymentsboard.org.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider the installation of a pedestrian crossing on the Church Road, Newtownabbey to improve safe access to the V36 Park.
answer_text: I am pleased to be able to confirm that design work to provide a Toucan Crossing on Church Road in the vicinity of the entrance to V36 Park is currently underway and it is anticipated the crossing will be installed and operational later this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will consider purchasing York Street Police Station, to support the development of social housing.
answer_text: The availability of the site occupied by the (vacant) York Street Police Station was notified via the Public Sector Surplus Site trawl on 3rd October 2019.  The Housing Executive initially expressed an interest in the site (to be developed for the provision of social housing) on 22nd October 2019.  The Housing Executive undertook a planning and feasibility study of the site.  The findings of the Housing Executive’s feasibility study were forwarded to a housing association in order to inform their assessment of whether or not the site could viably be developed to provide new social housing.  Upon further investigation and their own feasibility assessment, the housing association concluded that, given the nature of the constraints, it would not be economically viable to develop the site for social housing.The Housing Executive subsequently advised Land & Property Services of the decision to withdraw interest in the site (for the purposes of development for social housing) on 9th January 2020.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the review of the Neighbourhood Renewal Programme in North Belfast.
answer_text: The review is  underway with an inclusive co-design approach. The Review will recommend an effective, efficient and more integrated place-based approach to target objective need which will align with the emerging Anti-Poverty Strategy, consider Community Wealth Building and adopt a rights based approach.  Locality Co-Design Groups have been established within each council area and will include local council, statutory and community and voluntary sector including nominees from existing Neighbourhood renewal partnerships.  In addition to the local co design groups an advisory panel will provide input from both an academic and lived experience perspective.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s resurfacing plans for Glengormley in this financial year.
answer_text: My Department currently has no plans to carry out resurfacing works in Glengormley during the current financial year, however officials are considering a number of roads for potential inclusion in medium and longer term programmes.  We will continue to carry out routine cyclical maintenance inspections on roads in Glengormley and repair any defects that meet current intervention levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department’s plans to improve rail travel in North Belfast.
answer_text: You will be aware of the ongoing work in relation to the All-Island Strategic Rail Review. The Review will allow us to consider our rail network across this island to view how it can be improved for everyone. It will examine the potential for rail connections across the island including how our existing connections could be improved and where new rail links would be best placed. It will also examine how we can connect our rail network to areas across the North, our international gateways through our ports and airports, the potential for transporting freight using the rail network whilst also considering how we can move to a low or zero emission rail network. It is anticipated that the review will be completed in the autumn of 2022.My Department is also currently developing the new Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan (RSTNTP) which will set out priorities for investment in our road, rail and inter-urban limited stop bus network, including park & ride, through to 2035. This will, in association with Translink, include high level assessments on a variety of potential rail improvements. The RSTNTP will be subject to public consultation and I will be pleased to consider representations on the plan including those in relation to the rail network.In addition, Translink’s current capital plan includes significant investment in the existing rail network to renew track signalling and structures as assets are life expired. Towards North Belfast, this investment includes:A new station development proposed to replace the existing Yorkgate Station facilities;Replacement of 2.5km of track and associated railway infrastructure between Lagan Junction to Yorkgate;Replacement of 1km of track at the Dark Arches area in Newtownabbey; andFortwilliam Signalling and Cabling & Track Renewal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education the number of referrals made by schools in North Belfast to the Educational Welfare Service which remain unassigned to an Educational Welfare Officer.
answer_text: The Education Authority have advised that 152 cases are currently unallocated in North Belfast Education Welfare Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how the number of referrals allowed by each school to the Educational Psychology Service is determined.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Educational Psychology Service (EPS)  apportion their available time based on the annual staffing complement within the EPS and is equitably distributed across schools based on the size of the school, school attainment and a social index of need. The Service delivery model ensures that a school of a similar size and demographic background will receive an equitable amount of psychology timeEach school has consultation time with EPS which is used to identify the appropriate referral pathways for children, this initial consultation does not come out of the school’s allocated time from the EPS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the average time for a referral to the Educational Welfare Service to be allocated to an Educational Welfare Officer.
answer_text: If a school is concerned about a pupil’s pattern of attendance or if there is a cause for concern, consultation with the designated Education Welfare Officer must be undertaken. Referrals to the Education Authority’s Eduction Welfare Service are agreed in accordance with Partnership Agreements with schools. Current EWS industrial action has impacted on the ability to allocate cases in a timely manner. The Education Autority has advised that in the period 1 June 2021 to 31 May 2022, the average time taken from referral to allocation was 55 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why the Educational Welfare Service has informed schools that they have paused referrals.
answer_text: The Education Welfare Service (EWS) has not paused referrals to its service, and it continues to receive referrals if a school is concerned about a pupil’s pattern of attendance. If there is a cause for concern, consultation with the designated Education Welfare Officer must be undertaken by the school. Referrals to EWS are agreed in accordance with Partnership Agreements with schools. The Service is currently reviewing Key stage 4 referrals – Year 12s to determine appropriate support. This is a normal part of the service’s considerations at this stage of the school year. These actions are informed by additional factors such as Children Looked After (CLA), Special Educational Needs (SEN), Child Protection Registration (CPR), Emotional Health and Wellbeing, EWS Court Action (Current or Pending involving a sibling) and other assessed needs.All referrals are considered based on consultation and assessed planning to ensure best outcome for the young person.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the installation of road markings to improve road safety in Abbey Park, Newtownabbey, will be completed.
answer_text: My Department has scheduled the installation of the road markings for mid July 2022, subject to favourable weather conditions prevailing at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when the Education Authority will complete outstanding maintenance works at Whitehouse Primary School, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that 89 maintenance task orders have been raised for Whitehouse Primary School from 1 April 2021 to date, and 87 have been completed.The remaining 2 outstanding orders for perimeter fence repairs, and repairs to sliding doors in Assembly Hall will be completed on 15 July 2022 and 30 June 2022 respectively.The EA’s Maintenance Service continues to carry out response maintenance repairs as and when they are reported to the Helpdesk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure will Seaview Primary School, Belfast be included in 20mph speed limit pilot programme.
answer_text: The second tranche of part-time 20mph speed limits at schools is currently being rolled out and upon completion, over 200 schools across the North will be benefitting from this initiative. When all of the tranche two sites are fully operational and have had sufficient time to bed in, my Department intends to carry out a review of this work to determine if a further tranche is justified. Until this review is carried out, I am not in a position to commit to install part-time 20mph speed limits at any more schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the planned Nurture Group for Whitehouse Primary School, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: Following my announcement on 01 March 2022, of an expansion to the Nurture Programme, the Education Authority (EA) is carrying out site visits in order to scope the building work required for all 16 schools identified to receive a new Nurture Group.Whilst this process is underway and in parallel, the Principal and staff at Whitehouse primary school can work with the Nurture Advisory & Support Service (NASS) in the EA to make preparations for the Nurture Group and to introduce a nurture approach in education to their school

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how many teachers in North Belfast have undergone the training provided by the Nurture Advisory and Support Service.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s Nurture Advisory & Support Service (NASS) has advised that for North Belfast schools, from August 2021 to 01 June 2022, 609 school staff have attended 71 teacher professional learning sessions and 6 Nurture Group Staff have attended 6 cluster support sessions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support her Department provides to the Thrive Programme.
answer_text: I am aware of the excellent work carried out by the Thrive collaboration to help children and young people in the Rathcoole and Monkstown area achieve their full potential. My Department provides significant funding and supports the project through attendance at board meetings and sub groups.Thrive’s attainment workstream receives financial support through seven participating schools, with each providing approximately £5,000 per annum from their Extended Schools budget or main school budget.Thrive is one of four partners in the Department’s WRAP programme, which is funded by the Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime Programme.  Under WRAP, Thrive will receive £104,000 in 2022/23 towards the Boost and Aspire educational programmes and coordination generally.In February 2022, I announced funding of £143,000 under Fair Start (over two financial years) for one of the Thrive partners, Monkstown Boxing Club (MBC).  This funding will provide further support to the Aspire Programme and MBC’s wider work with children and young people and their families, as well as helping to inform the design of the Reducing Educational Disadvantage programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what funding under A Fair Start is being provided to schools in North Belfast to improve oracy in Early Years.
answer_text: I am currently considering proposals submitted for 2022/23 in relation to the report “A Fair Start”. One of the proposals being considered relates to work in North Belfast, and would involve a two-year programme for children with additional needs and emerging Special Educational Needs. I hope to finalise programme and budget allocations shortly.Last year, 23 actions were initiated following publication of the report on 1 June 2021. A progress report was published on 16 December 2021 and I will be publishing a 2nd progress report in June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the 85 per cent school attendance figure required for a referral to the Educational Welfare Service.
answer_text: The Education Welfare Service (EWS) provides a range of services to schools to ensure that pupils are supported appropriately and attend school regularly.If a school is concerned about a pupil’s pattern of attendance or if there is cause for concern, consultation with the designated Education Welfare Officer should be undertaken. Pupils whose attendance at school falls below 85% and where there is a concern, may be referred to the EWS. A review of the Education Welfare Service is on-going and is likely to recommend that schools increase their emphasis on proactive measures to address pupil absence in order to resolve issues early.Regular attendance at school is everyone's responsibility - pupils, parents, statutory bodies, employers and the community. We all need to ensure that children's learning is prioritised which is why regular attendance at school is so important.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she is conducting a review into the School Building Handbook used to determine the specification for new school buildings.
answer_text: The School Building Handbook is a live document which is kept under continuous review. Relevant changes to legislation and processes and procedures are regularly published as an Addendum to the Handbook. An updated version of the Primary Schools Building Handbook which consolidated these changes was uploaded to the DE website in November 2020. Work is ongoing to complete a similar update of the Post Primary Schools Handbook.Going forward my Department intends to undertake a detailed review of the School Building Handbook, including engagement with key stakeholders, to ensure high quality accommodation that meets the needs of our children continues to be provided in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail her plans to increase the number of Nurture Groups in (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools in North Belfast.
answer_text: My Department currently funds 18 Nurture Groups in primary schools in North Belfast. The schools which have Nurture Groups are:	Abbots Cross Primary School, Doagh Road, Newtownabbey	Blackmountain Primary School, Blackmountain Place, Belfast	Bunscoil Bhean Mhadigan, Cliftonville Road, Belfast	Currie Primary School, Limestone Road, Belfast	Edenbrook Primary School, Tennant Street, Belfast	Glenwood Primary School, Upper Riga Street, Belfast	Harmony Primary School, Forthriver Crescent, Belfast	Hazelwood Integrated Primary School, Whitewell Road, Newtownabbey	Holy Trinity Primary School, Monagh Road, Belfast	John Paul II Primary School, Whiterock Road, Belfast	Our Lady’s Girl’s Primary School, Deanby Gardens, Belfast.	Rathcoole Primary School, Derrycoole Way, Newtownabbey	St Clare’s Primary School, Cupar Street, Belfast	St Joseph’s Primary School, Slate Street, Belfast	St Kevin’s Primary School, Falls Road, Belfast	St Oliver Plunkett Primary School, Glenn Road, Belfast	St Patrick’s Primary School, Churchill Street, Belfast	Whitehouse Primary School, Doagh Road, NewtownabbeyFunding is provided to primary schools only as the principles behind Nurture were developed to specifically address Social, Behavioural, Emotional and Wellbeing needs (SBEW) and attachment difficulties in young children. However, the Nurture in Education Programme, which is delivered by the Education Authority, is available to all primary, special, post-primary schools and Education Otherwise Than At School (EOTAS) settings to allow staff to avail of training and support in nurture approaches.Any further increase in the number of Nurture Groups will be subject to available funding and the location of schools to receive a Nurture Group will be selected based on objective criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Nurture Groups funded by her Department in (i) primary schools; and (ii) post-primary schools in North Belfast.
answer_text: My Department currently funds 18 Nurture Groups in primary schools in North Belfast. The schools which have Nurture Groups are:	Abbots Cross Primary School, Doagh Road, Newtownabbey	Blackmountain Primary School, Blackmountain Place, Belfast	Bunscoil Bhean Mhadigan, Cliftonville Road, Belfast	Currie Primary School, Limestone Road, Belfast	Edenbrook Primary School, Tennant Street, Belfast	Glenwood Primary School, Upper Riga Street, Belfast	Harmony Primary School, Forthriver Crescent, Belfast	Hazelwood Integrated Primary School, Whitewell Road, Newtownabbey	Holy Trinity Primary School, Monagh Road, Belfast	John Paul II Primary School, Whiterock Road, Belfast	Our Lady’s Girl’s Primary School, Deanby Gardens, Belfast.	Rathcoole Primary School, Derrycoole Way, Newtownabbey	St Clare’s Primary School, Cupar Street, Belfast	St Joseph’s Primary School, Slate Street, Belfast	St Kevin’s Primary School, Falls Road, Belfast	St Oliver Plunkett Primary School, Glenn Road, Belfast	St Patrick’s Primary School, Churchill Street, Belfast	Whitehouse Primary School, Doagh Road, NewtownabbeyFunding is provided to primary schools only as the principles behind Nurture were developed to specifically address Social, Behavioural, Emotional and Wellbeing needs (SBEW) and attachment difficulties in young children. However, the Nurture in Education Programme, which is delivered by the Education Authority, is available to all primary, special, post-primary schools and Education Otherwise Than At School (EOTAS) settings to allow staff to avail of training and support in nurture approaches.Any further increase in the number of Nurture Groups will be subject to available funding and the location of schools to receive a Nurture Group will be selected based on objective criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 150/22-27, to detail (i) the options she is considering; and (ii) when the process will be completed.
answer_text: My Department is currently gathering information on the options, following which the suitable options available will be clearer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the extension of the car park at Whitehouse Primary School and Nursery Unit, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: A planning application for the traffic management and car parking scheme at Whitehouse Primary School and Nursery Unit was submitted to Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council Planning Department on 14 March 2022.  The application is currently pending a decision with one objection received. The Education Authority submitted amendments to the Planning Department on 27 May 2022 following discussions with Planners and await a response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when the Assets Recovery Community Scheme will reopen for applications.
answer_text: As set out in the NI Organised Crime Strategy 2021-24, initiatives such as the Assets Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS) are used to fund programmes that tackle crime or reduce the fear of crime and send an important message that crime does not pay. The current interim Scheme, which spanned a six-month period, ended on 31 March 2022.  While I had hoped to launch a longer-term scheme this year, given the prevailing context post Covid, my Department is preparing for the delivery of a new round of one year ARCS funding, which I hope to announce in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to explain the delay in starting work on a new building for Seaview Primary School, Belfast.
answer_text: From the offset, a lack of alternative sites along with a competing priority to address a shortfall in Special School accommodation in Belfast has impacted progressing this project to date. The Education Authority has now determined that there is only one viable option remaining – an extension and refurbishment of the existing school building. This will be investigated through a fresh technical feasibility study. The procurement process to appoint an Integrated Consultancy Team to undertake this study is to commence.I appreciate how frustrating this must be for the entire school community, but be assured the delivery of this project remains a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the new building for Seaview Primary School, Belfast.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the business case for the major capital investment project at Seaview Primary School, Belfast is being re-written. This is due to one of the original sites under consideration, the Fortwilliam Resource Centre no longer being available. The Fortwilliam site is now being used as a second site for Harberton Special School and is likely to remain so for the medium term. In the absence of an alternative site in North Belfast being available, it has been determined that there is only one viable option remaining - an extension and refurbishment of the existing building. This will be investigated through a fresh technical feasibility study.The EA is to commence the procurement process for an Integrated Consultancy Team to undertake this fresh study. Following appointment of an Integrated Consultancy Team, a draft programme will be prepared for acceptance and approval of the Project Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the schools in North Belfast that are benefitting from the School Enhancement Programme.
answer_text: Four schools in the North Belfast constituency have been announced to advance in design under the second call to the School Enhancement Programme (SEP2).School NameCurrent StatusCliftonville Integrated Primary SchoolDesign Team has been appointed. A Technical Feasibility Report has been completed and is currently under review by the Education Authority.  Our Lady of Lourdes Primary SchoolDesign Team has been appointed and design is progressing. The planning application has been submitted.St Malachy's CollegeDesign Team has been appointed and design is progressing. The planning application has been submitted.Belfast Royal AcademyScoping of the project is underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what powers the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has to designate existing properties as accommodation for people over 55 years old.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has no specific powers to designate existing properties as accommodation for people over 55 years old.  However, under the rules of the Common Selection Scheme provision is made to allow for local lettings policies through Rule 84.  In the past this Rule has been used to designate certain geographically specific properties as suitable to allocation to those aged over 55 only.  Please note, any schemes designated as sheltered accommodation have not been considered within the remit of your question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department’s investment plans for Whiteabbey Hospital.
answer_text: As part of the launch of the 10 year Cancer Strategy in March 2022 I committed to the development of two Rapid Diagnostic Centres (RDC) to be piloted in South Tyrone Hospital in Dungannon and Whiteabbey Hospital in Newtownabbey. The services would support the rapid diagnosis for any person who presented to their GP with non-specific but concerning symptoms and where they do not meet the criteria for red flag referral.  A project to take forward RDC development and implementation is underway and is being led by Departmental officials working with the Trusts and clinicians. We hope to be welcoming the first patients from across Northern Ireland by December 2022. This is a very important step forward this year in helping to achieve the goals in the Cancer Strategy. In addition, as part of the 10 year capital priorities review, the Northern Health and Social Care Trust submitted a proposal for a new primary and community care hub on the Whiteabbey Hospital site. The Trust are currently developing the outline business case for this project, prior to submission to the Department for approval.I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care. As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.However, my ability to strategically plan capital investment is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the process for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to designate existing properties as accommodation for people over 55 years old.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that any decision to designate properties as accommodation for people over 55 is undertaken through a specific application of Rule 84 of the Common Selection Scheme which allows for local lettings policies to be applied.In respect of Housing Executive stock the local Housing Executive Office drafts a proposal, which is then evaluated centrally. If the proposal is deemed suitable it is then submitted for Housing Executive Board approval.  In considering any decision to approve a local lettings policy the Housing Executive Board will consult with the Department for Communities as specified in Rule 84.Please note that the Housing Executive has not considered any schemes designated as sheltered accommodation as within the remit of your question.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the Regional Dementia Care Pathway.
answer_text: The Integrated Care Partnerships, who will be responsible for implementation of the pathway, have submitted business cases in line with the commissioner’s service specifications. The business cases outline the additional resource requirements to implement the pathway in:An identified prototype site in their respective Trust localities. All areas of Northern Ireland. It is acknowledged that implementation of the pathway in either of these two options will require substantial financial investment in the region of millions of pounds.Implementation of the regional dementia care pathway is required to address the current presenting challenges with delivering effective, safe and high quality dementia services. The regional dementia care pathway outlines a transformational service delivery model which will require a primary care led approach working closely with secondary care dementia services. At the current time there is no identified source of funding to implement the regional dementia care pathway. I am very conscious of pressing demands for extra funding across many areas of health and social care. As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available. Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.  My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department’s resurfacing plans for the Kings Park area of North Belfast.
answer_text: My officials advise that the roads in the Kings Park area are in relatively good condition, and as such my Department currently has no plans to carry out any resurfacing works at this time. However, the roads will continue to be inspected on a periodic basis with defects actioned for repair in accordance with our current maintenance policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department’s resurfacing plans for the Rathfern area of North Belfast.
answer_text: My officials advise that with the exception of Altnacreeve Park, the roads in the Rathfern area are considered to be in relatively good condition. I can confirm that Altnacreeve Park is currently included in my Department’s three to five year carriageway and footway resurfacing programme, delivery of which will be subject to competing priorities and the availability of contractor resource and funding at the time. You may be aware that in 2018/19 my Department made a considerable investment of circa £200,000 in this area, upgrading existing flagged footways to asphalt. Although there are no currently no plans to carry out resurfacing works elsewhere in the Rathfern area, these roads will continue to be inspected on a periodic basis with defects actioned for repair in accordance with our current maintenance policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department’s resurfacing plans for the Seaview area of North Belfast.
answer_text: My Department carries out an annual programme of reconstruction and resurfacing works which is implemented on a scheme priority basis, the scale of which is largely dependent upon the funding allocated to my Department.I can confirm that a number of the streets in this area are currently listed on Roads Eastern Divisions resurfacing programme where they will compete with other similar schemes for inclusion in future annual resurfacing programmes, delivery of which is subject to the availability of funding and contractor resources.I have recently confirmed capital allocations for resurfacing and other functions and Divisions are now developing programmes within these allocations. I have asked Eastern Division to update you on resurfacing plans for the Seaview area of North Belfast after their programmes have been finalised.In relation to capital, as the largest capital spending department here, the absence of an Executive and a three year budget, will have implications for my Department, risking the deliverability of its full capital requirements for 2022-23 and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department’s resurfacing plans for the Rush Park area of North Belfast.
answer_text: My officials advise that with the exception of Beechwood Avenue, the roads in the Rushpark area are considered to be in relatively good condition. I can confirm that Beechwood Avenue is currently included in my Department’s 3 to 5 year resurfacing programme, delivery of which will be subject to competing priorities and the availability of contractor resource and funding at the time. Although there are no currently no plans to carry out  resurfacing works elsewhere in the Rushpark area, these roads will continue to be inspected on a periodic basis with defects actioned for repair in accordance with our current maintenance policies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department’s resurfacing plans for the Rathcoole area of North Belfast.
answer_text: I am pleased to say that resurfacing works on 11 roads in the Rathcoole area commenced in February 2022 and are expected to be completed by end of September 2022.Details on the individual schemes are contained in my Department’s Annual Report to Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council, which can be viewed in the Assembly library and also on the Department’s website via the following link:Annual Report to Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council 2021 (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department will provide adequate funding to fully commission children’s services with the Northern Ireland Hospice.
answer_text: The Department commissions the Northern Ireland Children's Hospice to provide a range of services for children with life limiting conditions and their families. These services include short breaks for children with complex physical healthcare needs, a paediatric and life limited service for babies and children coming to the end of their short lives, bereavement care and support in the community to children with life limited conditions and their families. The Department is working with the NICH to review the contract for children's services and is making additional funding available to the NICH on an in year basis in advance of the detailed work to rebase the contract. Details of the additional funding to be provided are being worked through with the NICH and a final position on in year funding and associated activity will be concluded by end June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will visit the Northern Ireland Hospice.
answer_text: I visited the Northern Ireland Hospice and Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice in June 2021 when I had the opportunity to meet with Hospice staff and to see the valuable services provided by the Hospice. 				Funding for adult inpatient and community hospice services is based on guidance put in place by the Department of Health in 2004.  Department of Health officials and colleagues in the Health and Social Care Board (now Strategic Planning and Performance Group within my Department) have had regular engagement with the Northern Ireland Hospice as part of contractual and funding arrangements for hospice services.  				In response to representation from the NI Hospice, in 2021/22, the Health and Social Care Board (now SPPG) undertook to commit to annually benchmark the Hospice’s service costs and indicated its willingness to consider any assessment of in-year service pressures from the Hospice should there be any threat to service sustainability. Any additional funding required in respect of this benchmarking exercise and assessment of pressures would be subject to funding availability within the system. 		Additional non-recurrent funding has been made available to the Northern Ireland Hospice (and other hospices) in response to funding pressures.  In 2020/21, additional non-recurrent funding of £16million was made available to local hospices to recognise the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on both fundraising ability and the provision of services.  The NI Hospice, including the NI Children’s Hospice, received £8.23million of this additional COVID-19 funding.In 2021/22, in recognition of financial pressures highlighted by the NI Hospice, additional in-year funding of £899k was made available to hospices, which included £381k to the Northern Ireland Hospice. In addition, funding was provided by the Public Health Agency in response to nursing in-year pressures of £1.384m facing hospices. 		In recognition of changing demographics and dynamics of care, it has been agreed that a comprehensive review of adult specialist palliative care services, based on a co-production approach, will be undertaken to inform future services and funding arrangements.  The review would allow for greater clarity about what specialist palliative care services are needed and how these can best be commissioned, delivered and funded to ensure that specialist palliative and end of life care across the system meets the future needs of our population.  This will be a substantial body of work and it is intended that hospices would be fully engaged in this review.  In terms of children’s palliative care, I am very aware of the care and support that the Northern Ireland Children’s Hospice provides to children and young people with palliative and end of life care needs and for their families. Separate commissioning arrangements are in place for the NI Children’s Hospice (NICH) for the provision of short breaks, bereavement support and educational sessions.  In addition, funding has b

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what communication his Department has issued to GPs regarding the delays faced by patients in seeking a face-to-face appointment.
answer_text: I recognise how important it is that people can access GP services when they need to do so.  It is important to emphasise that GP practices are open and will continue to provide both face to face appointments and alternative consultation options for patients as appropriate.  GPs are independent contractors who contract with the Department of Health to provide primary medical services under the terms of the General Medical Services (GMS) Contracts Regulations 2004.  As independent contractors, GPs are responsible for the day to day management of their practice, including patient appointment arrangements.  There are no plans to change GMS contract arrangements in Northern Ireland.General practice telephone first consultation allows patients to seek timely medical advice from their GP.  The GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise; where a face to face appointment is appropriate, that will then be arranged.  This approach has enabled GPs to appropriately manage, treat and support a greater number of people than would otherwise have been the case.  Practice teams are carrying out approximately 200,000 patient consultations on a weekly basis. It is essential that we make use of all the tools at our disposal to ensure that we can make best use of the available resources.  It is important that Primary Care continues to transform to meet growing patient demand.  Technology and new ways of working will continue to play an important role alongside existing options, such as face to face appointments, which remain available as an option to patients based on the GPs assessment of individual clinical need. If people have symptoms or an unexplained illness or have any reason to be concerned, they should in the first instance contact their GP who will be able to provide advice.  GPs want anyone who has a health concern to feel reassured that they will be able to get an appointment and see a GP if necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what funding her Department has awarded to the IFA in support of the Northern Ireland Women’s Football Team.
answer_text: My Department has provided funding of £100,000 to the Irish Football Association (IFA) to support the development of women’s football and the Women’s Football Team ahead of the team’s first appearance at the European Women's Football Championship finals this summer.Sport NI Sport Institute is currently delivering support to the Women’s IFA and are engaged in discussions finalising what further support will look like.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many pupils from North Belfast did not receive a post-primary school place this year.
answer_text: At 31 May 2022, 12 children who reside in North Belfast and who had applied to post-primary school for September 2022 admission were still to be placed.My Department is continuing to work with schools in areas of pressure to create additional capacity where it is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when she will approve the implementation of the 2021 National Joint Council pay increase for Northern Ireland Housing Executive staff.
answer_text: Housing Executive 2021/22 Pay Remits have been approved

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what action the Education Authority is taking to support pupils who did not receive a place in Glengormley High School this year.
answer_text: Glengormley High School (HS) remains under-subscribed at Year 8 for September 2022 admission, therefore no child’s application to the school was unsuccessful.  Should the school become over-subscribed at any point my Department will consider any request from the school for an increased intake.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils who placed Glengormley High School as their first choice, to start in September 2022, and did not receive a place in year eight.
answer_text: Glengormley High School (HS) remains under-subscribed at Year 8 for September 2022 admission, therefore no child’s application to the school was unsuccessful.  Should the school become over-subscribed at any point my Department will consider any request from the school for an increased intake.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what additional resources can be awarded to Glengormley High School to meet the demand for additional places this year.
answer_text: Glengormley High School (HS) remains under-subscribed at Year 8 for September 2022 admission, therefore no child’s application to the school was unsuccessful.  Should the school become over-subscribed at any point my Department will consider any request from the school for an increased intake.

answer_text: Glengormley High School (HS) remains under-subscribed at Year 8 for September 2022 admission, therefore no child’s application to the school was unsuccessful.  Should the school become over-subscribed at any point my Department will consider any request from the school for an increased intake.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether additional school places will be allocated to Glengormley High School to meet the increased demand for year eight places.
answer_text: Glengormley High School (HS) remains under-subscribed at Year 8 for September 2022 admission, therefore no child’s application to the school was unsuccessful.  Should the school become over-subscribed at any point my Department will consider any request from the school for an increased intake.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the development of land owned by the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at the corner of Sunningdale Gardens and Ballysillan Road.
answer_text: This site will be used for a Modern Methods of Construction/ Low Energy re-provision pilot scheme that has been developed by the Housing Executive and approved by my Department.  The scheme will provide 6 no. 3-person 2-bedroom semi-detached houses.  The Housing Executive is currently finalising the tender documents for issue this month and, subject to a successful tender exercise, intends to start on site this summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has considered a loan scheme to help homeowners with the removal of Finlock guttering from their properties, and who are not eligible for a grant.
answer_text: My Department is considering a number of options, recognising the issues of those who can’t receive grant, in order to bring the matter to a close as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the development of land owned by her Department in Glenbryn Drive.
answer_text: My Department owns vacant lands on both sides of Glenbryn Drive, Belfast that are managed by the Belfast Regeneration Directorate. The portion of land on Glenbryn Drive adjacent to Glenbryn Park has been made available to provide storage and site facilities to contractors working on behalf of Clanmil Housing Association (CHA) who are currently building 12 houses at Glenbryn Park. The land adjacent to Glenbryn Parade has recently had substantial maintenance works carried out which included fencing to provide additional security for the local residents. My Department has been working closely with the Housing Executive in this area.  To date 12 housing units have been delivered at Glenbryn Park through CHA.  Housing Executive will complete a review of the waiting list once CHA’s current scheme has been completed and allocated.  My Department has committed to making the land at Glenbryn Drive available to Housing Executive if it is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on his Department’s weed spraying programme in North Belfast.
answer_text: My officials have issued Works Orders to our external contractor in relation to the 2022 weed control programme in Belfast North and Belfast South, who is finalising their work programme including confirmation of the start date.  I can confirm that my Department’s internal contractor has begun weed control works in the Belfast North and Belfast South areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is considering a road resurfacing scheme in Carolhill Road, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: I can confirm that Carolhill Road is currently on my Department’s 3-5 year resurfacing programme.The timing of delivery of future resurfacing schemes, including Carolhill Road, will be subject to ongoing prioritisation taking into account the availability of funding at the time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the work to replace finlock gutters in the Silverstream area of North Belfast.
answer_text: My Department is considering a number of options, recognising the issues of those who can’t receive grant, in order to bring the matter to a close as soon as possible.

answer_text: The Housing Executive has provided the following information for schemes in the Queens Park area of North Belfast:8 properties included in Bathroom/Kitchen/Rewire scheme 21701026, currently scheduled for September 2024.All of the properties in Queens Park are included in External Cyclical Maintenance scheme 21781019, currently scheduled for August 2025The majority of properties in Queens Park had new heating installed in recent years, the remainder will be included in heating schemes scheduled between 2025 and 2028 as per the age of the installations, specific scheme details are not yet in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what engagement her Department has undertaken with residents of the Rathcoole tower blocks, regarding the Tower Block Strategy.
answer_text: Following approval of the Draft Tower Blocks Action Plan by the Housing Executive’s Board in May 2018 the Housing Executive undertook an extensive consultation exercise as follows: In June 2018 a letter was issued to each resident and owner advising that the Housing Executive would be consulting with them about the future of the tower blocks.In September 2018 a further letter was issued advising that Housing Executive staff would begin door to door consultation. This letter was accompanied by a block specific leaflet which set out the proposal for their block and provided advice on issues such as rehousing and compensation.  The consultation was carried out over two months with an average 80% contact rate achieved.Residents were advised of the results of the consultation exercise in March 2019.The final Tower Blocks Action Plan was approved by the Housing Executive’s Board in March 2019 and took cognisance of the consultation exercise by revising the categorisation of some blocks. Residents were advised in May 2019 that the final Tower Blocks Action Plan was approved and detailed the proposal for their blocks.Since the approval of the Action Plan the Housing Executive continues to update the local Housing Community Network on progress. In addition the following updates have been provided to residents:In August 2021 a letter was issued advising the business case for demolition of Monkscoole House had been approved by the Housing Executive’s Board.A further letter was issued to residents of Abbotscoole House in August 2021 to update them on the progress of the Tower Blocks Action Plan. In September 2021 a letter was issued advising that demolition work to Monkscoole was due to commence on 18th October 2021.In November 2021 a Tower Blocks Action Plan leaflet was sent to residents in Abbotscoole providing advice on compensation matters. In March 2022 a Tower Blocks Newsletter was sent to all residents providing an update.  A Newsletter will now be issued bi-annually to all residents and owners affected by the Tower Blocks Action Plan with the next edition due in August 2022.  Further consultation will be carried out with residents of Abbotscoole, Carncoole and Glencoole when preparing the business case for each block.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much his Department has spent to date on the York Street Interchange.
answer_text: Expenditure on the York Street Interchange scheme to the end of March 2022 is £22.3M.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Northern Ireland Housing Executive's Tower Block Strategy.
answer_text: The information as sought is available at https://www.nihe.gov.uk/Documents/Tower-Block-Action-Plan/Tower-Blocks-Update-Spring-2022-(PDF-114-kb)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the York Street Interchange.
answer_text: The Placemaking and Active Travel Review, which aimed to maximise the social, economic and environmental benefits of the York Street Interchange project and its contribution to the future development of Belfast, has now been completed. The report identified a number of options to enhance placemaking and improve active travel provision. Work is ongoing to refine the shortlisted options and consider factors relating to their potential implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to improve access to GP services.
answer_text: I recognise how important it is that people can access GP services when they need to do so.  It is important to emphasise that GP practices are open and will continue to provide both face to face appointments and alternative consultation options for patients as appropriate. The general practice telephone first consultations allow patients to seek timely medical advice from their GP for both routine and urgent problems.  The GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise.  Where a face to face appointment is appropriate, that will be arranged.  Alternatively, the GP may decide, based on their clinical assessment, that a telephone consultation is appropriate or that the patient should be signposted or directed to other relevant services.  This approach helps the GP to appropriately manage, treat and support the large number of us who seek clinical support and advice from our primary care practitioners.  I recognise that patients have experienced issues in contacting their GP services and understand the frustration this can cause.  My Department has established a working group to examine issues around access to GP services, with a view to recommending actions to be taken in the short and long term to improve the situation.  This will include scoping a new GP tech platform to facilitate an improved telephony service.In recognition of the increasing demand for primary care services, my Department has continued to invest in our GP workforce and has increased the number of GP trainees by over 70% from 2015 levels.  Work is underway on a review of GP trainee places to ensure we have the workforce in Primary Care we need going forward.  Whilst this work is ongoing, I recently announced that my Department would be increasing of the number of GP training places for 2022/23 by 10, bringing the total number in Northern Ireland to an all-time high of 121 training places this year.  This will help stabilise our GP workforce and ensure it is sustainable for the future.  Any future increases will be informed by completion of the review and subject to the availability of funding.It is important to note however that the increase in demand for primary care services cannot be met solely by increasing the number of GPs.  Rather this is part of a wider programme of work to help improve patient access to services in primary care.  Other elements include the wider roll-out of primary care Multi-disciplinary Teams as well as the introduction of Advanced Nurse Practitioners and additional General Practice Nurses, all of which are making a difference to how services are delivered in primary care and contributing to improved patient outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department is taking to improve access to dental services.
answer_text: To address the issue of access the Department has continued to provide significant financial support to the sector, with changes made in recent months to more closely link support to activity.  We have already seen an increase in activity which will help more patients get the treatment they need.  £31m in financial support was provided in 21/22 and £2.7m has been provided to date in 2022/23.The Department is also planning to increase the availability of appointments for non-registered patients who have urgent and emergency treatments needs.  In the meantime, the Urgent Dental Care Centres continue to provide cover for patients with dental emergencies who cannot be seen in practice. Patient registration timeframes have also been extended since the beginning of the pandemic to ensure that patients registration status is unaffected by any difficulties encountered in attending. A £5m Revenue Grant Scheme was launched on 27 January 2022 for dental practices to support the cost of improving and increasing their delivery of General Dental Services. This funding is designed to improve patient safety, practice efficiency and improve the resilience of practices in order to mitigate against future transmission of aerosol based respiratory diseases, and other infectious diseases.
